text: The Records of the Xuan Room [Tang] Zhang Du ○ Yin Jun During the time when the former Tang Minister Li Gong Shen was stationed at the northern gate, there was a Daoist named Yin Jun. He lived in seclusion in Jin Mountain, did not eat grain, and often ate pine leaves. Although his hair was completely white, his appearance was like that of a child, and he often wandered alone in the city. In the village, there was an old man over eighty years old who turned to the people and said, "When I was a child, I saw Li Gong and he said, 'Li Gong was my maternal grandfather. And he said, "When I was seven years old, I had already known Yin Jun. Until now, over seventy years have passed, and Yin Jun's appearance has remained unchanged. Is he not a deity? I am old, and I can only estimate how much longer I can remain in the human world. You are still strong, so you should remember Yin Jun's appearance." From then until now, over seventy years have passed, and Yin Jun never showed any signs of aging. Is it not as if a thousand years is just a moment?"

A North Gate Attendant from Fengyi, Yan Gong Shou, was a man fond of the unusual. He admired Yin's attainment of the Dao and would visit him every ten days during his rest periods. Later, Yan Gong Shou rose from the position of military司马 to become the commander of the northern gate, and he welcomed Yin to his office, lodging him in the government residence and sitting with him all day. Yin often emitted an unusual fragrance from his flesh, which further increased Yan Gong Shou's respect for him. Yan Gong Shou had a younger sister who studied Buddhism. She once said, "Buddhism and Daoism have different goals," and she was angry that her brother associated with Daoists. One day, she secretly mixed a poisonous substance into the soup and ordered Yin to drink it. After drinking it, Yin was shocked and exclaimed, "I am going to die, I suppose." Soon after, he vomited out a solid object with an unusual fragrance. Yan Gong Shou ordered it to be cut open and found it to be a musk pod. From then on, Yin's appearance declined and his teeth fell out. That night, he died in the government residence. Yan Gong Shou, upon learning what his sister had done, was extremely angry. He immediately ordered his subordinates to handle the funeral arrangements. Two days later, Yin was buried twenty li west of the Fen River.

The following autumn, a Daoist named Zhu Taixu from Zhaosheng Temple performed a ritual and arrived at Jin Mountain, where he unexpectedly met Mr. Yin in the mountains. Zhu Taixu was surprised and asked, "Why are you here, Master?" Mr. Yin smiled and said, "Last year, while I was at the Northern Gate, someone gave me a poisonous drink. I showed them death. However, how could such a poisonous drink harm my true nature?" After saying this, he suddenly disappeared. Zhu Taixu privately found the event strange. Upon returning, he reported to Governor Yan, saying, "I have heard that immortals do not die. If there are any who die, it is through the process of '尸解.' Otherwise, how could such a transformation occur?" Governor Yan initially planned to investigate further, but he stopped out of concern for public reaction. There was a monk named Qixu, originally the son of a Li family from Gouzang. His father served as an imperial censor during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. Qixu developed an interest in Buddhism from a young age. At the age of twenty, he shaved his head, dressed in brown robes, and lived in a Buddhist temple in Chang'an. When An Lushan broke through the Tongguan Pass, Emperor Xuanzong fled west to Shu Gate. Qixu fled to Mount Taibai, where he gathered and ate pine leaves. From then on, he stopped eating.

Once upon a time, a Daoist priest named Qiao Jun arrived at the abode of Qixu. Qiao Jun had a gaunt appearance, with completely white beard and hair. He greeted Qixu and said, "Your spiritual bone structure is very solitary and exquisite; you will surely wander in the fairy realm in the future." Qixu replied, "I am an ordinary, worldly person. How could I possibly reach the fairy realm?" Qiao Jun said, "The fairy realm is very close. You can make an effort to go there." Qixu then asked Qiao Jun to guide him on the path. Qiao Jun said, "You can prepare food at an inn in Shangshan. When you meet a traveler, reward and feed them in Shangshan. If anyone asks where you are going, just say you originally intended to visit Zhi Chuan, and there will be a traveler who will guide you away." Upon hearing these words, Qixu was very happy. After the defeat of An Lushan, the emperor returned from Shu to Chang'an, and the world was at peace. Qixu immediately went to Shangshan, stayed at an inn, and prepared delicious food to await the traveler's arrival. After only a few months, he encountered over a hundred travelers, all of whom were fed and then departed. Qixu's intention began to wane, and he thought Qiao Jun had deceived him, so he planned to return to Chang'an. After preparing for the journey, that night, a very young traveler approached Qixu and asked, "Where is your master going?" Qixu replied, "I have long wished to visit Zhi Chuan." The young traveler was astonished and said, "Zhi Chuan is a divine realm. How could your master possibly reach it?" Qixu responded, "I have been fond of immortals since childhood. I often met enlightened beings who encouraged me to visit Zhi Chuan. It is not far from here." The young traveler said, "Zhi Chuan is very close. Can your master truly accompany me there?" Qixu said, "If I can truly travel to Zhi Chuan, I will not regret it, even if it means death."

Zhaizi and Qixu both arrived at Lantian, where they prepared their supplies. That evening, they immediately ascended Yushan, crossing dangerous terrain and climbing over rocks and peaks for about eighty miles. They reached a cave where water flowed out. Zhaizi and Qixu carried stones together to block the cave entrance, stopping the flow of water. After three days, the water in the cave finally stopped. Both entered the cave, but it was so dark and dim that they could not see anything. They saw a gate several dozen miles away and proceeded toward it. Once they emerged from the cave, the wind was gentle, the sun was warm, and the mountains and waters were clear and beautiful, a true land of immortals. They continued for over a hundred miles and climbed a high mountain. The mountain had clustered peaks that rose sharply, and the stone path was perilous. Qixu was dizzy and hesitant to climb, but Zhaizi said, "The land of immortals is already near; why hesitate?" He took Qixu's hand and they continued. When they reached the summit, the top was flat and level, and looking down, the rivers and plains were so vast that they could not be seen. They then traveled another hundred miles and entered another cave. When they emerged, they saw an endless accumulation of water with a stone path about one foot wide and over a hundred miles long. Zhaizi led Qixu to step on the stone path and they went away. At the foot of the mountain, they saw a large tree with dense shadows, towering thousands of feet high. Zhaizi climbed the tree and sang loudly for a long time. Suddenly, a cool autumn wind arose from the treetops, and soon they saw a large rope tied to a row of bags descending from the top of the mountain. Zhaizi ordered Qixu to close his eyes and sit in the bag. After only half a day, Zhaizi said, "The master can now wake up and see." Qixu looked around and found they were already at the top of the mountain.

Seeing cities and palaces, interwoven with pearls and jade beyond the clouds, the guide pointed and said, "This is Zhi Chuan!" They then went together to the place, where they saw a hundred young immortals lined up in front and behind. One immortal said to the guide, "What is this monk doing here? Is he not a mortal?" The guide replied, "This monk often wished to visit Zhi Chuan, so I brought him here." They then arrived at a hall, where a dignified figure with a headdress and crown sat on a jade table, surrounded by strict guards. The guide commanded the monk to pay respects and said, "This is the True Lord of Zhi Chuan." The monk bowed. The True Lord summoned the monk and asked, "Have you defeated the enemies of the Three Kings of Peng?" The monk could not answer. The True Lord said, "You cannot remain here!" He then ordered the guide to ascend the Cui Xia Pavilion.

The pavilion stretches into the sky, and the building rises like clouds. I saw a person who was naked and blinking, with hair several feet long, thick and glossy, and dark. The hair shone brightly, capturing my attention and filling my eyes with light. The guide said to the visitor, "You should bow and pay respects." The visitor asked, "Who is this person and why are they blinking?" The guide replied, "This person is Yang Wailang, a member of the royal family of the Sui dynasty. He served as an official in the southern palace. During the end of the Sui dynasty, the world was divided and in chaos, and he retreated to the mountains. Now he has attained enlightenment. This is not physical blinking but a form of spiritual vision. Spiritual vision allows one to see beyond the physical world." The visitor asked, "Can you open his spiritual vision?" The guide then spoke to Yang Wailang, who suddenly opened his spiritual vision and looked around. The light became even more intense, like the sun and moon. The visitor was filled with awe and fear, his back drenched in sweat, and his hair stood on end. Then he saw another person lying under a stone wall. The guide said, "This person is Yi Zhirun." The guide explained, "He is also from the human world but has attained enlightenment and arrived here." Afterward, the guide led the visitor back. The path they took was the same as before.

Che Xu then asked his disciple, "Earlier, I paid respects to the True Lord, who asked me about the enmity of the Three Rebels, but I could not answer." The disciple replied, "The Three Rebels are the surnames of the Three Corpses, who always reside within people, spying on their sins. Every Gengshen day, they record them to the Supreme Deity. Therefore, those who seek immortality must first eliminate the Three Corpses. Only then can one attain immortality; otherwise, even if one suffers, it will be of no use." Che Xu understood this matter. From then on, he returned home and lived in Taibai Mountain, abstaining from food and breathing air. He never spoke of the affairs of Zhiyuan to others. During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, he moved to live at the foot of Mount Hua. There, a man from Yongyang named Zheng Sen and a man from Wuxing named Shen Yu both came east of the pass from Chang'an and arrived at the foot of Mount Hua. It happened to be evening and raining heavily, so the two of them stopped. Che Xu, abstaining from food, did not prepare any cooking. Zheng was surprised by his fasting and noticed that his bones were well-formed and handsome. He then asked Che Xu for the truth. Che Xu told Zheng about the affairs of Zhiyuan. Zheng, being a curious man, was both happy and surprised upon hearing the story. After returning from the east of the pass, Zheng returned to Che Xu's dwelling, but Che Xu had already fled, and no one knew where he was. Zheng often told the story, calling it *The Record of Zhiyuan*.

The Ten Fairy Sons Emperor Xuanzong of Tang often dreamed of more than ten fairy sons riding on clouds and descending, arranging themselves in the courtyard, each holding an instrument and playing it. Their tunes were clear and ethereal, truly the music of the celestial realm. When the music ended, one fairy stepped forward and said, "Do you, O Emperor, know this music? It is the divine *Ziyun Qu*. Now I wish to teach it to Your Majesty, to be the correct beginning sound for the Holy Tang, fundamentally different from the *Xianchi* and *Daxia*." Emperor Xuanzong was very pleased and immediately accepted the transmission. Soon he woke up, and the lingering echoes still seemed to be heard. Emperor Xuanzong hastily ordered a jade flute to be played and practiced it, fully grasping its rhythm, but kept silent and did not reveal it. When morning came, he listened to government affairs in the Purple宸 Hall, and the prime ministers Yao Chong and Song Jing entered, presenting matters before the emperor. Emperor Xuanzong seemed not to hear them. The two prime ministers were afraid and presented it again. Emperor Xuanzong immediately stood up and finally did not look at the two prime ministers. The two prime ministers became even more afraid and hurried out. At that time, Gao Lishi was serving Emperor Xuanzong, and he immediately reported, "The prime ministers requested matters, and Your Majesty should personally decide whether to approve or not. What Yao and Song said earlier were all major state affairs, but Your Majesty finally did not look at them, could the two prime ministers be guilty?" Emperor Xuanzong laughed and said, "I dreamed last night that a fairy played music called *Ziyun Qu*, so he taught it to me. I lost its rhythm, so I kept silent and practiced it, so I had no time to listen to the two prime ministers' reports." He immediately took out a jade flute from his clothes and showed it to Gao Lishi. That day, Gao Lishi went to the Secretariat and told the two prime ministers about the matter. The two prime ministers were a little relieved. The tune was later passed on to the Music Bureau.

Chapter on Zhang Quansu Jiang Sheng from Wu County was fond of immortals and, in his youth, abandoned his family to seek seclusion in the foothills of the Four Bright Mountains. He once studied alchemy with a Taoist priest and set up his furnace and cauldrons, heating the fire and pumping air into it, for ten years. Yet, his attempts at creating an elixir of immortality ultimately failed. Later, he traveled to Jingmen, where he saw a beggar in the market, emaciated and bare, sick and shivering, unable to speak. Jiang Sheng pitied the beggar's poverty and hardship, took off his coat to clothe him, and ordered him to serve as his attendant. When Jiang Sheng inquired about his family, the beggar replied, "I am a man from Chu, a son of the Zhang clan, my name is Quansu. My family lived in Nanchang, where we had hundreds of acres of fertile land. Due to famine, we were displaced to the Jingjiang region for nearly ten years. Our land was reclaimed by the government, and I am too ill to support myself. Fortunately, you have shown kindness and allowed me to stay." Thus, Zhang Quansu accompanied Jiang Sheng back to the foothills of the Four Bright Mountains. However, Zhang Quansu was extremely lazy, often sleeping and resting during the day. Jiang Sheng scolded and beat him countless times. One day, Jiang Sheng noticed a stone inkstone on the table and Zhang Quansu said to him, "Master, you have been studying alchemy for a long time. The elixir of immortality, when consumed, transforms bones into gold. If that is true, how could one not live forever? Can your divine elixir turn this stone inkstone into gold? If so, I would be a true master of the Dao." Jiang Sheng realized that his efforts had been in vain and felt deeply ashamed.

Translation: 1. And he rejected it with other words, saying, "You, a hired worker, how could you know about the affairs of immortals? If you speak falsely, you will bring upon yourself the punishment and disgrace of beating and cursing." Quansu laughed and left. 2. After a month, Quansu took out a very small gourd from his clothes and turned to Jiangsheng, saying, "There is immortal elixir in this gourd that can turn stone into gold. I wish to get your stone inkstone and apply a small amount of elixir on it, can I?" 3. Jiangsheng was light-hearted and impulsive, and he considered it absurd, scolding and cursing, "I have studied alchemy for ten years, but I have not yet fully grasped its subtleties. How dare a hired worker speak to me so persistently!" 4. Quansu pretended to be afraid and did not reply. The next day, Jiangsheng went alone to the mountains and waters, ordered Quansu to guard the house, and then locked the door and left. 5. When he returned at dusk, he saw that Quansu had already died. Jiangsheng then covered the body with a mat, prepared a coffin, and buried it in the wild. 6. When he removed the mat, Quansu's body had disappeared, leaving only his hat, belt, clothes, and shoes behind. Jiangsheng was greatly surprised and considered Quansu to be a deity who had attained the Dao. 7. He immediately looked at the stone inkstone on the table, and it was also gone. Jiangsheng was even more surprised. 8. The next day, Jiangsheng saw light under the alchemy furnace, saying, "Isn't this my immortal elixir?" He immediately searched for it in the ashes, found the stone inkstone, and part of it had turned into purple gold, shining brightly and clearly, this was the result of Quansu's immortal elixir. 9. Jiangsheng finally realized that Quansu was indeed an immortal, regretted not recognizing it earlier and felt even more ashamed and resentful. 10. Later, Jiangsheng continued to study alchemy but failed, and he eventually died in Siming Mountain.

He dismissed him with other words, saying, "You are a hired worker—how could you possibly know anything about immortals? If you speak recklessly, you will bring upon yourself the punishment of beating and scolding." Quanshu laughed and left. After a month, Quanshu produced from his clothing a very small gourd, and turning to Jiang Sheng, said, "This gourd contains an immortal elixir, which can turn stone into gold. Might I ask you to place a single grain of it upon your stone inkstone?" Jiang Sheng, who was naturally impulsive and dismissive, scoffed and said, "I have studied alchemy for ten years and have yet to grasp its mysteries. How dare you, a mere servant, argue with me so boldly?" Quanshu feigned fear and said nothing. The next day, Jiang Sheng went alone to wander among the mountains and waters, instructing Quanshu to guard the house, and then locked the door and left. When he returned in the evening, he found Quanshu already dead. Jiang Sheng then covered the body with bamboo mats and prepared to place it in a coffin and bury it in the fields. When he removed the mats, Quanshu’s body was gone, leaving only his cap, belt, clothes, and shoes behind. Jiang Sheng was greatly astonished and believed Quanshu to be a sage who had attained immortality. He looked upon the stone inkstone on the table, and it too was gone. He was even more amazed. On the following day, Jiang Sheng saw a light beneath his alchemy furnace and said, "Could this be my immortal elixir?" He reached into the ashes and retrieved the stone inkstone, whose upper inch had turned into a brilliant purple gold, shining clearly. It was indeed the transformation caused by Quanshu’s immortal elixir. Jiang Sheng then realized that Quanshu was truly a immortal, and he was filled with regret and sorrow for not recognizing him sooner. Afterwards, Jiang Sheng continued studying alchemy but ultimately failed, and in the end, he died on Simuming Mountain.

**True Person of Yin** In the eastern several miles of Jianwei Prefecture, there is a Taoist temple nestled in a deep cave. The temple is surrounded on all sides by stone walls, and a Taoist named Yan lived there. In the temple hall, there is a stone box, more than three feet long. On top of the box, birds, beasts, and flowers are intricately carved, their patterns delicate and exquisite, nearly divine in quality. The box is sealed and locked with extreme security, without the slightest gap. The villagers passed down the story, saying it is the stone box of Yin Xi. The True Person’s deeds are well-documented in the annals. As he was about to ascend to immortality, he entrusted the stone box to his disciples, warning them, "This box contains magic talismans. Be cautious and do not open it, or there will surely be great disaster." Thus, all the people of the region held it in deep reverence.

In the Da Li era, there was a man named Cui Jun from Qinghe who served as the governor of Jianwei. Cui Jun had always relied on his own strength and determination. Upon arriving in the prefecture, he heard about a box belonging to Master Yin, a Daoist master, and laughed at his subordinates, saying, "This is just like the deceit of Xin Yuanping." He immediately went to the box and ordered it to be opened. Yan, a Daoist priest, said, "This is Master Yin's stone box. The master left a message saying, 'Those who open my box will suffer great misfortune.' I hope Your Excellency does not violate the agreement with the immortal officials." Cui Jun became angry and said, "Master Yin has been dead for a thousand years. How can he alone have a stone box? I don't believe it." Despite Yan's insistence, Cui Jun refused to listen. Cui Jun then ordered the lock to be broken, but after a long time, the box remained firmly closed. Angered, he attached a large rope to the box and used dozens of oxen to pull it. After half a day, the stone box finally opened. Inside were dozens of scrolls of talismans, written on yellow silk with red ink, all looking fresh and vibrant. After viewing them, Cui Jun turned to Yan and said, "I thought there might be a treasure inside the box, so I opened it to see. Now, it turns out there are only talismans." He then ordered the box to be sealed and locked as before.

After returning to the prefecture, that night, the magistrate suddenly died. Three days later, he woke up. All officials and subordinates went to ask Mr. Cui and interrogated him. Mr. Cui said, "I am very foolish and have never heard of any immortal matters. Earlier, I accidentally opened the stone box of the true person Yin, and was indeed chased and captured by the officials of the underworld. Initially, I saw a person wearing purple clothes, who came to my bed and said to me, 'I am an official of the underworld. Now I am sent to summon you, and certainly cannot be resisted. If resisted, the disaster will be even greater, so you should leave quickly.' I initially felt worried and wanted to refuse with excuses, but unconsciously went out of the prefecture city with the messenger, only traveled fifty miles, and reached the underworld. The official there was the former prime minister Lü Gong. He said to me, 'Why did you open the stone box of the true person Yin? By the command of the Supreme Deity, and will reduce your salary and lifespan, what will happen?' Then he summoned the clerks and ordered to check my salary and lifespan records. The clerk reported to Lü Gong, 'Mr. Cui has served in five positions and has a lifespan of seventeen years. Now by the command of the Supreme Deity, will take away all five positions and reduce fifteen years of lifespan. Now only have two years left.' Then allowed Mr. Cui to return. Two years later, he indeed died.

Fang Jian, the Duke of Qinghe, resided in Huan Mountain Prefecture. He had a nature that favored the extraordinary and enjoyed the teachings of the Tao. He often received talismans of the Six Days and the *Nine Chapters of True Talismans* from Taoists, accumulating twenty years of practice. Later, he traveled south to Mount Heng, where he met a Taoist with a clear and refined demeanor. The Taoist spoke with Jian about the marvelous events of the Upper Clear Palace and the Isles of Penglai, describing each in detail as if he had experienced them firsthand. Jian found this extraordinary. After about ten days, Jian left Mount Heng for the South Sea. The Taoist told Jian, “I once stayed in the South Sea for ten years. There was an official named Li who commanded his troops. Li gave me a jade hairpin, which I gave to you as a gift. You should treasure it.” Jian received the hairpin, greatly pleased, and then parted ways. That autumn, Jian arrived at the South Sea. One day, he visited the Kaiyuan Temple alone. In the northern hall of the temple, there were two brick statues in the likeness of immortals. The one to the east was the Left Xuan True Person. Upon seeing the statue, Jian realized it was the Taoist he had met at Mount Heng. He marveled and remained in awe for a long time. When he noticed the Left Xuan’s crown, he saw that the hairpin was missing. At that time, several Taoists were staying at the temple. Jian explained the entire story to them and then showed them the jade hairpin. The Taoists were astonished and said, “Ten years ago, there was an official named Li who commanded troops in the South Sea. He once used two jade hairpins to adorn the statues of the Left and Right True Persons. It has been nearly ten years since the hairpin on the Left Xuan was lost. The hairpin you have found is indeed the one we lost.” Jian was amazed and returned the jade hairpin to the Taoist.

Li He, whose courtesy name was Changji, was a descendant of the Prince of Zheng of the Tang Dynasty. From a young age, he was skilled in writing, particularly excelling in *yuefu* poetry, known for its novel ideas and beautiful language. At the time, no one dared to compare themselves with him, and thus his fame spread throughout the world. However, because his father's name was Jinsu, he could not take the imperial exams for the position of Jinshi. He died at the age of twenty-four while serving as an official in the Taichang office. His mother, Lady Zheng, loved her son deeply. After Li He's death, she was unable to relieve her grief. One night, she dreamed that Li He came to her, as if he were alive, and said to her, "I am fortunate to be your son, and you love me deeply. Therefore, I followed my parents' wishes from a young age, learning poetry and writing. The reason for this is not just to seek a position for self-decoration, but also to bring honor to our family and repay your kindness. Who would have thought that one day I would die, unable to provide daily care, which is indeed a source of regret! However, I did not die; it was the command of the Heavenly Emperor." Lady Zheng asked him about the matter, and Li He replied, "The Heavenly Emperor is the ruler of gods, immortals, and deities. Recently, he moved the capital to the Moon Garden, constructed a new palace, and named it 'White Jade.' Because I am skilled in literature, he summoned me and several other scholars to write the *New Palace Record* together. The emperor also built the Hall of Ethereal Tranquility and ordered us to compose musical pieces. Now, I am among the immortals, very happy. I hope you have no worries." Afterward, he departed. Lady Zheng awoke, finding the dream very strange, and her grief somewhat eased.

**Hou Dao Hua** In Yongle County of the Hezhong region, there was a Daoist temple called Daojing, situated in a beautiful location within the area of Pu. There were several Daoists living there. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, a Daoist named Deng Tai Xuan was refining elixirs in the medicine hall. When the elixir was completed, he suspected that the work was not yet fully realized, so he left it stored in the courtyard for everyone to watch over. After Deng Tai Xuan's death, his disciple Zhou Wuxian took charge of the temple. At that time, there was a young man from Pu named Hou Dao Hua who served as an attendant to Zhou Wuxian. The other Daoists treated him like a slave, making him perform menial tasks such as sweeping and cleaning, and he did everything they asked without complaint, but he remained cheerful. He also loved history and literature, always reading books and reciting them by heart. Sometimes others would ask him, "Why do you need this?" He would reply, "There are no foolish or ignorant immortals in heaven." Everyone laughed at him. The region of Pu was known for its large dates, and it was said that in a year, there were only one or two seedless ones. Hou Dao Hua, however, managed to eat seedless dates every three years. One day, he held an axe and cut the drooping branches of an ancient pine tree, and they fell as if they had been shaved off. No one in the temple understood the meaning of his actions. The next morning, before dawn, when everyone got up, Hou Dao Hua's room was empty. Under the ancient pine tree, a table had been placed, with a cup of water on it. He had taken off his shoes and placed them in front of the table, and his clothes were hung on the pine tree. The monks looked around and found a poem left inside the tree, which read:

Wrapped in a small cloth, the great elixir of return, Its color remains unchanged for many years. Last night, it was stolen and consumed, Today, it flies in the azure sky. Ashamed and deeply cherishing, Highly value the Taoist master Deng. In the future, when the elixir is refined, Keep it for Neizhi. My teacher knows this technique, Quickly refine, do not be late. The Three Pure Ones especially wait for you, The highest celestial realm has a fixed time. Below are the small characters, which read: "Last July 1st, I was granted the surname Li and the given name Neizhi by Lord Han, and was assigned to reside in the Shangqing Shanjin Hall." Following these words, there are several sentences. It was on the 21st day of the fifth month in the fifth year of the Dazhong era of the Tang Dynasty. The people in the hall verified that Dao Hua had stolen the Supreme Mystery Elixir and ascended to immortality. They then collectively reported this to the Military Governor and Grand Secretary Zheng Gongguang. After inspecting the traces and finding them to be true, they reported the matter to the emperor. The emperor then ordered the distribution of 500 bolts of silk, bestowed imperial robes, renovated the halls and pavilions, and named the place "The Hall of Ascending Immortality."

**Translation:** The Story of Lüqiu Zi There was Zheng Youxuan from Xingyang, a son of a distinguished family. He lived in Chang'an and, from a young age, studied alongside the son of a neighbor from the Lüqiu family at the school of a teacher. Zheng Youxuan was arrogant, often boasting about his family's high status. He would mock and insult the Lüqiu family, saying, "The Lüqiu family is not of my class, and yet I study with them. Even if I do not speak, how can you not feel ashamed in your heart?" Lüqiu Zi, silent and with a look of shame, remained silent. Several years later, Lüqiu Zi died of illness. Ten years later, Zheng Youxuan passed the Mingjing examination with top honors and was subsequently appointed as a military advisor in Tang'an County. Upon arriving at his post, the county governor appointed him as a temporary military officer in Tangxing. There was a fellow student named Chousheng, the son of a wealthy merchant, who was just turning twenty and whose family had assets amounting to tens of thousands. Chousheng often met with Zheng Youxuan. Zheng Youxuan repeatedly received his money and gifts and frequently went out with him. However, Chousheng was not of the scholar-gentry class, and Zheng Youxuan never treated him with courtesy. One day, Zheng Youxuan held a grand gathering and invited many people, but Chousheng was not invited. When the wine was about to run out, someone said to Zheng Youxuan, "Chousheng is your fellow student, and yet he is not invited to this gathering. Is he not at fault?" Zheng Youxuan felt ashamed and immediately summoned Chousheng. When Chousheng arrived, Zheng Youxuan offered him a cup of wine, but Chousheng refused to drink it all. He firmly declined. Zheng Youxuan then angrily shouted, "You commoner from the marketplace, you only know about small gains. How dare you inappropriately hold an official position? It is your good fortune that I am with you, yet how dare you refuse to drink?" He then ripped his clothes and stood up. Chousheng felt deeply ashamed and was pulled away. He then resigned his position, closed his doors, and ceased to interact with others. After several months, he died of illness.

The following year, Zheng was dismissed from office and temporarily lived in a Buddhist temple in Mengyang Commandery. Zheng often admired the teachings of Huang-Lao, and at that time, there was a Daoist priest from Wu who was renowned for his Daoist arts and had built a hut on Shumenshan Mountain. Xuan admired his style and was driven to pay homage, wishing to become his disciple. The Daoist priest said, "Since you admire immortals, you should live in the mountains and forests, and do not be eager and busy in worldly affairs." Xuan happily thanked him, saying, "Sir, you are indeed a truly enlightened person. I wish to serve as a subordinate to you. Is it possible?" The Daoist priest agreed and kept him. For fifteen years, Xuan's resolve gradually weakened. The Daoist priest said, "You cannot maintain a firm resolve, and merely living in the mountains and forests is of no benefit." Xuan then left and leisurely wandered in Mengyang Commandery for a long time.

Later, Yuanxuan traveled eastward into Chang'an, stopping at Baocheng and lodging at an inn. There, he met a child who was about ten years old and had a very beautiful appearance. Yuanxuan conversed with the child, who displayed extraordinary eloquence and wisdom, changing topics effortlessly. Yuanxuan felt that he could not match the child's intelligence. The child then said to Yuanxuan, "I have been friends with you for many years, do you remember?" Yuanxuan replied, "I have forgotten." The child continued, "I once lived in the house of Lüqiu and resided in Chang'an, where we studied together under a teacher. You considered me poor and lowly, and said, 'not of my kind.' Later, I became a son of the Qiu family and served as a magistrate in Tangxing, living in the same quarters with you. You received a lot of money from me, but you never treated me with courtesy, calling me a market person. How arrogant you are!" Yuanxuan was surprised and then bowed twice, apologizing, "It is indeed my fault. But you are not a sage, how can you know about three lives?" The child replied, "I am a true person of the Supreme Clarity. The Heavenly Lord has given you the breath of the Dao, so he sent me to live among humans, to be your friend, and to teach you the secrets of true immortality. But you are proud by nature, and you will never achieve the Dao. Alas, it is sad!" After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. Yuanxuan then understood the matter, feeling very ashamed and angry, and eventually died of worry.

**The Hermit Yuan** During the reign of Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a hermit named Yuan who lived in the region of Xiang and Chu. He was skilled in the "Yin-Yang Divination Song" consisting of one hundred and twenty chapters. At that time, the former chancellor Li Jifu, who had been demoted to the southeast from his position as a high-ranking official, received a visit from the hermit. Li Jifu had long heard of the hermit's reputation and invited him to speak. Li Jifu asked the hermit to calculate his own fortune and career. The hermit said, "Your fortune truly leads to the position of chancellor. Your lifespan will be ninety-three years." Li Jifu replied, "My ancestors have never lived past seventy, how could I dare to hope for ninety-three years?" The hermit said, "When calculating the numbers and figures, it is indeed ninety-three." Later, Li Jifu indeed became the chancellor of Emperor Xianzong, governed the region of Huainan, and then returned to the capital to serve as chancellor again before passing away at the age of fifty-six on October 3, 814. When comparing the year, month, and day of his death, it also corresponded to the number ninety-three. Is this not a profound insight into the mysteries of divination? The hermit wrote the "Yin-Yang Divination Song," and Li Jifu wrote a preface for it.

程逸人 In Shangdang, there lived a man known as Cheng Yiren, who was skilled in the use of talismans. Liu Wu served as the military governor of the Ze-Lu region. A wealthy commoner named Xiao Jiping from Linzhao County died suddenly without any illness. Cheng Yiren had once received great kindness from Xiao Jiping. Upon hearing of his death, Cheng Yiren immediately rushed to see him and told Xiao Jiping's son, "Your father was not meant to die; he was summoned by the mountain god. If we treat him, he can still be saved." Cheng Yiren then wrote a talisman in red ink, threw it into the air, and within a short while, Xiao Jiping indeed revived. His son asked his father, "How were you feeling just now?" Xiao Jiping replied, "I just woke up today and suddenly saw a man in green clothing who said, 'The god of Huoshan Mountain has summoned you.' I then accompanied the messenger on a journey of about fifty miles when we encountered a man in red clothing, wielding a sword and glaring fiercely, who came down from the sky and said to me, 'Cheng Zhanxie has summoned you; you may go now.' The man in green clothing then fled in fear. The man in red clothing took me back with him, and after a short while, I suddenly woke up." The family was amazed and questioned Cheng Yiren, "Who is this Cheng Zhanxie you mentioned?" Cheng Yiren replied, "I studied the dragon and tiger talismans of exorcism from the masters of Shi and Gui." He then showed the talisman bag he was wearing, and the people finally believed him. Later, Cheng Yiren traveled to Minyue, but eventually disappeared without a trace.

Camel Hair

Camel Hair, a resident of Zhaozhou's Zhaqing district, was a minor official who offended the county magistrate and thus fled. The magistrate, angered by this, ordered a widespread and urgent search. Consequently, Camel Hair hid himself in the mountains and valleys. Suddenly, he encountered an old man dressed in coarse clothing, with an ordinary and unremarkable appearance, standing under a tall pine tree with a staff in hand. The old man called out to Camel Hair and asked, "How did you end up here?" Camel Hair replied, "I offended the county magistrate and fled here. Thank you for taking me in." The old man led Camel Hair into the deep mountains, walking only about ten or more miles, until they reached a cave. Inside, they saw two thatched huts facing each other east and west, with a pool of water in front. Precious trees and exotic flowers were arranged on both sides. There was also a young servant, who was quite young, with a child's hairstyle, wearing short, coarse clothing, a white robe, a belt, and leather shoes, living in the western hut.

 - **Camel Hair**: This is a direct translation of the name 骆玄素, which is rendered as "Camel Hair" to maintain consistency and avoid confusion with other names. - **赵州昭庆**: This is translated as "Zhaozhou's Zhaqing district" to provide context and clarity. - **县令**: This is translated as "county magistrate" to accurately reflect the role of the local administrative official. - **遁迹而去**: This is translated as "fled" to convey the act of escaping. - **分捕甚急**: This is translated as "widespread and urgent search" to capture the intensity of the pursuit. - **质状凡陋**: This is translated as "ordinary and unremarkable appearance" to describe the old man's looks. - **策杖立于长松之下**: This is translated as "standing under a tall pine tree with a staff in hand" to set the scene. - **尔安得至此耶**: This is translated as "How did you end up here?" to capture the old man's question. - **幸翁见容**: This is translated as "Thank you for taking me in" to convey gratitude. - **引玄素入深山**: This is translated as "led Camel Hair into the deep mountains" to describe the journey. - **仅行十余里**: This is translated as "walking only about ten or more miles" to indicate the distance. - **至一岩穴**: This is translated as "until they reached a cave" to describe the destination. - **二茅斋东西相向**: This is translated as "two thatched huts facing each other east and west" to describe the layout. - **前临积水**: This is translated as "with a pool of water in front" to describe the setting. - **珍木奇花，罗列左右**: This is translated as "Precious trees and exotic flowers were arranged on both sides" to describe the surroundings. - **有侍童一人，年甚少，总角，衣短褐，白衣纬带革舄，居于西斋**: This is translated as "There was also a young servant, who was quite young, with a child's hairstyle, wearing short, coarse clothing, a white robe, a belt, and leather shoes, living in the western hut" to describe the young servant.

○ Luo Xunsu A man from Zhaozhou Zhaoqing named Luo Xunsu, who served as a minor official, incurred the wrath of the county magistrate and thus fled into hiding. The magistrate, enraged, launched an urgent search, forcing Luo to conceal himself in the mountains. Suddenly, he encountered an old man dressed in a coarse brown robe, of plain and unremarkable appearance, standing with a staff beneath a tall pine tree. The old man called out to Luo and questioned him, saying, “How have you come to be here?” Luo replied, “I have offended the county magistrate and fled here; I am fortunate that you, sir, would receive me.” The old man led Luo deep into the mountains, traveling only about ten miles before arriving at a cave. There, they saw two thatched pavilions facing each other, with a pool of water in front of them, and rare trees and exotic flowers arranged on either side. Inside the western pavilion lived a young attendant, very young, with braided hair, wearing a short brown robe, a white robe with a woven belt, and leather shoes.

In his eastern study, there was a medicine stove, and he ordered Xuan Su to watch the fire. An old man introduced himself as the Eastern True Lord and instructed Xuan Su to address him as such. The Eastern True Lord gave Xuan Su more than ten pills of medicine and told him, "These can cure hunger." From then on, Xuan Su abstained from eating solid food. After barely a year, he was taught talismanic techniques and breathing methods, mastering them all. One day, the Eastern True Lord said to Xuan Su, "You may return now." He then escorted Xuan Su to several miles south of the county, where they parted ways with a handshake. From then on, Xuan Su practiced talismanic magic in the village. There was once a pregnant woman who did not give birth on time. Xuan Su gave her a talisman to eat, and that night she gave birth, with the child holding the talisman in his hand. Other miraculous effects were too numerous to describe. Later, Xuan Su broke the law and was beaten to death by the governor. For more than a month, his body remained as if alive, showing no signs of decay, due to the elixir he had consumed. The villagers then buried him. It was the summer of the first year of the Baoli era.

Yushou During the time when Shangshu Wang Gong Qian was the governor of Jingnan, there was a man surnamed Lü who was wearing worn-out clothes and carrying a staff, showing signs of hunger and cold. He presented a card and came to pay respects. The governor did not show him courtesy, leaving him very displeased. Consequently, he stayed at an inn. After a month, his poverty and destitution worsened, and he eventually sold his donkey at the Jingzhou market. There was an elderly gatekeeper named Yu who summoned Lü and spoke to him, asking about his circumstances. Lü said, "I come from the north of Wei River. My family is poor, and my parents are old and unable to provide them with the nourishment they deserve. The governor is my great uncle. I traveled far to seek his compassion and assistance. I came to pay respects, but he did not even glance at me. Is this not fate?" Yu replied, "Although I am poor and have no means to provide food to help you in your dire situation, I felt very indignant when I saw you showing signs of hunger and cold earlier. Tonight, I will prepare food for you, and I hope you will stay under my roof. You cannot refuse." Lü agreed, and Yu invited him into a room. The room was small, low, and dilapidated, with a collapsed roof and broken walls, lacking beds, cushions, and mats. Yu placed a worn-out mat on the ground and sat down with Lü. After talking for a long time, he ordered food to be served, which consisted of only a simple meal of millet porridge in a pottery dish. After finishing the meal, it was already late at night. Yu then said to Lü, "In my early years, I was fond of the Dao and often lived in seclusion on Sima Mountain, learning techniques to reverse aging from Daoist masters. Despite my aspirations, I have not succeeded, and I have hidden myself here for nearly ten years, and no one in Jingnan knows about me. Seeing that you are suffering in your travels, I wonder if it has moved your heart. Tonight, I will perform a small trick for you to provide the means for your journey home. Would that not be acceptable?" Although Lü was skeptical and found it strange, he was also intrigued by Yu's words.

The old man then took a jar and placed it on the ground. After a meal's time, he lifted it and looked inside, seeing a figure about five inches tall, wearing a purple sash and a golden belt, who bowed respectfully. The old man pointed and said, "This is the spirit of the official, Wáng Gōng, the Grand Secretary." Lǚ, upon closely examining the figure's appearance, found it indeed resembled Wáng Gōng, and he secretly found it strange. The old man then warned, "Lǚ is your cousin. His family is very poor, unable to provide daily sustenance, so he has come from the north of Wèi not far away. You should provide generous lodging and food, fully fulfilling the duties of kinship. Why are you proud and not even giving him a glance? Is this human nature? I will not punish you, but you should provide generously, so that he does not become a lingering guest." The figure in purple bowed and seemed to accept the instruction. The old man added, "Lǚ has no horse or servant; you can provide one horse, one servant, and two hundred bolts of silk for him." The figure in purple bowed again. The old man then placed the jar on top and after a while lifted it again, but there was nothing to be seen inside. The next morning, as dawn was approaching, the old man told Lǚ, "You will depart soon; the official will summon you shortly." Upon returning to his inn, the official indeed sent for Lǚ. When they met, the official apologized, saying, "You have come all the way to visit me, but due to the busy affairs of the military office, I failed to have a conversation with you for a day, and I deeply feel ashamed. Please understand." That day, the official provided lodging for Lǚ at the驿站亭 (yì zhàn tíng), and they enjoyed feasts and outings for several days. When Lǚ was about to leave, the official gave him a servant, a horse, and two hundred bolts of silk. Lǚ found the whole experience even more strange, but he dared not speak about it. After returning to the north of Wèi, a few years later, because he met with several friends for a night's stay, and spoke about spirits and strange occurrences, he began to tell others about the matter.

A Man Named Shi Min There was a man named Shi Min, whose origins were unknown. He had been wandering aimlessly for many years. His mystical arts were profound and almost impossible to comprehend. During the Longqing era, he resided as a guest in Wanzhou. A son of the Lei family, who was a military commander in Xuanzhou, once held a drinking party at a villa in the south of the county, and Shi Min was also present. One of his servants caught a fish with a net, which was several feet long, and brought it to the house. On that day, Lei and his guests were all deeply drunk, and all the guests left, leaving only Shi Min to stay at the Lei family's villa. It was the height of summer, and the next day, when they looked at the fish, it had already spoiled and was inedible. The servant was about to discard it, but Shi Min said to Lei, "This fish may be spoiled, but I have a good medicine that can still revive it; how can we throw it away?" Lei laughed and said, "Master, you are mistaken. If you truly have a good medicine, how could you revive this fish?" Shi Min replied, "My dear, please observe." He then took out a small bag from his clothes, which contained several pills, and threw them onto the spoiled fish.

In a short while, the fish was as fresh and moist as when it was first caught, and soon it began to shake its fins and scales, as if it were in a great river. Reborn was amazed and bowed twice in gratitude, saying, "Your artistry, Sir, can truly be called divine. We common folk, deaf and blind to the world, look up to your lofty conduct, as if a frog in a well compared to a bird in the sky, how could we possibly be in the same league?" Previously, Reborn had suffered from a long-standing minor illness. He then sought the elixir in Min's clothing, hoping to alleviate his long-term suffering. Min refused, saying, "My elixir is extremely pure and clear. You common people have no restraint in your desires, and within your bodies, filth and stench accumulate. If I were to place my elixir inside you, the impurities in your body would clash with the power of the medicine, like water and fire battling each other. Could anyone survive such a conflict? Do not eat it." Min also said, "It is not difficult to attain immortality, but the world is full of entanglements. If you are like a monkey in a cage or a bird in a cage, with only the heart of soaring and leaping, how can you achieve it?" In the reign period of Emperor Xuanzong, during the years 841-846, Min passed away in Wu County.

There was once a recluse named Yang Jushi in Nanhai Prefecture, whose name has been lost to history, but who referred to himself as a scholar-retreatee. He often traveled to the outlying districts of Nanhai, lodging with people for food and never knowing where he would stay. He would say to others, "I have a special skill, one that you common folk cannot possibly understand." Later, he would frequently visit the prefecture, and when the governor, who was curious by nature, heard that Yang Jushi had arrived, he was very pleased and treated him with great respect, inviting him to drink. At every banquet and gathering, Yang Jushi was always the first to be summoned. He took pride in this. One day, he drank to excess and offended the governor, who could no longer tolerate him. Later, another banquet was held in the prefectural hall, where female entertainers performed, but Yang Jushi was not invited. At that time, there were several guests who had also not been invited by the governor. They said to Yang Jushi, "Sir, you have always boasted of having a special skill. I have long admired you from afar. It is truly fortunate to meet you here today. However, now that I hear the governor is hosting a grand banquet in the prefectural hall, and you are not invited, can you not perform a special skill to impress us? Or do you really not have any special skills at all?" Yang Jushi smiled and said, "This is just a trivial skill." Then he said, "You just watch me. I will summon the female entertainers for you, and they can help entertain us with their performances."

Everyone said, "We are willing to do it." The recluse then ordered wine to be prepared, and had all the guests sit in a circle around the seating area. He also ordered a young boy to close the empty room in the western annex. After a long time, the door was opened, and there were three or four beautiful women who came down from the wing, adorned with magnificent decorations, and carrying musical instruments. The recluse said, "What do you think of my skill?" The guests were greatly surprised and found it almost impossible to comprehend. He then had them sit in rows, and they began to play music and sing. Some guests asked about his skill, but the recluse only smiled and did not answer. It was dark, and by midnight, the recluse told the women, "It is time to return." They all stood up and entered the empty room in the western annex. The guests looked at each other in amazement and exclaimed, but they still doubted that it might be the work of ghosts or bewitching spirits. The next day, an official from the county said, "Last night, the governor held a feast at the county hall, and the entertainers sat in rows. Soon, they all fell to the ground. In an instant, a strong wind arose, carrying their musical instruments away. By midnight, the entertainers awoke, and the instruments had returned to their original place. The governor questioned the entertainers, and they all said they saw nothing in the darkness. He could not fully understand the reason behind it." The guests were greatly surprised and recounted the events to the governor, and some reported it to him. The governor exclaimed at the strangeness of it and immediately apologized and sent them away, not daring to keep them in the county. This was during the early years of the Kai Cheng reign.

○冯渐 Feng Jian, a son of a prominent family from Hedong, entered officialdom through his knowledge of Confucian classics. His nature was contrary to the common ways, and he later gave up his official position to live in seclusion on the Yi River. There was a Daoist adept named Li Jun who was renowned for his Daoist skills, especially skilled in seeing ghosts. Officials all admired his abilities. Li Jun later retired to the regions of Ru and Ying, where he met Feng Jian in the area between the Yi and Luo rivers. Knowing that Feng Jian possessed extraordinary skills, he held him in high regard. During the Dali era, there was a prominent official from Boling named Cui Gong, who was a colleague of Li Jun and had a good relationship with him. Li Jun wrote to Cui Gong, saying, "In controlling ghosts today, no one surpasses Feng Jian." At that time, officials throughout the capital knew of Feng Jian's divine skills and often mentioned his name. After their parting, people in Chang'an often wrote the character "渐" on their doors, using it for this reason. ○王先生 There was a man named Mr. Wang who lived by the U River. He hid his traces, so the villagers could not recognize him, and some thought he was a sorcerer or a charlatan. One day, a fire broke out in the village, spreading to the houses. Mr. Wang went to see it and shouted loudly, "The fire will stop, the fire will stop." The fire then ceased. The villagers began to marvel at him. During the Changqing era, there was a scholar from Hongnong named Yang Hui Zhi, who traveled east from Chang'an to Wu and Chu. When he reached the U River, he heard of Mr. Wang's esteemed character and went to visit him. Mr. Wang wore a black silk cap and a brown robe, leaning on a table and sitting. He had a clear and beautiful demeanor. Yang Hui Zhi performed the proper courtesies, bowing twice, and Mr. Wang only bowed and gestured in return, inviting Yang Hui Zhi to sit beside him. His discourse was profound and smooth, far exceeding expectations. Yang Hui Zhi became even more admiring and stayed overnight. It was the twelfth day of the eighth month.

The master summoned his daughter, Miss Qi Niang, who was an elderly woman, over seventy years old, with completely white hair, and she came leaning on a staff. The master said to Hui Zhi, "This is my daughter, who is lazy and not fond of the Dao, and now she is old." Afterward, he told Miss Qi Niang, "You should carve paper to depict the moon of this night and place it on the eastern wall of the room." After a while, Miss Qi Niang placed the paper moon on the wall. In the evening, a strange light spontaneously appeared, illuminating the entire room so clearly that everything was visible. Hui Zhi was amazed and could not fathom it. When dawn came, he was about to leave, and the master drew a line on the ground with his staff. Suddenly, dust arose, and the sky and earth became completely dark. After a while, the dust settled, and when he looked at the courtyard, it was steep and dangerous cliffs, with overlapping valleys, and in front of him were mountains of stones stretching as far as the eye could see. Hui Zhi was so frightened that his heart was pounding, his back was drenched in sweat, and his hair stood on end. The master said, "Mountains and valleys have quickly transformed. Where will you go now, my son?" Hui Zhi was even more afraid and wept, saying, "I truly did not know that there would be such a sudden change in the world. Is it that the immortal realm has changed in an instant, while the mortal world has passed a thousand years?" The master smiled and said, "Do not be afraid. It is just for entertainment." Then he swept the courtyard with a broom, and dust arose again. After a while, the dust settled, and the courtyard returned to its original state. Hui Zhi was happy and rode away on his horse.

A Tale of Zhou Sheng During the middle of the Tang dynasty, there was a man named Zhou Sheng who lived in Dongting Mountain. He was known for using his Daoist skills to help the people of Wu and Chu, and many people held him in high regard. Later, he was traveling through Guangling and stayed at a Buddhist temple. On this night, around the Mid-Autumn Festival, the moon was clear and bright. Zhou Sheng and several guests were reciting poetry and admiring the moon. One of them mentioned the legendary story of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (also known as Ming Huang) visiting the Moon Palace during the Kaiyuan era. They sighed together, saying, "We ordinary people cannot reach that place. What can we do?" Zhou Sheng responded, "I have often studied with my teacher and have also achieved it. Moreover, I can bring the moon into my sleeves. Do you believe me?" Some guests doubted his claim, while others were intrigued by its strangeness. Zhou Sheng said, "If I am not clear, then it is false." He then ordered a room to be emptied and covered the walls so that there was no small gap. He also arranged hundreds of chopsticks and called his servant to tie them up and arrange them. He told the guests, "I will climb this to take the moon away. If you hear the call, you can come to see." He closed the door and stayed inside for a long time. The guests walked around the courtyard, waiting. Suddenly, they felt the sky dimming. Looking up, they saw that there was no cloud. Soon, they heard Zhou Sheng call out, "I have arrived!" He opened the door, and said, "The moon is in my clothes. Please come and see." He then lifted his clothes, and a bit of the moon appeared, making the whole room bright and cold. Zhou Sheng said, "You did not believe me before, do you believe me now?" The guests bowed twice and thanked him, wishing to collect its light. He closed the door again, and outside it was still dim. After a mealtime, everything returned to its original state.

In the Yuanhe period of the Tang dynasty, there was a monk named Hui Zhao at Kaiyuan Temple in Wuling Prefecture. He was frail and weak in appearance, often speaking of people's joys and sorrows, always hitting the mark. By nature, he was introverted and solitary, not socializing with others, often staying in seclusion, with no attendants. He often begged food from the locals. An elderly person in his eighties among the locals said, "Master Zhao has lived here for sixty years, and his appearance is no different from before. But I do not know his age." Later, there was Chen Guang, who became an official in Wuling through the Xiu-lian examination. Chen Guang was fond of Buddhism. One day, he visited the temple and met all the monks. When he reached Hui Zhao's room, Hui Zhao saw Chen Guang and said with both sorrow and joy, "Why did you come so late, Mr. Chen?" Chen Guang was startled, thinking he had never met Hui Zhao before, and asked, "I have never been acquainted with the master, why are you surprised that I came late?" Hui Zhao replied, "This cannot be fully explained immediately. I will have a quiet talk with you tonight." Chen Guang found this strange. The next day, he still visited Hui Zhao to stay overnight and asked about the matter. Hui Zhao then said, "I am a descendant of the Liu family, from Pengcheng, the great-great-grandson of Emperor Xiaowen of Song. My great-grandfather was Prince of Poyang, Xiu Yi, and my grandfather was Shi Hong, both of whom are well-documented in historical records. My ancestors were proud of their literary achievements and were known to Prince Zhi Liang of Jingling of Qi. Prince Zhi Liang invited talented scholars and literary figures, and my ancestors were among them. Later, they served as officials during the Qi and Liang dynasties, with my ancestors serving as the magistrate of Kuaiji. I was born in the fifth month of the seventh year of the Tongguan era of the Liang dynasty. I began my career at the age of thirty in the Chen dynasty, and by the time of Emperor Xuan of Chen, I held a low-ranking position, not well-known."

I formed a friendship with Shen Yanwen of Wu Prefecture based on poetry and wine. Later, Prince Shu Jian of Changsha and Prince Shu Ling of Shixing both widely gathered guests, making great shows of power and influence. Each relied on their power and favor, harboring discontent. I and Yanwen were both under the patronage of Prince Shu Jian of Changsha. When Prince Shu Ling was executed, I and Yanwen feared that Prince Shu Jian would not escape punishment, and thus our misfortune would follow. So we fled together, hiding in the mountains and forests. We lived on acorns and chestnuts, wearing a single short robe, without changing even through the seasons. One day, an old monk came to where I lived. He said, "Your bones are very unusual, you should not fall ill." Yanwen also bowed and asked for his medicine. The monk replied, "You do not have the lifespan of Liu Jun, how can you, even if you take my medicine, be any better?" So he took his leave. As he was leaving, he said to me, "In the mundane world, people compete for fame and profit, what do they ultimately gain? Only the Buddhist path can transcend this." I admired his words, and from then on, I knew nothing of worldly affairs for fifteen years. Later, I and Yanwen both went to Jianye. At that time, the Chen dynasty had already fallen, and the imperial palaces were all ruined. The Taicheng was desolate, with brambles covering the roads. The Jingyang and Jieqi palaces still stood, though empty. The scholars and cultural artifacts were gone, nothing to see.

Old friends met, clasped their sleeves, and wept, saying, "The last emperor was luxurious and debauched, and was destroyed by the Sui dynasty, truly a sad thing!" I too could not stop weeping. They then asked about the last emperor and the various princes of the Chen dynasty, who had all entered Chang'an. I immediately went with Yanwen, carrying a bag, begging for food along the way until we reached Guanzhong. I was a former guest of Changsha, who had treated me with great favor. When I heard that he had moved to Guazhou, I went straight to pay respects. Changsha had grown up in luxury and became prominent early, but even during his exile, he still did not engage in productive work. At that time, he was drinking with Consort Shen, and I and Yanwen kneeled and bowed twice in front of him. Changsha grieved for a long time, shed tears, and stood up, then said to me, "One day our country fell, our family members were scattered, isn't it the will of heaven?" From then on, I stayed in Guazhou. Several years later, Changsha died. Another few years passed, and Yanwen also died. I then shaved my head, became a monk, and retreated to a Buddhist temple in Kuaiji Mountain, staying there for twenty years. By then, I was a hundred years old. Although my appearance was withered and thin, my strength did not decline, and I still walked a hundred miles a day. I then went to Chang'an with a monk. At that time, the Tang emperor ruled, established the era name Wu德, and had been reigning for six years. From then on, I sometimes lived in the capital, sometimes traveled to the east of the river, and even went to the three Shus and five ranges, visiting everywhere.

Until now, two hundred and ninety years have passed, and despite extreme cold and heat, he has never had a slight ailment. At the end of the Zhenyuan era, I once dreamed of a man whose attire was very grand. Upon looking at him, I realized he was the King of Changsha. I greeted and invited him to sit, talking about old times, and felt saddened as if it were a lifetime. He then said to me, "In ten years, my sixth-generation descendant Guang will hold office in this prefecture. Teacher, remember this." I asked him, "What is the king doing now?" He replied, "He holds a very high position in the afterlife." Afterward, he wept and said, "Teacher, you still live, but I have already been reborn six times. Alas!" I woke up and recorded your name in my collection of scriptures. By last year, ten years had passed, and I sought your name and surname among the people of the prefecture, still surprised that you had not arrived. Yesterday, while begging for food in the village, I encountered a local official, inquired, and indeed found you. When you came, you still looked like the King of Changsha. However, from the dream until now, it has been eleven years, hence the surprise at your late arrival. Afterward, I felt sad and wept several times. Therefore, I took out the collection of scriptures and showed it to you. Guang then bowed twice and wished to be a disciple under your guidance. Teacher Zhao said, "You may go now, and come again tomorrow." Guang received the instruction and returned. The next day, when he arrived at his residence, Teacher Zhao had already disappeared, and no one knew where he had gone. It was the eleventh year of the Yuanhe era.

In the early years of the Dàhé era, Guang served as an official in the state of Bashu. While traveling through the road of Sichuan, he unexpectedly encountered Zhao, a hermit or spiritual figure. Zhao invited Guang to abandon his official duties and join him on a journey beyond worldly concerns. That night, they stayed together at an inn run by an innkeeper. Before dawn, Guang woke up to find Zhao had already left. From that point on, no one knew where Zhao went. However, Zhao was born in the seventh year of the Tóngpǔ era during the Liang Dynasty, which was the year 526 AD. By the tenth year of the Yuánhé era during the Tang Dynasty, which was 815 AD, Zhao had lived for 290 years. This timeline aligns with what Zhao had said. I often cross-reference the historical records of the Liang and Chen dynasties, and there are many similarities. Therefore, I am increasingly convinced that the story is not false.

In the early years of the Dàhé era, Guang served as a掾 official in Bāzhou, when he suddenly encountered Zhào along the Shǔ Road. Overcome with surprise and joy, Guang bowed twice and said, "I wish to resign from my post and follow my teacher on a journey beyond the world." Zhào agreed. That night, they both stayed at an inn. Before dawn, Guang arose, but Zhào had already departed. From that time onward, his whereabouts remained unknown. However, according to Zhào’s own account, he was born in the seventh year of the Pǔtōng era of the Liang Dynasty. According to the *Liang Shi*, the seventh year of Pǔtōng was the year of *Bǐngwǔ*. From that year to the tenth year of the *Yuánhé* era of the Tang Dynasty, the year *Yǐwèi*, a total of 290 years passed, which indeed corresponds with what Zhào had said. I have often compared the accounts in the official histories of the Liang and Chen dynasties, and found many similarities, thereby increasing my belief that his story is not false.

During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, Tang Xiujing served as Premier. There was once a monk who often spoke accurately and was skilled in charms and spells. Tang Xiujing held him in high regard. One day, the monk came and said to Tang Xiujing, "The Premier will face a great disaster within a few months, but it can be averted." Tang Xiujing was greatly alarmed and immediately bowed to him. The monk said, "I have no other skills, but I offer one plan, which I hope you will consider." Tang Xiujing replied, "I am fortunate to have my teacher's guidance." The monk asked, "Isn't it true that the Premier has the authority to appoint the local governors?" Tang Xiujing answered, "Yes." The monk then said, "The Premier should seek out a lonely, poor, and humble man of talent among the low-ranking officials and appoint him as the Governor of Cao Prefecture. This man will deeply appreciate the Premier's favor and can be the cause of the disaster. Once found, he should be willing to repay me." Tang Xiujing was both pleased and grateful, and so he sought out such a person among his friends and relatives. Zhang Jun, a man from a very poor family and a low-ranking official in the capital, was immediately promoted to the title of "a good official." Ten days later, he was appointed as the Governor of Cao Prefecture. Later, Tang Xiujing summoned the monk and said, "I have followed your plan and found Zhang Jun. Can I now instruct him?" The monk replied, "When Zhang Jun goes to his new post, he should be instructed to find two large and spirited dogs." Tang Xiujing agreed. Zhang Jun, grateful for Tang Xiujing's special favor, did not understand the purpose, and when he was about to leave for his new post, he bid farewell to Tang Xiujing. He then said, "My reputation is obscure, and my talent and knowledge are limited."

The Chancellor has promoted me, who was previously overlooked, and appointed me as the governor of a major region. From a humble savings of a few stones, I have received a salary equivalent to 2000 stones, a high official rank. I have ventured from a dry pond into the East Sea, and ascended from the depths of a valley to the heights of the sky. The kindness is indeed profound. However, beyond gratitude, I privately feel concern and fear, unsure of the Chancellor's intentions. Chancellor Xiu Jing said, "It is solely due to your talent, nothing else. Yet, I often hear that your county has many fine dogs. I wish to obtain two exceptionally fine ones." Zhang Jun replied, "I will follow your instructions." After arriving in the county for several days, he summoned all the county officials and told them, "I have received deep favor from Chancellor Tang Gong, who promoted me beyond my station, allowing me to govern a major region. Now, Chancellor Tang Gong seeks two fine dogs. Can they be obtained?" One official stepped forward and said, "My family has raised a dog with an unusual appearance. I am willing to offer it." Zhang Jun was delighted and immediately accepted the offer. When the dog arrived, it was several feet tall and well-fed, with a broad chest over a foot wide, and an exceptionally fine and spirited demeanor, yet it was also well-behaved. Zhang Jun said, "The Chancellor seeks two, how can this be?" The official replied, "There is only this one in the county, the others are ordinary. However, in a village ten miles south of the county, there is another one. The owner is very fond of it and will not part with it unless you personally go to fetch it." Zhang Jun immediately prepared to travel. He carried a generous gift to visit the village and indeed obtained the dog. The appearance of this dog was the same as the one offered by the official, but its spirit and beauty surpassed the first. Zhang Jun was very pleased and summoned the officials to present the two dogs to Chancellor Xiu Jing. Chancellor Xiu Jing was greatly pleased and found the dogs to be extraordinary, something he had never seen before. He then summoned a monk to examine the dogs. The monk said, "Take good care of them. These two dogs will save you from disaster."

After ten days, the monk returned, telling Xiu Jing, "The matter will occur tonight. I hope you will take serious measures." Xiu Jing immediately invited the monk to stay overnight. That night, Xiu Jing sat in the front hall, ordering more than ten people to hold bows and arrows and stand at the corner of the bed. The monk and Xiu Jing shared a bed. By midnight, the monk smiled and said, "Your disaster is truly averted. You may go to sleep." Xiu Jing was greatly relieved and thanked him. He then dismissed the attendants and went to sleep with the monk. Until dawn, the monk called Xiu Jing, "You may get up now." Xiu Jing got up and said to the monk, "The disaster is truly averted, but what use are the two dogs?" The monk said, "Let's both go and see." They went together to investigate the scene and found a person lying on the ground, dead. Upon closer inspection, they saw blood on the person's neck, indicating that he had been bitten by something.

Xiu Jing saw two dogs under a large tree and looked up, seeing a man baring his body and hiding on top of the tree. Xiu Jing was surprised and interrogated the man, asking, "Who are you?" The man cried and pointed to the dead body, saying, "I and that person were both thieves. We came together last night with the intention to harm the chancellor. We encountered these two dogs, which barked around us, and he was bitten to death. I was afraid, so I hid here, waiting for him to leave so I could escape. By morning, he had not left. Now I am willing to die here." Xiu Jing immediately called his attendants to bind the man. He said, "This crime deserves death, but it was not his will; he was forced by others. I wish to release him." Xiu Jing ordered the man to be unbound, and the thief bowed and cried as he left. Xiu Jing thanked the monk, saying, "Thanks to my teacher, otherwise, I would have died at the hands of these two people." The monk replied, "This is the chancellor's good fortune; it was not my doing." Xiu Jing had a cousin named Lu Zhen in Jingmen. A diviner told him, "You will have misfortune; you should seek someone skilled in warding off evil to help you, perhaps it will work." Lu Zhen knew the monk and wrote a letter to Xiu Jing, requesting his help. The monk gave the letter to Xiu Jing, saying, "The matter is in the letter." When the letter reached Jingmen, Lu Zhen had already died. His family opened the letter and found only a sheet of paper with no writing on it. Xiu Jing was even more amazed. Several years later, the monk disappeared, and no one knew where he went.

# 韦皋 唐故剑南节度使太尉兼中书令韦皋，既生一月，其家召群僧会食。有一胡僧，貌甚陋，不召而至。韦氏家童咸怒之，以弊席坐于庭中。既食，韦氏命乳母出婴儿，请群僧祝其寿。胡僧所自升阶，谓婴儿曰，“别久无恙乎？”婴儿若有喜色。众皆异之。韦氏先君曰：“此子生才一月，吾师何故言别久耶！”胡僧曰：“此非檀越之所知也。”韦氏固问之，胡僧曰：“此子乃诸葛武侯之后身耳。武侯当东汉之季，为蜀丞相，蜀人受其赐且久。今降生于世，将为蜀门帅，且受蜀人之福。吾往岁在剑门，与此子友善。今闻降于韦氏，吾固不远而来。”韦氏异其言，因以武侯字之。后韦氏自少金吾节制剑南军，累迁太尉兼中书令，在蜀十八年，果契胡僧之语也。 # Translation The late Tang official, Grand Commandant and Chief Councillor Wei Gao, who once served as the Military Governor of the South of the Sword, was born after one month. His family invited a group of monks for a meal. Among them was a tramp monk, who was very ugly in appearance, but arrived without being summoned. All the servants of the Wei family were angry at him and made him sit on a worn-out mat in the courtyard. After the meal, Wei ordered the wet nurse to bring out the baby and asked the monks to bless the baby's longevity. The tramp monk ascended the steps himself and said to the baby, "Have you been well since we last met?" The baby seemed to show a joyful expression. Everyone found it strange. Wei's father said, "This child is only one month old; why do you say you have been apart for so long, Master?" The tramp monk replied, "This is not something you can understand, Sir." Wei persisted in asking, and the tramp monk said, "This child is the reincarnation of Zhuge Liang. Zhuge Liang, during the late Eastern Han period, served as the Chancellor of Shu. The people of Shu have benefited from him for a long time. Now he has been reborn in this world and will become the commander of Shu, and will receive blessings from the people of Shu. I was at the Sword Gate in the past and became friends with this child. Now I hear he has been born into the Wei family, so I have come all the way from afar." Wei found his words strange and named the child after Zhuge Liang. Later, Wei rose to control the army in the south of the sword, was gradually promoted to Grand Commandant and Chief Councillor, and served in Shu for eighteen years, fulfilling the tramp monk's words.

○ Wei Gao The former Tang military governor of the Southern Circuit, Grand Marshal and Chief Secretary of the Central Secretariat, Wei Gao, when he was one month old, his family summoned a group of monks for a meal. Among them was a foreign monk, who appeared quite unattractive, yet arrived without being summoned. The attendants of the Wei family were all angry at him and seated him on a shabby mat in the courtyard. After the meal, Wei Gao's family summoned the nursemaid to bring out the infant and asked the monks to offer blessings for his longevity. The foreign monk ascended the steps himself and said to the infant, “Have you been well since we last met?” The infant seemed to show a look of joy. All present were astonished. Wei Gao’s father said, “This child is only one month old. Why does your teacher speak of a long separation?” The foreign monk replied, “This is beyond your knowledge, O benefactor.” Wei Gao pressed him for an explanation, and the foreign monk said, “This child is the reincarnation of Zhuge Liang, the Marquis of Wu Hou. Zhuge Liang, during the later years of the Eastern Han dynasty, served as the chancellor of Shu, and the people of Shu have long benefited from his wisdom. Now he has descended into this world and will become the commander of the western frontier of Shu, receiving the blessings of the Shu people. I was at Jianmen Pass last year, and I was friends with this child. Now I have heard he has been born into the Wei family, so I have come all this way.” The Wei family found his words remarkable and thus named him after Zhuge Liang. Later, Wei Gao, from an early age, was in command of the Jiannan military under the title of Jinwu, and was subsequently promoted to Grand Marshal and Chief Secretary of the Central Secretariat. He governed Shu for eighteen years, and indeed fulfilled the words of the foreign monk.

Xīn Qī Shī, a man from Shaanxi whose surname is Xīn, was very cautious and solemn when he was a child, never engaging in playful activities. His parents found him unusual and pitied him. At the age of ten, he became fond of Buddhist teachings, read Buddhist scriptures daily, and could naturally distinguish Sanskrit sounds without teacher's instruction. Later, his father became the governor of Shaanxi. Previously, there were seven kilns in the south of the county. After his father's death, Xīn Qī suffered greatly from grief and went mad and fled one day. His family servants followed his path and found him sitting calmly in one of the kilns in the south of the county, with a strange light shining on him, bright like refined gold. They were astonished. Visiting another kiln, they found another Xīn Qī there. Visiting all seven kilns, they found one Xīn Qī in each. Thus he was called 'Xīn Qī Shī.'

A Master of Yangzhou During the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk who stayed in Yangzhou. His name is unknown, but he called himself "Master," and the people of Yangzhou thus referred to him as "Master." The Master was physically unattractive and had a fondness for alcohol and meat. He wore tattered clothes and did not change them even in the heat of summer, which led to lice and fleas gathering on his garments. He lived temporarily at Xinggan Temple, residing alone in a room. Every night, he would close the door and sleep, which became his routine. He had a wild and rebellious nature, enjoyed slaughtering dogs and pigs, and daily fought with young men from Yangzhou, sometimes lying drunk on the roadside. The people of Yangzhou all disliked him for these reasons. One young man was known for his strength. On a particular day, the young man was playing a game of chance with someone, and the Master became angry, breaking the gaming board with his hand.
