The young man called the Master a fool and challenged his courage. The Master then cursed and spat in the young man's face. The two fought, and a large crowd gathered to watch. The young man eventually lost and fled. From then on, the people of Yangzhou believed the Master had supernatural strength. The Master also took pride in his strength, frequently stealing money and clothes from the market. The people of the market were all afraid of his courage and dared not resist.

Later, an old monk summoned the Master. Upon arriving, the monk said, "Monks should wholeheartedly adhere to the precepts. How can you consume alcohol, kill pigs, and steal money and goods from market people? And how can you also fight with young men? Is this the way of a monk? If officials arrest you and report to the authorities, are you not ashamed?" The Master angrily retorted, "Flies and mosquitoes only crave stench and filth; how can they know the heart of dragons and cranes? Then my path is also not something you can understand. Moreover, I am clear within but mixed without; are you petty and lacking in great virtue?" The old monk could not ultimately convince him with his words. The next day, the Master returned from outside. After entering the room, he closed the door. Someone was peeping through the crack in the door. The Master sat on the mat, and a strange light emanated from his brow, illuminating the entire room brightly. The observer found it strange and fully informed the group of monks. The group of monks came, and saw the light from the Master's brow. They pointed at each other and said, "I have heard that the Buddha's brow has the white hair-like light. Now the Master has it; he is truly a Buddha." They then proceeded to worship him. By the next morning at dawn, the group of monks all gathered in the courtyard, waiting to pay homage to the Master of Guangling. By the time they opened the door, the Master of Guangling had already passed away. The monks were even more amazed by this event and thus began to call the Master "Master Buddha."

In the early years of the Yuanhe era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Feng Sheng from Changle who lived in Wu. He was selected for an official position through the Ministry of Personnel based on his knowledge of the classics, but in that year, he was dismissed by the officials. Consequently, he temporarily resided in Chang'an. One day, an old monk who recognized his name came to visit Feng Sheng and said to him, "You bear my surname." From then on, they met frequently for nearly a year. When Feng Sheng was appointed as an official in East Yue, he began to prepare for his journey, and the monk came with his belongings to say goodbye. Feng Sheng asked, "Where are you going, Master?" The monk replied, "I have been living in the west wing of Lingyan Temple for a long time. Afterward, I traveled around Chang'an, and it has been ten years since then. It is fortunate to have met you. Now I am returning to my hometown, so I come to bid farewell. However, since you are being appointed as an official in East Yue, and your route will pass by Lingyan Temple, you should visit me once." Feng Sheng agreed, saying, "I will follow your advice."

After several months, Feng, having taken up his post from Chang'an, arrived at the gate of Lingyan Temple. He stood his horse and looked, saying, "Is this not the temple where Master Jian resides?" He then entered and went to visit. At the time, there was a monk in the courtyard. Feng asked, "Do you not know where Master Jian's abode is? I am about to go there." The monk replied, "Among us, none of us have heard of someone named Jian." Feng began to doubt and feel strange, silently thinking and saying, "Master Jian is a trustworthy person; could he be deceiving me?" He then walked alone in the temple courtyard, reaching the western wing, when suddenly he saw a group of monks' portraits. One of them looked like Master Jian. Feng exclaimed in great surprise, "Master Jian is truly a strange person, and even a spirit descended upon me." He cried for a long time in a moved manner. Looking at the inscription, it read, "A son of the Feng family, from Wu County. At ten years old, he learned the Buddha's teachings, becoming known for his virtuous conduct. He died at the age of seventy-eight." Feng, reading the inscription, found it even more strange.

**Li Deyu, the Tang Dynasty Chancellor, was appointed as the Junior Advisor to the Crown Prince and stationed in the Eastern Capital (Luoyang). He once summoned a monk to inquire about his future fortune. The monk said, "It is not immediately knowable, I wish to set up an altar and place a Buddha statue." The monk stayed in the temple for three days. Then he told Li Deyu, "Your misfortunes have not ceased, you will be exiled to the south." Li Deyu became very angry and scolded the monk. The next day, he summoned the monk again and asked him. The monk said, "I wish to observe more carefully, please allow me to set up the altar for another three days." After three more days, the monk told Li Deyu, "The time of going south will not be far off, it cannot be avoided." Li Deyu was even less happy and said, "Then how can your master prove that he is not lying?" The monk said, "I wish to prove it with what is happening now, to show that I am not lying." Li Deyu said, "If you really have something to say, please go ahead." The monk pointed to the ground and said, "There is a stone box under here, please dig it up." Li Deyu ordered his people to dig down several feet, and indeed, they found a stone box. When they opened it, there was nothing inside. Li Deyu was surprised and began to believe the monk. He then asked, "Going south is indeed unavoidable, but will I never return?" The monk said, "You will return."**

The official inquired about the matter, and the monk replied, "The chancellor's lifetime allotment of sheep was ten thousand, and he has now consumed nine thousand five hundred. The reason for his return is that there are still five hundred sheep remaining." The official sighed sadly and said, "My teacher is indeed a sage. Moreover, during the thirteenth year of the Yuanhe era, I served as an assistant to Chancellor Zhang Gong in the Northern Capital. Once, I dreamed of walking on Jishan Mountain and saw that the entire mountain was filled with sheep. There were several dozen shepherds who welcomed and bowed to me. I then asked the shepherds, and they said, 'These are the sheep that the chancellor has consumed in his lifetime.' I once remembered this dream and never revealed it to anyone. Now, is it true as my teacher said? I now understand that hidden virtue is indeed not false." A few days later, the military governor of Zhenwu, Mi Ji, sent an envoy to deliver a letter to the official and also presented him with five hundred sheep. The official was greatly surprised and summoned the monk to inform him of the matter. The monk sighed and said, "With ten thousand sheep approaching, will the official not return?" The official said, "If I do not consume them, can I still avoid the consequences?" The monk replied, "The sheep are now the property of the chancellor." The official was deeply saddened. A few days later, he was demoted to the position of Military Governor of Chaozhou, and was subsequently demoted to the position of Household Registrar of Yazhou, eventually dying in a remote and desolate place.

### Translation #### 抱玉师 The Master Embracing Jade was renowned for his spiritual practice, residing in Chang'an. Thousands followed him as their teacher. Each evening, he would sit alone in a room, closing the door and extinguishing the candle. Once, a monk peered through a crack in the door and saw auspicious clouds emerging from his mouth. He lived to be ninety years old and passed away during a hot summer. His body did not decay. The Tang Chancellor Wang Qi, who was on good terms with the Master, came to attend his funeral. They intended to pour fragrant milk into his mouth as a ritual, but then a brilliant light emerged from his mouth, illuminating the surroundings. The Chancellor was greatly amazed. Some said, "The Buddha emits auspicious light, and now the Master Embracing Jade has it—truly a Buddha." #### 佛陀萨 There was a monk named Buddha Sa, whose name was listed in the records of the Temple of the Dharma Gate in Qishan. He claimed to be of the Buddha clan, with "Buddha" as his surname and "Sa" as his given name. He often walked alone through the regions of Qishan, wearing yellow robes and carrying a staff. Although he was old, his appearance was childlike and innocent. He enjoyed speaking loudly in the marketplace, sometimes insulting and humiliating the monks, who all grew angry at him. His food, clothing, and other necessities were all obtained by begging from the villagers. The villagers pitied his foolishness and generously provided him with food and clothing, so that his resources were more abundant than those of the other monks. Buddha Sa also shared his resources with the poor and hungry in the village. The villagers came to admire his heart even more. In the sixth month of the fifth year of the Kai Cheng era, Buddha Sa summoned the villagers and said, "I will die tonight. You must build a stupa to bury my body." He passed away as he had said. The villagers then built a stupa on the western hill of Qishan, lacquered his body, and buried it. After a month, someone noticed that his hair was only a few inches long, so the disciples shaved it off. Soon after, his hair began to grow again. The villagers were greatly astonished and locked the door of the stupa, never opening it again.

○ Zhao Fan Zhao Fan, the highest-ranking official of the Imperial Academy during the Tang Dynasty, served as an official of the Ministry of Personnel in the seventh year of the Dàhé era. One day, a monk came to the door to beg for food and told the household servant, "I wish to see Zhao Gong, may I?" The servant informed Zhao Fan, who, upon hearing this, invited the monk in and had him sit down. The monk then said, "You are about to have worries, but it can also be averted." Zhao Fan immediately bowed and prayed to him. The monk replied, "Give me 1,500 knives, and perhaps it can save you from disaster; otherwise, within ten days, you will become the governor of a southeastern prefecture." Zhao Fan agreed and promised to send the knives the next day, while also asking for the monk's name and where he lived. The monk said, "I live at Qinglong Temple, and my name is Fayan." After saying this, the monk left. The next day, Zhao Fan prepared to send the knives. The messenger went to the temple and searched for the monk among the monks, but none of them looked like him. They also inquired about the whereabouts of Master Fayan, searched the entire temple grounds but found no trace or sign. Several days later, Zhao Fan was sent to serve as the governor of Yuzhou.

In the northwest corner of Xingfu Temple, there is a Buddhist hall from the Sui Dynasty. On its walls, there is a painting of the Ten-Light Buddhas, whose brushwork is extremely skillful and stands as the pinnacle of its kind. An informed person said that this is the work of the national master painter, Cai Sheng. Cai Sheng was renowned in the Sui Dynasty for his exceptional painting skills. When the hall was first built and completed, a monk offered a hundred gold pieces to solicit skilled painters, and Cai Sheng was hired. After completing the painting, he told others, "I have painted many things in my lifetime. But today, as I set brush to paper, it feels as if I were guided by divine assistance." Because of this, his name spread throughout Chang'an. In the early years of the Zhenguan era, the temple monks, concerned about the hall's age and potential collapse, hired several craftsmen and spent money on construction, intending to renovate its structure. One day, the monks held a meal in the temple courtyard. After they sat down, ten monks arrived one after another, all pale and slender, with ancient-looking appearances, and arranged themselves at the table. After finishing their meal, they all rose together and entered the Buddhist hall, followed by the other monks. Soon, the ten monks vanished without a trace. The monks looked at each other and marveled for a long time. Upon examining the Ten-Light Buddhas painted on the north wall, they found that their demeanor matched that of the ten monks who had arrived earlier. From then on, the monks did not dare to destroy the hall, and they used it to honor the Ten-Light Buddhas.

Master Dao Yan, residing at the Shili Temple in Chengdu, experienced a remarkable event on the 21st day of the fifth month in the 14th year of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty (726 AD). While lighting the eternal lamp in front of the Buddha hall, he suddenly saw a giant hand in the west pavilion of the hall. Dao Yan was greatly frightened and bowed down without moving. After a while, he suddenly heard a voice from the air saying, "Do not be afraid, do not be afraid. I am a good spirit. I dare not harm even the slightest part of the teacher. Why bow down and not move?" Dao Yan, after hearing this, was slightly relieved of his fear. He then asked, "Master, who are you, hiding your body and showing only your hand?" Afterwards, he heard the voice from the air reply, "Heaven has appointed me to protect the land of the Buddha temple because people like to spit on the ground of the Buddha shrine. I immediately receive it with my back, taking the spittle. Therefore, my back has sores, and the sores are very deep. May I apply ointment on it, is it acceptable?" Dao Yan then placed clear oil in the giant hand, and the hand withdrew. Dao Yan then requested, "I now wish to see your form. To have a painter depict it on the wall and write the story to record it, hoping that people will not dare to spit on the ground of the Buddha shrine." The spirit said, "My appearance is very ugly. Teacher, when you see it, do not be startled." Dao Yan replied, "Master, just see my body, do not hinder me." Under the west pavilion, he saw a spirit with an appearance very strange, broad head and large nose, sharp eyes and wide mouth, a tall and robust body shape, several zhang long. Upon seeing it, his back sweat like a flood. The spirit then disappeared. Dao Yan then described the spirit's appearance to the painter and ordered him to depict it on the wall of the west pavilion.

# Translation ## Source Text ○鸡卵 唐敬宗皇帝御历，以天下无事，视政之余，因广浮屠教。由是，长安中缁徒益多。及文宗嗣位，亲阅万机，思除其害于人者，尝顾左右曰：“自吾为天子，未能有补于人。今天下幸无兵革，吾将尽除害物者，使亿兆之民，指今日为尧舜之世，足矣。有不能补化而蠹于物者，但言之。”左右或对曰：“独浮屠氏不能有补于大化，而蠹于物亦甚，可以斥去。”于是文宗病之，始命有司诏中外，罢缁徒说佛书义。又有请斥其不修教者。诏命将行，会尚食厨吏修御膳，以鼎烹鸡卵，方燃火于其下，忽闻鼎中有声极微，如人言者。迫而听之，乃群卵呼“观世音菩萨”也。声甚凄咽，似有所诉。尚食吏异之，具其事上闻。文宗命左右验之，如尚食所奏。文帝叹曰：“吾不知浮屠氏之力乃如是耶！”翌日，敕尚食吏无以鸡卵为膳。因颁诏郡国，各于精舍塑观世音菩萨像 ## Translation Eggs Emperor Jingzong of Tang ruled over the calendar, and with the world at peace, in his spare time after governing, he promoted the Buddhist teachings. As a result, the number of Buddhist monks in Chang'an increased. When Emperor Wenzong succeeded to the throne, he personally reviewed the myriad affairs of state and thought about removing those who were harmful to the people. He once looked around and said to those around him, "Since I became emperor, I have not been able to benefit the people. Today, fortunately, there is no warfare in the world. I will eliminate all those who harm the people, so that the vast population can consider today as the era of Yao and Shun, which would be enough. If there are those who cannot contribute to the transformation and instead harm the people, just speak up." Someone replied, "Only the Buddhists cannot contribute to the great transformation and also harm the people greatly, they can be expelled." Thus, Emperor Wenzong was troubled by this, and he first ordered the officials to issue an edict to the entire empire, banning Buddhist monks from expounding on Buddhist scriptures. There were also requests to expel those who did not follow Buddhist teachings. The edict was about to be carried out when the kitchen staff preparing the emperor's meals happened to be cooking chicken eggs in a cauldron, igniting the fire underneath. Suddenly, they heard a very faint sound, as if someone was speaking. They listened closely and found that it was the eggs calling out, "Guanyin Bodhisattva." The sound was very sorrowful, as if they were making a complaint. The kitchen staff found it strange and reported the incident to the emperor. Emperor Wenzong ordered his attendants to verify it, and it was as the kitchen staff had reported. Emperor Wenzong sighed and said, "I did not know the power of the Buddhists could be so great!" The next day, he ordered the kitchen staff not to use chicken eggs for meals. He then issued an edict to the provinces, ordering that statues of Guanyin Bodhisattva be made in each temple. ## Footnotes ## Notes ## References ## Citations ## Acknowledgments ## Disclaimers ## Copyright ## License ## Disclaimer of Warranty ## Limitation of Liability ## Indemn

○ Chicken Eggs During the reign of Emperor Jingzong of the Tang dynasty, with the world at peace, in his leisure from state affairs, he promoted the teachings of Buddhism. As a result, the number of Buddhist monks in Chang’an increased greatly. When Emperor Wen succeeded to the throne, he personally reviewed all state affairs and sought to eliminate those harmful to people. Once, he turned to his attendants and said, “Since I became emperor, I have yet to bring benefit to the people. Now that the world is fortunately free of war, I intend to completely remove all harmful things, so that the hundred million people may regard this day as the age of Yao and Shun. If there are those who cannot contribute to the greater good and instead harm the world, speak of them.” One of his attendants replied, “It is only the followers of Buddhism who fail to contribute to the great transformation of the world and cause great harm, and thus should be dismissed.” Emperor Wen was troubled by this, and so he ordered the officials to issue a decree throughout the empire, banning Buddhist monks from expounding the meaning of Buddhist scriptures. Some also requested that those who failed to observe proper teachings be expelled. The decree was about to be carried out when, coincidentally, the officials in charge of the imperial kitchen were preparing a meal. They placed a cauldron over a fire to cook chicken eggs, and as the fire was just being lit, they suddenly heard a very faint sound from inside the cauldron, like someone speaking. Pressing their ears close to listen, they heard the eggs crying out, “Guanyin Bodhisattva!” The sound was very sorrowful, as though they were making a plea. The kitchen officials were astonished and reported the matter to the emperor. Emperor Wen ordered his attendants to investigate, and they confirmed the report as true. Emperor Wen sighed and said, “I did not know the power of Buddhism was so great!” The next day, he ordered the kitchen officials not to use chicken eggs in the imperial meals. As a result, he issued a decree to the provinces, commanding that in each monastery, an image of the Bodhisattva Guanyin be sculpted.

Xu Wendu During the Tahe period of the Tang Dynasty, Xu Wendu from Gaoyang was temporarily residing in Qiyang Prefecture. After suffering from a fever for nearly a month, one day he lay down on his bed, as if drunk. Several days later, he awoke. At first, Wendu dreamed of several figures dressed in yellow robes, walking with him through the fields. Looking around, there was no sound of chickens or dogs, and he could not tell how far they had traveled. The sky was dim and overcast, and his worries were as if tied up. One of the yellow-robed figures said to Wendu, "Do not be distressed. Life and death are finite. Even sages cannot escape their fate." Wendu suddenly realized that he had died, and he was very afraid. They then walked for more than ten li, reaching a body of water that seemed boundless. The waves were dark, and the depth was unknown. The yellow-robed figures all walked on the water and left, but Wendu was too afraid to cross. Soon after, two golden figures appeared, each about five inches tall, with a brilliant light shining around them, coming from the water. The yellow-robed figures saw the golden figures and turned pale with fear, retreating quickly and not daring to look back.

The two golden figures said to Wendu, "Why have you come to the underworld? I will now take you back to the path of life, be careful not to be afraid." Wendu's fear gradually subsided, and he bowed twice and thanked them. The golden figures then traveled with Wendu for dozens of miles, and soon they saw the gate of the village, overjoyed. Suddenly, they heard a harsh voice calling Wendu, and he woke up in a panic. He saw his wife and children crying in front of him, finding it both strange and admirable, yet exhausted and unable to move his limbs, so he did not have time to talk about the matter. After ten days, his illness slightly improved, and he leaned on a cane and walked in the courtyard. Suddenly, he saw the two golden figures again, both over five inches tall, at the Buddhist temple, the same ones he had seen in his dream before, and their appearance was not different in the slightest. Wendu found it even more extraordinary, and began telling his wife about the matter. His wife said, "Yesterday, because your illness was severe, I was worried and unable to resolve my anxiety. However, I often heard that Buddhism has the power to relieve suffering, so I gave up wealth and possessions, cast images of the two golden figures, and every morning, prepared food and offered it as a sacrifice. Since then, your suffering also disappeared, it was their power." Wendu was grateful for the quick reward of the two golden figures, abstained from eating meat, often read Buddhist scriptures, and thus thoroughly explored their ultimate meaning.

Mr. Shang, the Recluse There was a recluse named Mr. Shang from Sanhe County. At the age of seven, he was able to understand Buddhist scriptures, which amazed the villagers. Later, he lived in a hut in the fields west of Sanhe County, where he had hundreds of Buddhist texts. He would read and review these books every day without missing a single day. Hundreds of people followed him as their teacher. He often traveled alone to the city, and those who accompanied him heard that whenever Mr. Shang moved his body, it sounded like the sound of jade being struck. Listeners found this remarkable. Some people said, "Mr. Shang's bones are truly lock bones. Lock bones are like vines, spreading throughout the body, so when they move, the body produces a clear and resonant sound. It is indeed so. I have heard that according to Buddhist scriptures, the Buddha has relics in his body, and Bodhisattvas have lock bones. Now Mr. Shang, is he not a Bodhisattva? However, it is difficult for ordinary people to discern this." Mr. Shang lived to be over ninety years old. One day, he bathed and dressed formally, then summoned all his disciples to a meal. He told them, "I am ninety years old, and I am about to die soon. You should cremate my body. Be careful not to go against my wishes." The disciples wept and said, "We will obey your command." That night, he died sitting up. Three days later, the disciples cremated Mr. Shang in the wilderness. When they examined his bones, they found them to be lock bones. The bones were connected in a continuous manner, as if sewn together. When the wind blew, a delicate and resonant sound was produced. Thus, the villagers contributed money to build a pagoda to bury Mr. Shang's lock bones in the pagoda.

Ning Mian, a young man from Yunzhong, was brave, skilled in riding and archery, and able to fight wild beasts with strength, without using weapons. The governor of the northern capital admired his bravery and appointed him as a military officer. Later, with four thousand troops, he was stationed at Feixu City. At that time, the commander of Jimo Gate was arrogant and fierce, disregarding the emperor's laws, and rebellion was reported to the imperial court. Emperor Wenzong of Tang ordered the governor of the northern capital to attack the southern part. Before the edict arrived, the Jimo troops attacked Feixu City at night. The sound of drums shook the ground, and the people of Feixu were restless and uneasy. They told Ning Mian, "The Jimo troops are fierce and cannot be defeated. They are about to arrive, and the situation is very urgent. We wish to evacuate the city and flee. Otherwise, they will capture our city soon. I cannot bear to see my family and brothers die by the enemy's sword. It will be too late to regret! Even if the emperor is divine and powerful, how can he clear our injustice? Please think carefully."

Mian, assessing his own situation, believed that with his small number of troops, he could not break through the sharp edge of the Jì commander's forces. He considered listening to the words of the people of the town, fearing he might offend the emperor; yet, he also feared that if he were to defend firmly and remain on the defensive, the entire town might be completely slaughtered by the hands of the bandits. The worry was very great, but his strategy remained undecided. Suddenly, there was a spy who reported, "The bandits have completely broken and fled. There are discarded armor pieces at the base of the city walls, willing to be taken." Mian immediately climbed the city walls to look, and at that time, the moon was bright and clear. He saw the bandit soldiers fleeing in chaos, with countless fallen and stumbling, as if a large army was attacking from behind. Mian was greatly pleased and opened the city gates, letting his soldiers pursue them. They captured dozens alive and acquired many discarded armor pieces. Previously, Mian had been fond of the Buddha's teachings and often read the Buddhist scripture *Diamond Sutra*. After defeating the Jì commander's forces, he captured the prisoners and questioned them, saying, "Late in the day, I saw several giants on the city wall, over two zhang long, majestic and fearsome, with angry eyes and open mouths, naked and holding swords. When the Jì people saw them, they were terrified and sweating coldly, immediately fleeing, and how could they have any fighting spirit?" Mian realized that the giants were actually the Vajra (Diamond) warriors, becoming even more amazed by it. Mian held the position of Inspectorate Vice-Chancellor and later became the Deputy Envoy for Clearing the Frontier.

During the reign of Emperor Taizong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk from the Wuzhen Temple in Wanshun Mountain who, one night, went to Lan Creek. Suddenly, he heard someone reciting the Lotus Sutra, the sound being delicate and distant. The stars and moon were overhead, and looking in all directions for dozens of miles, everything was silent and empty. The monk felt fear and distress. When he returned to the temple, he reported the matter to the other monks. The next night, they all went to Lan Creek to listen, and then heard the recitation coming from underground. They marked the spot with a marker. The following day, they dug down to the marker and found a skull buried in accumulated earth. The bone was dry and shriveled, but only the lips and tongue were fresh and moist. They carried it back to the temple and placed it in a stone box under the west pavilion of the Thousand Buddha Hall. From then on, every night, there was a recitation of the Lotus Sutra coming from the stone box. Thousands of people from Chang'an came to watch. Later, a monk from Silla (Korea) stayed as a guest at the temple for almost a year. One day, when the monks had all gone down the mountain, the Silla monk was alone in the temple and stole the stone box and left. The monks followed him, but he had already returned to the East Sea (Korea). This was during the late Tang Dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (reigned 712-756).

Master Yèguāng of the Tang Dynasty was a native of Jìmen. From a young age, he was intelligent, studious, and had a deep inclination towards Buddhism, eventually becoming a monk and residing in his hometown. Within just ten years, he had thoroughly mastered the profound teachings of the Buddhist scriptures. There was another monk named Huìdá, who came from a very wealthy family and possessed millions of coins. He admired Master Yèguāng's learning and thus became his friend. At that time, Emperor Xuanzong favored both Taoist immortals and Buddhism, seeking out famous monks and Taoist practitioners. However, Master Yèguāng, constrained by poverty, could not travel west, and he often felt discontented. Huìdá knew this and provided him with 700,000 coins to support his journey. He also said to Master Yèguāng, "Your learning, skills, and talents, I humbly believe there is none superior to you. The Holy Emperor selects the finest talents from the world, and you will certainly lead the pack, bathing in the emperor's favor. From now on, you will be part of the monastic community, serving as a minister to the future emperor, and can wait with folded feet. However, at that time, there will certainly be someone sweeping at your door, so please do not forget our brief acquaintance." Master Yèguāng replied, "Thank you for your generous gift, allowing me to go west. If I become a fifth-rank official, then I will repay your kindness."

Yeguang arrived in Chang'an, where he bribed those close to Princess Jiu Xian, thereby gaining access to the hot springs. He was then ordered by the inner court officials to select ten learned monks, who engaged in discussions with Daoist scholars, and Yeguang was among those chosen. He elaborated on profound mysteries and clarified ambiguous points, and no other monks dared to compare with him. The emperor was amazed by his eloquence and issued an edict granting him a silver seal and red sash, appointing him as a Four Gates Doctor. He served at the emperor's side daily and was bestowed a mansion and thousands of gold coins and silk. At that time, he was called a favored minister. Huidada then traveled from Jimo to Chang'an to visit him. When Yeguang heard that Huidada had arrived, he thought that Huidada had come to collect debts from him, and he was very unhappy. Huidada understood his intention and took his leave. After returning north for more than a month, Yeguang feared that Huidada would return again, so he secretly wrote a letter to Zhang Tinggui, the commander of Jimo: "Recently, Huidada came to the imperial court and accused you of repairing weapons and armor, planning to rebel. Many people also knew about it. Given your loyalty, no one in the world does not know about it. Repeated slander can erode even gold, so one must be cautious." Zhang Tinggui was shocked and angry and immediately summoned Huidada to beat him to death. A few days later, Yeguang suddenly saw Huidada appear in the courtyard and cursed him, saying, "I lent you seventy thousand coins to travel west, how could you suddenly accuse me and cause me to die unjustly. How deeply have you wronged me!" After finishing his words, he leaped up. Zhang Tinggui pulled Yeguang, and after a long time, the vision disappeared. All of Huidada's disciples saw it. A few days later, Yeguang died.

In the reign of Emperor Dezong during the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Li, who came from the Huai River region. When he was young, he had great physical strength, was proud and fond of chivalry, and did not adhere to minor details, often associating with frivolous young men. By the time he was in his twenties, he began to change his ways and start reading, writing poetry, and was highly praised by people. He served multiple times as an official in the Huai River region and later became a recorder and military officer in Shen Prefecture. Li had a beautiful appearance, was good at conversation and laughter, had a thorough understanding of administrative affairs, and was honest, cautious, and capable. He was skilled in playing polo and drinking, and was highly regarded by the prefect.

During the time when Wang Wujun was commanding the Chengde Army, he relied on his achievements and held the masses in contempt, disregarding the laws and regulations. The governors of the subordinate prefectures looked at him with fear and suspicion. Once, he sent his son, Prince Wujun, to tour the subordinate prefectures, and they arrived at Shen Prefecture. The governor prepared a lavish banquet with beef and wine, and arranged music and entertainment for Prince Wujun. The governor feared Wang Wujun and treated Prince Wujun with great respect. He also worried that someone might offend Prince Wujun with their drinking, so he dared not invite any other officials or guests. Prince Wujun was greatly pleased, thinking that no other prefectures could match this hospitality. They drank until night, and then Prince Wujun said, "I am fortunate to be treated so well by you. I wish to enjoy the festivities tonight. Are there no distinguished guests? I wish to invite them." The governor replied, "This remote prefecture has no famous people. I fear the power of the Deputy Commander-in-Chief and dare not invite other guests to the banquet. Only the Chief Clerk, Mr. Li, is suitable to accompany our conversation." Prince Wujun said, "Just have him come." The governor then summoned Mr. Li, who entered and bowed. Prince Wujun looked at him with a very angry expression. After a while, he ordered him to sit, and Mr. Li became even more respectful, but Prince Wujun was even less pleased, glaring and clenching his fists, losing all his earlier joy. The governor was afraid and did not know what was happening. He looked at Mr. Li, who was embarrassed and sweating, unable to hold his cup, and everyone at the table was stunned. After a while, Prince Wujun shouted at his attendants to bind Mr. Li and lock him in prison. The attendants quickly took Mr. Li's sleeves and dragged him away to be locked up. After that, Prince Wujun continued drinking and feasting as before.

Until dawn, after the feast, the prefect was both surprised and fearful, and secretly sent someone to interrogate Li in the prison, saying, "Your appearance is very respectful, and you never spoke, certainly not in opposition to Lord Wang, do you know yourself?" Li wept bitterly for a long time, then said, "I often heard that the Buddhist doctrine of immediate retribution exists, and now I understand. In my youth, I was poor and unable to support myself, so I associated with knights and often seized the wealth of the villagers. I would ride a horse with a bow at my waist, travel back and forth on the Taihang road, covering over a hundred li each day. One day, I encountered a young man who was whipping a fine mule, carrying two bags. I coveted his wealth, looked around, and saw cliffs a thousand fathoms high, while the day grew dim. I then forcefully pushed him, and he fell into the ravine. I immediately drove his mule to the inn keeper, opened his bags, and obtained over a hundred pieces of silk and brocade. From then on, my family became somewhat wealthy, so I broke the bow and cut the string, closed the door, and studied. I entered officialdom and reached this position, and until now, it has been twenty-seven years. Last night, Lord Jun commanded an invitation to a banquet with Lord Wang. After entering, I saw the appearance of Lord Wang, and it was the young man I killed in the past. After the first bow, I felt ashamed and fearful, and I knew I would die soon. Now I extend my neck to await the blade, what more can I say? On my behalf, thank Lord Jun, fortunately, you understand me deeply, dare to entrust my affairs after death."

After a short while, Shi Zhen, having become enlightened while drunk, hastily summoned his attendants: "Go fetch Li's head." The attendants immediately beheaded him in the prison and presented his head. Shi Zhen looked at it carefully and laughed, and afterwards, he drank heavily with the prefect in the prefectural hall. The prefect, being drunk, became cheerful. The prefect then said: "I am unworthy, but fortunately I have been appointed to govern this prefecture, and the deputy ambassador came down to inspect my corrupt governance, but was lenient and did not punish me. Yesterday, the deputy ambassador ordered me to invite other guests, but the prefecture is remote and small, so there were no guests. I privately thought that Li was good at drinking, so I invited him. But Li was foolish and ignorant, not accustomed to etiquette, and greatly offended the honorable official. It was my fault. Now that the honorable official has already executed him, it is appropriate. I have something that I do not understand, and dare to ask above. What was Li's crime, and may I ask for a clear account of it, so that I may learn from it in the future?" Shi Zhen laughed and said: "Li had no crime, but I saw him and immediately felt a strong aversion that stirred my heart, and I had the intention to kill him. Now that he is dead, I do not know why I did it. You should not speak of it again."

After the banquet ended, the prefect secretly inquired about his age, and he was twenty-seven years old. It was the year when Li killed the young man, but Shi Zhen was born in the Wang family. The prefect was amazed for a long time, and he then used his family wealth to give Li a grand burial. ○ Zheng Sheng Zheng Sheng from Xingyang, Tang dynasty, was skilled in riding and shooting. Known for his bravery, ferocity, and agility, he lived in the suburbs of Gong and Luo. Once, one day, he was drunk, holding a bow and carrying arrows, riding a swift horse, and alone driving through the fields, far from a certain residence, about tens of miles away. Just as it was getting dark, a heavy rainstorm came. Zheng took shelter under a large tree for a long time. When the rain stopped, it was already evening, and he lost his way. Letting the horse run freely, he saw a gate and a building by the road, which was a temple of the gods. Zheng tied his horse outside the door and was about to enter the house. Suddenly, he felt a sudden fear in his heart, and he immediately hid himself under the eastern eaves. He heard a rustling sound in the empty room on the left side of the temple and suspected it was a ghost. He then drew his bow and tightened the string to wait for it. Soon, he saw a man, tall, wearing a short, sleeveless robe, carrying a bag and holding a sword, coming out of the empty room. Afterward, leaning on his sword, he loudly said, 'I am a thief.'

After the banquet ended, the prefect secretly inquired about his age, and it turned out he was twenty-seven years old. It was the year when Li Sheng killed the youth, and Shi Zhen was born into the Wang family. The prefect marveled at this for a long time, and so he used his family wealth to give Li Sheng a generous burial. ○ Zheng Sheng A man from Yingyang during the Tang Dynasty, Zheng Sheng was skilled in riding and archery, renowned for his bravery, fierceness, and agility. He lived in the suburbs of Gongluo. Once, on a day when he was drunk, he held a bow in his hand and an arrow at his waist, rode a swift horse, and rode alone through the fields, nearly several tens of miles away from someone's residence. Just as the sky turned dark, a heavy rain and wind came. Zheng Sheng took shelter under a large tree for a long time. When the rain stopped, it was already evening. He had lost his way, so he let his horse run freely, and saw a gate and building by the roadside, which was a shrine. Zheng Sheng tied his horse outside the gate and was about to enter the building, when suddenly he felt a sharp sense of alarm and immediately hid himself behind the eastern wing. He heard a rustling sound from the empty house on the left side of the shrine, and suspected it was a ghost. He therefore drew his bow and tightened the string, waiting for it. Soon, a tall man appeared, wearing a short-sleeved robe, carrying a bag and a sword, emerging from the empty house. Afterward, he leaned on his sword and loudly declared, "I am a thief."

"You are not a thief?" Zheng Sheng replied, "I live in the outskirts of Gong and Luo. Earlier, I was driving alone in the fields and encountered a sudden storm, which caused me to lose my way, so I hid here." The man with the sword said, "Since you are not a thief, could you have ill intentions against me? As I flee, I must pass under the eastern wing. Please untie the bowstring and give it to me, so I can leave. Otherwise, I will be killed by this young man." Previously, Zheng Sheng had hidden another string in his sleeve. After untying the string and throwing it before the sword-wielder, he secretly tied the string from his sleeve to the bow. The thief, having obtained the string, went to the eastern wing and intended to kill Zheng Sheng to silence him. Zheng Sheng quickly tied an arrow to the string, and the thief fled. The thief then said, "You truly are a wise one, I truly deserve to die." Zheng Sheng replied, "I do not harm you, why do you doubt me?" The thief knelt twice and thanked him, and Zheng Sheng then went to the western wing to hide. After the thief left, Zheng Sheng feared that he might return with his followers, so he climbed a tree to hide. After a long time, the stars and moon began to appear, and he suddenly saw a woman with a beautiful appearance emerge from an empty house and weep in the courtyard. He asked her, and she said, "I lived in a village, but was lured here by bandits. They also coveted my clothes and belongings, so they killed me in the empty house and left my body behind. I hope you can clear my name." She also said, "Tonight, I should hide in the tomb of Tian Heng. Please hurry to catch them and do not let them escape." Zheng Sheng agreed, and the woman thanked him and left. By morning, Zheng Sheng saw the body. He then rode his horse to Luoyang and reported the incident in detail to Governor Zheng Shuce of Henan. The governor ordered officials to capture them, and they were indeed found in the tomb of Tian Heng.

○Fan Zongliang During the Tang dynasty, Fan Zongliang served as the governor of Mizhou. At that time, there were a group of bandits in the subordinate counties. They led troops into the home of the commoner Yin family, plundered gold and silk, and killed their father and sons, three people died. The governor captured them very urgently, but after more than a month, they were not caught. There was a man named Wei Nan Hua from Juxu, who lived temporarily between Qi and Lu. His family was very poor, and Zongliang ordered him to take on the role of judicial official. One night, Nan Hua dreamed of several people, all with their hair loose, who lined up and told him, saying, "We are the Yin family, father and sons three, all died without any guilt. We hope you will clear our innocence." Nan Hua asked, "Who killed you?" They replied, "In the east of where we live, there is a man surnamed Yao, who is the leader of the bandits." Nan Hua agreed and woke up in a hurry. A few days later, Zongliang said to Nan Hua, "The bandits killed my commoners, and it has been more than a month, but we cannot find their tracks. Is it not because the officials are not fulfilling their duties? You are the judicial official, just go and investigate." Nan Hua rushed to the place. Before he arrived, he suddenly saw a fox rise from the deep grass by the roadside and rush into the residence of the local man surnamed Yao. Those who were shouting and chasing numbered in the hundreds. The fox entered a hole. Nan Hua ordered to dig it up with a spade and found a lot of gold and silk. It was the bandits who robbed the wealth of the Yin family. He immediately summoned the son of the Yao family, questioned him about his origins. The man's eyes moved, and he spoke hesitantly. Nan Hua immediately arrested and impeached him. He was indeed the leader of the bandits. From then on, all his accomplices were captured, about ten in total. The fox, although hidden in the hole, was finally found. Is it not the soul of the wronged person that has taken possession of the fox? It was during the Dahan period.

Wang Dongwei, a Daoist priest of the Jingyun Temple in Fen Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, lived in Xiaoyi County. Initially serving as a minor clerk, he had a nature that enjoyed killing and often engaged in fishing, hunting, and trapping. From the age of adulthood to middle age, he killed tens of thousands of wolves, foxes, pheasants, rabbits, fish, turtles, and birds. Later, he became a village magistrate and suffered from a fever for more than a month. Suddenly, he felt that there were tens of thousands of birds, beasts, fish, and turtles inside the room, surrounding his bed and gnawing at him. Scars and wounds covered his body, almost no part of his skin was intact. After midnight, his parents and brothers all heard a group of birds chirping inside his room, clearly distinguishable. For several years, his illness became increasingly severe. Someone told Wang's father, "Your son is gravely ill; it would be best to move him to the Jingyun Temple." So, they moved on a divinely chosen day. After a month, a gathering of Daoist priests performed a ritual and granted talismans, and that night Wang's fever subsided. Ten years later, he ultimately died from the illness.

### Translation **Chastising the Golden Image** In the early years of the Tang Dynasty, there was a divine image made of gold. According to tradition, during the period between the Zhou and Sui dynasties, a Taoist magician melted and cast it into shape. During Wu Zetian's reign, it was placed in the palace and kept very strictly locked away. Emperor Xuanzong once visited the palace and inspected the image. At the time, Emperor Suzong was in the imperial palace, and Emperor Daizong was still young; both attended the emperor. The emperor asked the eunuch Li Shi, "What is special about this divine image? Is there a story behind it?" Li Shi replied, "This image was made in ancient times and can predict how many years a ruler will reign. The method is to shout loudly at it. If the reign is long, the image will shake and tremble for a long time; otherwise, it will only shake once and stop." The emperor then shouted loudly at the image, and it seemed to be afraid, shaking and trembling for a long time before falling to the ground. The emperor laughed and said, "Just as was said, how long will I reign as emperor?" Li Shi then bowed twice in celebration. The emperor then ordered the crown prince to shout at the image, which shook slightly. He then ordered the emperor's grandson to shout at it, and it also shook for a long time. The emperor said, "My grandson resembles me." Later, Emperor Xuanzong reigned for fifty years, Emperor Suzong for six years, and Emperor Daizong for nineteen years, all matching the prediction.

○迎光王 The Grand Chamberlain Lu Zhen had a nephew who once served as a Buddhist monk. During the Kai Cheng period, he was forced to return to secular life due to the government's anti-Buddhist policies and was unofficially appointed as a military officer in the Prince Guang's household. One day, he dreamed that his former teacher came to his home and greeted him. Lu Zhen told him that his lowly position was not what he desired and that he often wished to shave his head again and return to monastic life. The teacher said, "You truly have this wish, and the revival of Buddhism is not too late." Before he finished speaking, suddenly, from all sides, he saw banners with the sun and moon, thousands of chariots and horses. They loudly proclaimed that Prince Guang was ascending to the throne as emperor. Soon after, Emperor Wu died, and Prince Guang indeed became the emperor. Thus, the prophecy was fulfilled. ○彭偃 During the Dali period of the Tang Dynasty, Peng Yan was not yet in office when someone told him, "You will gain wealth and status by finding a pearl, but you will also suffer misfortune later." Soon after, he was demoted and exiled to the position of military governor of Li Prefecture. Upon arriving, he was delighted because there were many clams in the river and believed that pearls could be obtained. He immediately ordered people to collect them. They gathered many clams, but ultimately, no pearls were found. When Zhu Ci rebelled, he summoned Peng Yan to serve as a false Secretary of the Central Secretariat. At that moment, Peng Yan realized that the "pearl" was actually Zhu Ci's rebellion.

### Translation --- ### ○李师道（一） 唐李师道既以青齐叛，章武帝将讨之，凡数年而王师不胜。师道益骄。尝一日坐于堂，其榻前有银鼎，忽相鼓，其一鼎耳足尽坠。后月余，刘悟手刃师道，青齐遂平。盖银鼎相鼓之兆也。 During the Tang Dynasty, Li Shidao rebelled in Qing and Qi regions. Emperor Xianzong of Tang planned to suppress him, but the imperial army was not victorious for several years. Li Shidao grew increasingly arrogant. One day, while sitting in the hall, he noticed two silver tripods before his seat. Suddenly, they began to strike each other, and one of them fell completely apart. More than a month later, Liu Wu killed Li Shidao with his own hands, and Qing and Qi were finally pacified. It was said that the incident with the silver tripods was a sign of the impending defeat. --- ### ○王涯 唐丞相王涯，大和九年掌邦赋，又主簿盐铁。其子仲翔尝一日避暑于山亭，忽见家僮数十皆无首，被血来仲翔前。仅食顷，方不见。仲翔惊异且甚，即具白之，愿解去权位。涯不听。是岁冬十一月，果有郑注之祸。 In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Wang Ya, a high-ranking official, oversaw the national finances and the salt and iron industries. One day, his son Zhongxiang sought refuge in a mountain pavilion to escape the heat. Suddenly, he saw dozens of servants without heads, covered in blood, approaching him. After just a meal's time, they disappeared. Zhongxiang was greatly alarmed and immediately reported what he had seen, wishing to resign from his position. However, Wang Ya did not listen. In the eleventh month of that year, Wang Ya indeed suffered the calamity brought by Zheng Zhu. --- ### ○温造 新昌里尚书温造宅，桑道茂尝居之。庭有二柏树甚高。桑生曰：“夫人之所居，木蕃茂者，皆宜去之。且木盛则土衰，由是居人有病者，乃土衰之致也。”于是以铁数十钧镇于柏树下。既而告人曰：“后有居，发吾所镇之地者，其家长当死。”唐大和九年，温造居其宅，因修建堂宇，遂发地得桑生所镇之铁。后数日造果卒。 In Xinchang Lane, there was a residence belonging to Minister Wen Zao, where Sang Daomao once lived. The courtyard had two very tall cypress trees. Sang Daomao said, "Where people live, if the trees are abundant and flourishing, they should all be removed. Moreover, when the trees are abundant, the earth becomes weak, which is why the residents may fall ill." Therefore, he placed several dozen iron hooks under the cypress trees. Later, he told others, "If anyone disturbs the place I have secured, the head of the household will die." In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Wen Zao lived in this residence and began to renovate the hall and buildings. He dug up the ground and found the iron hooks that Sang Daomao had placed. Several days later, Wen Zao indeed died. ---

### Translation #### 李宗闵 The Tang chancellor Li Zongmin was dispatched to Hanzhong in the summer of the seventh year of the Dazhong era. The following winter, he re-entered the position of chancellor. In the middle of the next summer, after returning home from court duties in Jing'an Lane, he noticed that an iron used for pressing clothes on his bed suddenly jumped and moved for a long time. Li Zongmin found this strange and unpleasant. At that time, Li Xun and Zheng Zhu had gained favor through their cunning and deceit. They frequently spoke to the emperor about these matters. Li Xun learned of this and subsequently submitted a report to the emperor, leading to Li Zongmin's punishment. Ten days later, an imperial decree demoted him to the position of governor of Mingzhou, and he was further demoted to the position of household administrator in Chaozhou. This was indeed a sign of his misfortune. #### 柳公济 Shangshu Liu Gongji, during the Dazhong era, was ordered by the emperor to campaign against Li Tongjian. After setting out, shortly thereafter, his standard spear suddenly broke. A guest who saw this exclaimed, "When a great general sets out on a campaign, if his standard flag or spear breaks, the army will surely suffer defeat. Otherwise, the general himself will die." A few months later, Liu Gongji indeed passed away. Generally, if crows or kites follow a military force, it is a sign of impending disaster. There was a man named Zeng Jingyun, who once served as a deputy general in the northern capital. During the rebellion led by Li Shidao, Zeng commanded several thousand troops in the field army. Whenever he set out on a campaign, crows or kites would follow him, indicating that the army would surely suffer defeat, and this became a common occurrence. Later, he left his family to become a monk and settled in the Ninding Temple in Taiyuan. In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Luo Liyan, who served as the governor of Jingzhao, once entered the court wearing his official robes. Looking into a mirror, he could not see his head. He then told his younger brother about this incident. Eventually, he was implicated in the same case as Li Xun and was executed.

Former Minister of Punishment from Pei State, Liu Zungu, during the fourth year of the Great and Harmonious era (830 AD), served as the military governor of the eastern part of Shu. Previously, there was a wealthy book collector in Shu who had amassed a vast collection of books. When Liu arrived, he borrowed several hundred volumes, though he did not read them all in detail. The following summer, the Fu River experienced a major flood that broke through the city's defenses and destroyed houses within the city. After several days, the water level began to recede, but Liu's books, artifacts, and treasures were all soaked and damaged by the flood. Liu then ordered the books to be arranged in the courtyard to dry them out. A few days later, Liu found a scroll titled *The Commentary on the I Ching* among the books. The handwriting was exquisite, and the script was slightly ancient, indicating that it was not a recent work. At the end of the scroll, it was inscribed: "On the eleventh day of the third month of the second year of the Shangyuan era (761 AD), while reading the *I Ching*, I wrote this commentary. From this point, there have been twelve or thirteen era names, leading to the Great and Harmonious era, when a great flood would occur, and the books would be spread out to dry. Hengyang Taoist priest Li Dechu." Liu read the inscription and was amazed for a long time. He investigated the origin of the book and discovered that it was one of the books collected by the people of Shu. He then summoned officials to see it. The phrase "twelve or thirteen era names, leading to the Great and Harmonious era" refers to the fact that "one person with eight thousand mouths" is the era name "Great and Harmonious." From the Shangyuan era, through Baoying, Guangde, Yongtai, Dali, Jianzhong, Xingyuan, Zhenyuan, Yongzhen, Yuanhe, Changqing, and Baoli, to the Great and Harmonious era, there have been thirteen era names in total. The record matches the actual situation. It is uncertain whether Li Dechu was a real person or if the name was fabricated for future reference.

**Translation:** **Divine Painting** Yunhua Temple has a Divine Painting Hall, known in Chang'an as the Seven Divine Paintings. Initially, the hall had already been constructed, and the temple monks summoned painters to apply colorful decorations. However, the price was considered too high, and it did not meet the expectations of the monks, so they eventually left. After several days, two young men came to the temple and said, "We are skilled painters. We have heard that this temple is about to hire painters. We do not seek profit from our work; we wish to offer our labor, may we do so?" The monks wanted to see their previous work first, but the young men replied, "My brothers and I, seven in total, have never painted in Chang'an. How could we have any previous work?" The monks thought they were lying and tried to discourage them. The young men said, "We do not accept payment from the monks. If our work does not meet your expectations, then we will simply whitewash the walls, and it will not be too late." The monks, seeing that they would not have to pay, agreed to their offer. The next day, the seven men arrived as promised. Each carried their painting tools and entered the hall, telling the monks, "For the next seven days, carefully do not open our door, nor provide us with food or drink, as we must avoid the influence of wind and sun. You may seal our door with mud, leaving no gaps, or else we cannot achieve our work's perfection." The monks followed their instructions. For six days, there was no sound. The monks whispered to each other, "This must be some kind of supernatural occurrence. We cannot keep our promise." They then broke the seal together. When the door was opened, seven pigeons flew gracefully into the sky. Inside the hall, the paintings were arranged as if in the four corners, except that the northwest wall was not fully decorated. Later, the painters who had seen the work were greatly surprised and said, "Truly the work of a divine and wondrous brush!" After that, no one dared to continue the coloring.

**Li Sheng (Part Two)** During the Tianbao era, there was a man from Longxi named Li Sheng who was selected for the position of military officer in Gui Prefecture despite not holding an official rank. After taking up his post, he fell ill with a fever for over ten days and felt severe pain in his left breast. Upon examination, it appeared to be a boil or abscess. He summoned a doctor to check his pulse, and the doctor said, "There is nothing wrong with your internal organs. If there is something inside your chest, probing the breast with a tool will cause pain, and the boil will not heal." After another ten days, his condition worsened. One day, the boil burst, and a pheasant suddenly flew out from his left breast, its destination unknown. That night, Li Sheng died. **Liu Shide** When Liu Shide was still an ordinary man, he often suffered from a long-term illness. One night, he dreamed of a man dressed in purple who bowed twice in front of his bed and said, "Your illness will soon subside. Please accompany me." The man then led him out. Suddenly, he felt a great surge of strength and believed his illness was cured. After walking several miles, he saw a government office with red doors and officials on either side. The man said, "This is the Underworld Court." Liu Shide was surprised and asked, "How can the Underworld Court be in the human world?" The man in purple replied, "The path of the underworld intersects with the human world. How could ordinary people know about it?" Liu Shide entered the court, and the officials retreated in awe. He saw an empty room and asked, "What is this place?" The man replied, "This is the Office of the Life and Death Registrar." Liu Shide asked, "What is your duty?" The man said, "I record the fortunes and destinies of people." Liu Shide secretly looked at the records and saw thousands of documents on the desk. Beside him was a man in green clothing who called himself a clerk. Liu Shide asked the clerk to bring his own record. The clerk took a scroll and handed it to Liu Shide, who read it and found his name listed. It recorded his official rank, position, and lifespan, showing a clear and honorable career path, serving in high-ranking government positions, and living to be eighty-five years old.

Jian was pleased and said to the assistant magistrate, "I am just a commoner, content with not being hungry or freezing. How could I dare to have other ambitions?" Before he finished speaking, suddenly a sound descended from the sky, shaking the walls and eaves. The assistant magistrate was startled and said, "The heavenly drum is about to sound; you should quickly return home, you cannot stay here anymore." Hearing the sound, Jian suddenly woke up from his dream, realizing it was all just a dream journey. At that time, the sky had already dawned, and there was a Buddhist temple to the east of his residence, striking the dawn bell. This was what the assistant magistrate had referred to as the "heavenly drum." That day, his illness also improved. Later, he entered government service, and the officials he held were all as recorded. When he became the commander of Xiliang, one day, he saw a messenger in yellow robes come to the front of the hall and say, "A minor official from the underworld, has been ordered to invite you." Jian replied, "I have seen the register of the God of Destiny, which records my position, which should reach the highest rank, and my lifespan is eighty-five years. Why am I summoned so soon?" The messenger in yellow robes said, "When you held a certain office, you once mistakenly killed an innocent person. Your rank and lifespan were lowered by the chief official, and now you are in dire straits." After finishing his words, he suddenly disappeared. From then on, Jian fell ill and died after three days.

### Translation #### Yang Yan Former Prime Minister Yang Yan, before he entered officialdom, once dreamed of ascending to the peak of a high mountain. From there, he looked down upon the human world, which was vaguely indistinguishable; looking up, he saw the auspicious sun within arm's reach, its red light bright and striking, illuminating ten thousand miles. He then raised his left and right hands to hold it, feeling a warm and radiant energy as if it were heating his heart and eyes. After a long time, he woke up. Looking at his hands, they were still sweating profusely. He found the dream strange and told it to others. Someone explained, "The sun represents the ruler. Now you dream of ascending a mountain to hold the sun, is it not a sign of ascending to the position of chancellor and assisting the ruler?" Later, Yang Yan traversed through clean and prestigious positions and eventually became chancellor. Indeed, it fulfilled the auspicious sign of holding the sun. #### Dou Can During the Zhenyuan period, Prime Minister Dou Can served as the Chief Censor. One night, he dreamed that Emperor Dezong summoned him to a private hall and asked him about matters of state. The emperor was pleased and bestowed a half-sleeved robe made of brocade on him. When he woke up, he found the dream strange and silently thought to himself, "The arm is a shelter. The large city shelters my body. Now I dream of a half-sleeved robe, is it not that the emperor has bestowed me with an honored position and will give me half of my salary, allowing me to retire from office?" He felt distressed for a long time. He then told his dream to others, and a guest explained, "Your dream is a sign of good fortune. And a half-sleeved robe is the garment of the limbs. Now you dream that the emperor bestows it on you, is it not that the emperor entrusts you with the position of a trusted minister?" The next day, he was indeed appointed as the Vice-Chancellor and Chancellor.

○贞卢犹子 The loyal relative of Lu Shangshu Zhen, a guest of the Crown Prince, was a monk. During the Kai Cheng period, when the monks were purged and expelled, he was returned to his family and appointed to the position of Canjun in the Guang Wang's household through hereditary privilege. One night, he dreamed that his teacher, whom he had revered during his time as a monk, came to comfort him. The teacher asked repeatedly about his current situation, and he replied that Buddhism had been destroyed, leaving him with no place to return. Now, he held a minor official position, merely passing the days, hoping to shave his head again to finally fulfill his wishes. He wept and lamented, and after a long while, the teacher said, "If my wish is indeed fulfilled, I will revive Buddhism." Before the teacher could finish speaking, he saw eight armies encamped around him, thousands of chariots and horses, flags and banners like the sun and moon, and splendidly dressed attendants forming a grand procession, truly the imperial entourage. The soldiers were loudly proclaiming, "Welcome the Guang Wang!" The procession was orderly, moving forward in sequence. Lu was startled and could not comprehend what was happening, and he suddenly woke up in shock. His soul trembled, and he broke out in a cold sweat, taking a long time to regain his composure. He never dared to reveal this dream to anyone. Not long after, Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne from the Guang Wang's residence, and Lu was appointed to a higher position according to the usual protocol. From then on, Buddhism gradually revived, with the restoration of temples, monasteries, and the old system for monks and nuns, all in accordance with what he had dreamed. Lu compared the teacher in his dream to the master of the military office, who was a close disciple of his former teacher. He believed this was a divine sign. Could it be that the gods' will was manifested through subtle signs becoming clear? ○郑光 During the spring of the sixth year of the Kai Cheng period, Zheng Shangshu Guang of Ziqing dreamed that he was driving a cow cart, with a radiant sun in the cart, illuminating the earth. He held the reins and walked down a wide street. Suddenly, he woke up in surprise and was amazed. A month later, Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, and due to his status as the emperor's maternal uncle, he was repeatedly appointed as the Shangshu and Governor of Ziqing, fulfilling the prophecy of his dream.

Zhang Shēn, during the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, was appointed by the imperial officials after serving as the magistrate of Wangwu County. Suddenly, he dreamed of an imperial messenger arriving. Zhang Shēn immediately prepared his official attire to welcome him. The messenger said to Zhang Shēn, "There is an imperial decree summoning you; please come with me." Zhang Shēn was both surprised and delighted, thinking that the emperor would soon employ him. He then ordered his carriage and went out with the messenger. Outside the door, there were more than ten officials waiting to escort him, which made Zhang Shēn even happier. They then left through the Kaiyuan Gate and headed west. On the left side of the road, there were many officials bowing twice in front of Zhang Shēn. After traveling nearly two hundred miles, they arrived at a city. The streets were bustling with people and carriages, and the roads were crowded. The willow trees cast shadows on both sides, and the smoke rose in a continuous line. About several miles northwest of the city, there was another city. Outside the city gates, there were hundreds of armored soldiers standing in rows on either side, holding spears and flags, forming a strict and orderly formation, as if it were the residence of an emperor. Upon reaching the gate, the messenger ordered Zhang Shēn to dismount. Zhang Shēn adjusted his cap and attire, and the messenger led him into the gate. There were many soldiers inside. They saw the majestic palaces and halls, which were both grand and beautiful. They then arrived at another gate, where the messenger led Zhang Shēn and more than a hundred others, all dressed in official attire, into the courtyard, where they stood in a formal and respectful manner. Another hall stood imposingly before them, adorned with jewels and precious stones, truly the imperial hall. On both sides of the hall, there were dozens of armed soldiers standing. Inside the hall, there were many imperial messengers dressed in red and purple. One person with a tall hat and a ceremonial robe adorned with dragon patterns was seated on a jade table in the eastern part of the palace. Another person, dressed in a manner resembling a woman, was also seated on a jade table in the western part of the palace. There were dozens of palace attendants standing in front. The messenger told Zhang Shēn, "The emperor is in the eastern part of the palace; you may go forward to pay respects." Zhang Shēn hurried to the front of the eastern part of the palace and bowed twice. A red-robed imperial messenger stood in front of the palace's front hall.

The Emperor said, "You should now hasten to attend to my palace affairs, and do not let there be any that do not conform to the rules." Sheng bowed twice and danced, showing his deep respect. Soon after, the imperial messenger led him to the west pavilion, where the ceremony was similar to that of the east pavilion. After the ceremony, the messenger led him out of the gate. Sheng felt very frightened and then said to the messenger, "I have been long stationed in a distant province and have never seen the emperor. Is it not against the rules for me to have been present at the court audience?" The messenger smiled and said, "Our lord is lenient; do not be afraid." As he finished speaking and looked east, hundreds of soldiers galloped towards them. The messenger told Sheng, "These are the night watch soldiers. You must hurry away and do not violate the strict prohibition." He immediately called a servant to prepare a carriage. Sheng woke up in a state of confusion and found the dream strange, but he did not dare to speak of it to anyone. A few days later, Sheng was appointed as the magistrate of a certain county. All that he experienced matched what he had dreamed. During the burial of the Empress alongside the emperor, Sheng's dream of the east pavilion, where a man wearing a tall hat and a dragon robe was Emperor Gaozong, and the west pavilion, where a woman dressed like a lady was the Empress Wu Zetian, came true. A few months later, when he arrived in Chang'an, he met with several friends and told them about the dream. Someone showed him the true portraits of the emperors and empresses, and indeed, Emperor Gaozong and Empress Wu Zetian were the ones he had seen in his dream.

Xuan said, “You should now hasten to manage the affairs of my palace, and do not allow anything to be done contrary to the law.” Shen again bowed twice and performed a ceremonial dance. Soon after, the imperial messenger led him to the western part of the hall, following the same ritual as at the eastern part. After bowing, the messenger then led him out through the gate. Shen was extremely anxious and said to him, “I have long served in the outer provinces and have never had the chance to see the Son of Heaven. Earlier, when I stood before the court, was that perhaps not in accordance with the rites?” The imperial messenger smiled and said, “Our lord is lenient; there is no need to fear.” After saying this, he looked eastward, and hundreds of soldiers galloped toward them. The messenger said to Shen, “These are the night watch soldiers. You must leave quickly, and do not violate the strict regulations.” He immediately called a clerk to prepare a carriage. In the midst of his confusion, Shen awoke, secretly finding the dream strange, and dared not speak of it to anyone. A few days later, Shen was appointed as the commander of a certain mausoleum. All the places he subsequently visited corresponded exactly to what he had dreamed. Moreover, when the Tianhou was buried alongside the emperor, the figure Shen had seen beneath the eastern part of the hall—wearing a tall cap and the dragon-embroidered robes of an emperor—was indeed Emperor Gaozong. The figure beneath the western part of the hall, dressed in attire resembling that of a woman, was the Tianhou herself. A few months later, when Shen arrived in Chang’an, he met with several friends and stayed overnight with them, telling them the entire story. One of them showed Shen the authentic portraits of the successive emperors, and indeed, Emperor Gaozong and the Tianhou were exactly as he had seen in his dream.

Hou Sheng Hou Sheng, a man from Shanggu, resided in Jingmen. He entered officialdom through his knowledge of Confucian classics and was appointed as the magistrate of Yucheng County in Song Prefecture. He had recently married a woman from the Han family of Nanyang, and it had been five years since their marriage. One night, Han woman dreamed of several people dressed in yellow robes who summoned her out of her door and led her east for more than ten li to an official residence. Under the eaves, there were dozens of officials and soldiers. The buildings were grand and magnificent, and there were many people. They then led her to a courtyard where there was a person dressed in green, wearing a tall hat and square shoes, who appeared very majestic and imposing. Hundreds of attendants stood on both sides, with tables, mats, and seats arranged in front and behind. Han woman bowed twice. Soon, a woman about twenty years old, tall and beautiful, wearing a green robe with red sleeves adorned with gold and jade hairpins, came from the door and introduced herself as Lu.

Han said to her, "I have been your enemy for a long time, do you know about it?" Han replied, "I am just a woman, never having left my inner chamber, how could there be an enemy?" Lu's face turned very angry, and she said, "In my previous life, I was an official. You falsely accused me of a crime and took my place, causing me to be exiled and die in the wilderness. Isn't that an enemy? Now I have appealed to the Supreme Being, and want to clear the injustice of my previous life. The Supreme Being has agreed to my request, and your death is imminent." Han became increasingly afraid, wanting to refuse with words, but Lu kept talking incessantly. The person in plain clothes said to Lu, "Your injustice is indeed as you say. But Han is not necessarily destined to die. It is not right to do so." Then he ordered the official to bring out the records. The official said, "Han has one year left." The person in plain clothes said, "You can quickly send him back, do not keep him here for long." He ordered to send him to the door. After walking a few miles, Han suddenly felt afraid and woke up. He was afraid and dared not speak of it. From then on, his demeanor became dejected, as if he were ill. Hou asked him about it, and he told him about the dream in detail. Several months later, Han dreamed again that Lu came to his house and said, "You are going to die." Han woke up in shock. From then on, his illness worsened, and he died a year later.

Mr. Hou secretly sighed in amazement, never telling anyone. Several years later, while traveling through the regions of Xiang and Han, he encountered a county official from Lanling, Mr. Xiao, at Fushui. Mr. Xiao admired Mr. Hou's virtue and arranged for his daughter to marry him. When Xiao's daughter returned home, she always wore red sleeves and green shirts, adorned with gold and jade hairpins, and was tall and beautiful, matching the description in Han's previous dream. Mr. Hou then told her about Han's dream. Upon hearing this, Xiao's daughter was very unhappy and said, "My family is from the Lu clan. Since childhood, my uncle remembered me and designated me as his own daughter, so I was given the name Lu. Therefore, your deceased wife's dream is true."

### Final Translation --- ### 太白老僧 In the Tang Dynasty, there was a young man from the Lu family in Pingyang, who was curious and had traveled with Taoist practitioners since his youth. Later, he settled in Mount Tai. One day, an old monk knocked on his door. The young man invited him to sit and they talked for a long time. The monk said, "You who are curious, but you have not yet reached the profound and mysterious essence. It is futile to live in the mountains alone. Would it not be better to wear fine furs, ride swift horses, and wander through the capital city? Could this not fulfill your lifelong aspirations, rather than associating with deer?" The young man thanked him, saying, "Your words, sir, seem to be those of a truly wise person. However, you have not shown me any signs of your profound wisdom. Why then do you speak of empty words to boast?" The monk replied, "Let me show you the signs of my profound wisdom." Saying this, he took out a small box from his clothes, about one inch in size, black and shiny. After opening it, he entered it. Soon after, he transformed into a bird and flew into the sky. ### 开业寺 On October 23, 757 CE, during the reign of Emperor Suzong of Tang, at the Opening Temple in Fengle District, there were footprints of a divine being, very long, from the outer gate to the Buddha hall. Before this, the gatekeeper, who was on duty, dreamed of a person, over six feet tall, wearing golden armor and holding a spear, standing outside the temple gate. Soon after, the person used his hand to pull the door, and all the locks were undone. The divine being then entered the temple, walking to the Buddha hall, and looked around for a long time before disappearing. The gatekeeper woke up in shock. In the morning, he saw that the door was already open. He reported his dream to the monks at the temple. The monks saw the footprints of the divine being. They reported this to the Governor of Jingzhao. The governor sent an imperial envoy to verify the event, and it was as they所述的那样。

During the time when Chen Shaoyou was stationed in Huainan, he once sent a soldier named Zhao to the capital to deliver letters to the high officials. Before departing, he warned Zhao: "I have urgent matters, and I await your report upon your return. Because of your bravery and strength, I am sending you westward, and you must not linger. If you do not arrive on schedule, you will be executed." Zhao traveled hundreds of li each day without tiring. Upon reaching Huayin County, he stayed at an inn. Just as he was about to fall asleep, he suddenly saw a man dressed in green. The man said to Zhao: "I am a servant of the King of Gold Heaven. The king has summoned you, and you should depart quickly." Zhao was puzzled and immediately set out with the messenger. They arrived at the temple before the prison, where the messenger went inside to inform the king that Zhao had arrived. Shortly after, Zhao was called. He rushed to bow at the foot of the steps. In the hall, the candles were lit, and he saw a man sitting at a table, surrounded by strict guards. The man spoke to Zhao slowly: "I have a son-in-law in Shu who has been there for several years, and I wish to send someone to visit him. There is no one else to send. I have heard that you are skilled at traveling, covering hundreds of li each day. Would you be willing to go to Shu on my behalf?" Zhao replied: "The Chancellor has ordered me to go west to Chang'an, and there is a set date. If I fail to comply, I will be executed. Now, if I go to Shu for the king, it would mean abandoning the Chancellor's command. I truly dare not return to Guangling. Moreover, my parents, wife, and children are all waiting for me. How can I leave them behind and not return to my hometown? I am not daring to refuse out of any other reason, but please, Your Majesty, understand." The king said: "Go directly for me, and it won't be that bad. You can return via Chang'an after your mission in Shu, which is not too late." The king then left Zhao to stay the night in an empty room behind the temple, providing him with food and drink. Zhao was anxious and confused, unable to sleep. He eventually went to Shu, fearing that he might be punished. He firmly declined to go, but also worried that the disaster might affect others.

The plan had not yet been decided when it gradually grew light. Hearing a noisy sound in the temple, he went out to see and saw nearly ten thousand tigers, leopards, deer, foxes, rabbits, birds, and other animals in the courtyard, as well as thousands of strange-looking spirits, all arrayed in rows and bowing as if in a formal ceremony of court attendance.
