Soon, several people came in to bring a lawsuit, and the Golden Heaven judged the case very clearly. After a long time, they left. The king then called Zhao, who responded and went away. The king commanded Zhao to ascend the steps and took out a scroll from his sleeve, giving it to Zhao and saying, "Take this and go to Shu Prefecture for me. Visit Xiao Jingzhi of Chengdu and give it to him. I have many officials here, but because the matter is confidential, I fear it might leak out, and it cannot be entrusted to ordinary people. You should return quickly within one or two days, and do not stay long." The king then gave Zhao a thousand coins. Zhao bowed and thanked him before leaving. Upon reaching the gate, Zhao told the official, "The king gave me a thousand coins, but since I am walking, where can I carry them?" The official replied, "Put them in your pocket." Zhao then placed the coins in his pocket, finding it did not hinder him and he did not feel its weight. After walking a few miles, Zhao checked his clothes and found that they were all paper money, which he immediately discarded by the roadside. Soon, someone followed and caught up with Zhao, giving him several thousand coins and saying, "Before, I mistakenly gave you the money used in the secret path, which you would not have needed. Now, I am giving you this instead." Zhao accepted the coins.

Traveling day and night, Zhao arrived in Chengdu after more than ten days. He visited Xiao Jingzhi and handed over a letter to him. Xiao opened the letter and was very happy, then ordered a meal and said to Zhao, "I am a human, and my family is in the Zheng area. Last year, I went to the capital for an appointment and was on the way to Huayin when I was forced to marry the King of Gold Heaven. Now my wife is here, and she is no different from ordinary people. Earlier, I strongly sought an official position, and now it has been achieved, so I have sent you to report this." Xiao kept Zhao for a day, gave him several pieces of silk, and sent him back with the letter. Passing through Chang'an, Zhao delivered the letter to Shao You. Upon receiving the reply, Zhao traveled day and night back to Huayin. The King of Gold Heaven was very happy to see Zhao and comforted him, saying, "No one else can be sent. Now I am sending you back. If the Chancellor interrogates you, just say you were sent by me, and I will send you as a deputy general; do not be afraid." He then gave Zhao several pieces of silk and said, "This is silk from the human world, which you can use." Zhao bowed and thanked him, then directly returned to Huainan. Shao You interrogated Zhao about his delay, and Zhao fully answered with the facts. Shao You was angry, did not believe him, and imprisoned him. That night, Shao You dreamed of a man wearing golden armor and holding a sword, who said, "The King of Gold Heaven informs the Chancellor that earlier he indeed sent Zhao to Shu, and now he has heard of his offense and wishes to release him." Shao You woke up in fear and marveled at the dream for a long time. The next morning, he told his attendants about the dream and immediately ordered Zhao to be released and appointed as a deputy general. Zhao was still alive during the Yuanhe period.

### Yuan Zai and Zhang Wei Yuan Zai, when he was a commoner, was close friends with Zhang Wei, who had previously served as Vice Minister of Rites. They were poor, lacking servants or horses, and traveled through the regions of Chen and Cai in tattered clothes. One day, as night fell, a sudden storm with thunder and lightning darkened the countryside. Yuan Zai and Zhang Wei sought shelter in a temple on the side of the road. At that moment, several thieves, armed with swords and bows, were hiding under the temple. Upon seeing the thieves, Yuan Zai and Zhang Wei became even more fearful and stood against the wall, afraid to move. Suddenly, a voice from within the temple called out: "The Chancellor Yuan and the Vice Minister Zhang are coming. The thieves should leave quickly, as they will not harm the noble men." The thieves looked at each other in shock and hurriedly fled. Yuan Zai and Zhang Wei then congratulated each other, saying, "We were once worried about starving to death, but today we truly believe the words came from a divine being." They were both relieved and grateful. Later, Yuan Zai indeed became a chancellor under Emperor Dezong, and Zhang Wei remained a Vice Minister of Rites. ### Chen and Yuan Sheng At the beginning of the reign of Emperor Dezong, Chen Yuan Sheng, who had once served as a military officer in Tang'an, resigned his post and traveled to Bashu. He stayed at an inn. Suddenly, a man dressed in white came to pay his respects. After sitting down, the man said to Yuan Sheng, "I am a son of the High Clan, and my family lives in Xinming County of this prefecture. In the past, I served in the military, but I have since retired and am traveling here." Yuan Sheng engaged in conversation with him, and the man's intelligence, eloquence, and breadth of knowledge were remarkable, far surpassing those of ordinary people. Yuan Sheng was impressed. The man then said, "I am skilled in calculations and can analyze your life events." Yuan Sheng asked him to do so, and the man recounted Yuan Sheng's past events, describing them as if they were written down. Yuan Sheng was greatly surprised. That night, when it was deep into the night, the man quietly told Yuan Sheng, "I am not a human being."

I hope to present this to a gentleman, may it be acceptable?" Upon hearing this, Yuan Sheng was filled with fear and immediately stood up, saying, "Are you not human? Are you a ghost, and will you bring misfortune to me?" Gao Sheng replied, "I am not a ghost, nor will I bring misfortune to you. The reason I am here is to entrust something to you. I am the deity of the Red River, and there is a shrine in the south of Xinming. Last year, there were months of heavy rain, and all the houses are now ruined. The people of the prefecture have no one to repair it, and I am being eroded by wind and sun, and daily insulted by woodcutters and shepherds. The people of the village treat me like a handful of earth. Now I am appealing to you. If you think it is right, then speak; if not, then leave. I hope you will not be angry." Yuan Sheng said, "If the god has a wish, how can it not be possible?" The deity said, "Next year, you will be appointed as the magistrate of Xinming. If you rebuild the shrine for me and offer timely sacrifices, then it will truly be a great favor. I hope you will not forget." Yuan Sheng agreed. The deity then added, "When you first arrive in the county, you should come to see me once. However, the relationship between humans and gods is distant, and I am concerned that your servants might desecrate me. You should dismiss your servants and enter the temple alone. I hope to speak with you fully." Yuan Sheng said, "I will follow your instructions."

During that winter, Yuan was indeed appointed to a new order. When he arrived at his post and investigated the matter, he found that there was indeed a temple dedicated to the Red Water god, located several miles south of the county. After a few days, he went to the temple. Before reaching it by a hundred steps, he dismounted and had the carriage and officials clear the way, then entered the temple alone. He saw that the eaves and roof were in ruins, overgrown with weeds and wild grass. He stood and looked for a long time, and then a man in white clothes came out from behind the temple—it was Gao, who looked very happy. After bowing, Gao said to Yuan, "You did not forget our previous agreement, and today you have come to me. How fortunate!" They then walked together in the temple. At the steps and walls, Yuan saw an old monk wearing shackles, with several people standing beside him. Yuan asked, "Who is this person?" The god replied, "This monk is Daocheng, who lived in a temple in the eastern part of the county. He has suffered misfortune, so I have bound him for a year now. Every morning and evening, I beat and whip him. From now on, in a few days, I will release him." Yuan asked again, "Since this monk still exists, how can he be bound here?" The god said, "I bound his soul. So the person naturally fell ill, and how could he know what I am doing?" The god then told Yuan, "You have kindly promised to build a temple for me. Please hurry and plan it." Yuan replied, "I will not forget." After returning, Yuan planned the work, but he was very poor and had no means to fund it. He then thought to himself, "The words of the god about binding Daocheng's soul, he naturally fell ill. And he said that in a few days, he will be released. I will now fabricate another story to build the temple. How could there be any doubt?"

A monk named Daocheng fell gravely ill and was bedridden for a year. He said, "I am dying, and my entire body aches day and night." Yuan, a knowledgeable man, replied, "Your illness is severe and you are close to death. However, I can cure you. Can you build a temple to the Red Water God with money?" Daocheng said, "If I am indeed cured, how could I use money for such a purpose!" Yuan then lied and said, "I am skilled in seeing ghosts. Recently, I visited the Red Water God Temple and saw your soul bound with shackles under a wall. I summoned the Red Water God to ask about the matter, and he said, 'This monk has committed past misdeeds, so he is bound here.' I pitied your suffering and told the god, 'Why bind a living person? Release him quickly. I will command this monk to build a temple, so be careful not to disobey.' The god was happy and promised me, saying, 'From now on, after more than ten days, his sin will be forgiven.' Therefore, I told you that your illness will heal, and you should build the Red Water God Temple. If you do not build the temple after your illness heals, you will become complacent. If so, disaster will come upon you." Daocheng feigned agreement and said, "I respectfully accept your instruction." After more than ten days, he indeed recovered. He then called his disciples and said, "When I was young, I left home to study Buddhist teachings. Now at fifty, I am unfortunately suffering from a serious illness. Earlier, Mr. Yuan told me, 'Your illness is caused by the Red Water God. If you are cured, you can repair the temple.' Setting up a temple is to protect the people and respond to prayers for blessings. Now that it has harmed me, how can it not be removed?" Immediately, he took his disciples and carried hammers to the temple, completely removing the statues and the temple, leaving nothing behind.

The day after tomorrow, Daocheng visited Yuan Sheng. Yuan Sheng was delighted and said, "Has the teacher's illness indeed recovered? Was my statement false?" Daocheng replied, "Yes. Thank you for saving me, how dare I forget your kindness?" Yuan Sheng said, "You should quickly plan to repair the Red River God Temple, otherwise, you will be afraid of disaster." Daocheng said, "The reason why gods rely on people is because their blessings can be extended, and their misfortunes can be averted. In case of drought, they pray for rain; in case of flooding, they pray for clear skies. Therefore, the emperor decrees to all the prefectures and kingdoms, even in the smallest town or village, they must build a shrine, in order to benefit the people. If the Red River God does not benefit people but harms them, how can it not be removed? I have already completely destroyed its temple." Yuan Sheng was both surprised and fearful, and thanked him. Daocheng's spirit became more abundant, while Yuan Sheng was very afraid.

It was during the reign of Emperor Yuanhe. | "后月余而锷薨" is translated to conclude the story with Wang E's death and the time period. | ```

A month later, an official committed a crime, and Yuan Sheng redeemed him. Soon after, the official died, and his family petitioned the county, leading to Yuan Sheng being exiled to Duanxi. On his journey to the Three Gorges, he suddenly encountered a man in white clothes standing on the left side of the road. Looking at him, he realized it was the god of Chi Shui. The god said, "Previously, I entrusted you to repair my shrine. How could Daocheng destroy my dwelling and abandon my image, leaving me with nowhere to return? This is your fault. Now that you are exiled to the remote wilderness, this is my revenge." Yuan Sheng immediately apologized, saying, "It was Daocheng who destroyed you. Why should I be blamed?" The god replied, "Daocheng’s fortune and blessings were too great for me to move against. Now that your fortune and destiny have declined, I am finally able to take my revenge." With these words, the god vanished. Yuan Sheng was greatly disturbed by this, and a few days later, he died of illness. ○ A Minor Clerk of Taiyuan Wang E governed Taiyuan. Once, at midday, a minor clerk saw a divine figure, over ten feet tall, clad in armor and wielding a sword, slowly walking from the government office. He then stood there for a long time, as if waiting for something. The clerk, greatly frightened, reported this to the officials Jin Yuan and Zhang He. They went together to see it, and it was exactly as the clerk had said. Soon after, a violent wind arose, and the figure suddenly vanished. A month later, Wang E died. This was during the Yuanhe era.

○ Village Man Chen翁 In the Yun-Shuo region, there was once a severe drought. The heat was also intense, and among the villagers, thousands suffered from heat illness. There was a villager named Chen翁, who was walking alone in the fields when he suddenly encountered a man whose appearance was very strange. The man was wearing golden armor, had a bow and arrows on both sides, was holding a long sword, and was riding a fine horse. He had red tassels and golden ornaments, radiating a splendid and brilliant aura. He whipped the horse to gallop. Just as he met Chen翁, he stopped the horse and spoke: "Are you not one of the villagers?" Chen翁 replied, "I am a farmer, and I have lived here for many years." The divine being said, "I am an envoy of heaven. The Heavenly Deity has caused the people of your village to suffer from heat illness. Is it not just due to the scorching sun? Moreover, there is a malevolent ghost in your town, which is why the people are ill. The Heavenly Deity has commanded me to chase it away." And then he disappeared. Chen翁 immediately reported the matter to the villagers. From then on, the heat illness in the Yun-Shuo region was cured. ○ Cui Ze Wang E governed Taiyuan, and there was a man from Qinghe named Cui Ze, who served as the prefect of Fangzhou during the Longqing period. He often sought refuge from the heat in his courtyard, and at that time, the wind and moon were clear and bright. Suddenly, he saw a tall man, wearing a high hat and wide sleeves, descending from the front of the hall, standing on the steps, and shouting loudly. He shouted three times and then stopped. The entire Cui family saw him. Cui Ze was frightened and repulsed, and he ordered his servant to drive him away. But the man had already disappeared. That night, Cui Ze fell ill. The next day, he sent a messenger to present a letter, expressing his wish to resign from his official position and retire. The chancellor's office did not permit it. After a month or so, Cui Ze died in the prefecture.

### Final Translation #### Han Yu (I) The Department of Personnel Deputy Minister Han Yu, during the fourth year of the Changqing era in the summer, neglected his official duties due to illness. By the ninth month of autumn, he was dismissed from office, and his illness worsened. In the eleventh month of winter, while lying down during the day in Jing'an Li, he saw a divine figure, over ten feet tall, clad in armor, wielding a sword, and carrying a bow and arrow. The figure had a stern demeanor and entered his bedroom, standing at the foot of his bed. After a while, the figure told Han Yu, "The emperor has commanded me to discuss matters with you." Han Yu quickly rose, adjusted his cap, and sat up, saying, "I am unfortunate to have an illness, but how dare I sit here in such a casual manner before Your Majesty?" The divine figure replied, "The powerful and malevolent nation of Weicui Guo has been an enemy of the Han family for generations. Now we wish to attack them, but our strength is insufficient. What do you think?" Han Yu responded, "I am willing to follow Your Majesty in this campaign." The divine figure nodded and departed. Han Yu then wrote down the words and placed them beside his seat, but he could not understand them for several days. He passed away in December. #### Li Fengji The former prime minister Li Fengji once served as an official under Fan Xichao at the Xiongnu chieftain's office. At the time, there was an old monk named Wuwei at the Golden City Temple, who was over seventy years old. One day, he was alone in his meditation hall, sitting against the wall, and blinking his eyes while counting his breaths. Suddenly, a figure clad in armor and holding a long staff appeared at the temple. Soon after, he heard that Li Fengji was coming. From then on, whenever Li Fengji visited the Golden City Temple, the old monk Wuwei would see the same divine figure arrive first, and this became a regular occurrence. The military officer Jian Ying, who was friendly with Wuwei's disciple Fazhen, often spoke to him about these events.

○李回 The former Chancellor Li Hui of the Tang Dynasty, when he was young, suffered from a long-term illness. His elder brother, Qian, summoned shamans to set up wine and food in the courtyard to please the gods. Li Hui lay facing the wall, when suddenly he heard noise in the courtyard. He turned around and saw dozens of people in the hall, some wearing yellow and green clothes, competing to take and eat the wine and food. After a long time, they were about to disperse, and the shaman wanted to clear the table. Suddenly, a person descended from the sky, with wings on both sides. All the ghosts retreated in fear, and said, "The spirit of Master Lu has arrived." The shaman also exclaimed, "The spirit of Master Lu has come." He immediately ordered to bring wine and food to the courtyard. The figure's head bowed over the table, almost finished eating, then drank the wine. In a moment, his face turned red, as if drunk, and then flew away. The ghosts also disappeared. After several days, Li Hui's illness was cured. ○郄元位 Xie Yuanwei, a military officer under the Hedong governorship, was frequently sent on missions to the capital during the Tahe era. He traveled to Shayan as evening approached. He saw a person over ten feet tall, wearing purple and carrying gold, with a majestic appearance, riding a white horse, which was also over ten feet tall. Accompanied by nearly ten attendants, all of whom had unusual appearances and carried bows and arrows, the figure came from the south. Xie Yuanwei was greatly surprised and dismounted to avoid the person. The attendant suddenly raised his whip to point west, as if seeing something, and the attendants followed the direction and looked. Xie Yuanwei also looked west, but saw nothing. When he turned back, he saw nothing. Xie Yuanwei suddenly broke out in a cold sweat, his thighs trembled, and his heart was terrified, causing him to fall off his horse. He fell ill with a fever and was carried back home. He recovered after more than ten days. At that time, Li Yuan, the Governor of Hedong and Grand Master of Works, died.

In the county of Xiayang, which belongs to Fengyi Prefecture and is situated along the Yellow River, there is a pavilion to the east of the county. Facing Mount Hua and the Zhongtiao Mountains, the pavilion is surrounded by mist and mountain haze, visible from dawn to dusk. To the south of the pavilion lies the Feno Spring, whose water is crystal clear, revealing even the smallest details. During the middle period of the Northern Wei dynasty, there was a man named Zhao who served as an official in Xiayang. One evening after the rain had stopped, Zhao and his friends walked together and gazed at the moon by the Feno Spring. Suddenly, they saw a person with a dark complexion and wearing a green robe, who had been swimming in the water for a long time and then recited, "The night moon shines brightly, the green waves flow endlessly." Zhao was startled, and the person suddenly turned back to look at the shore, as if frightened, and then dived back into the water, leaving only his head visible. After a short while, he disappeared as well. The next day, Zhao returned to the spring and found a temple about dozens of steps from the shore. The entrance of the temple was marked with the words "God of the Feno Spring." Zhao entered the temple and saw that the god was seated on either side, with clay figurines molded beside him. The figure wearing a green robe resembled the person he had seen in the water the previous night. Zhao said, "This is the god of the Feno Spring, who can still deceive people. What else could it be but a supernatural phenomenon?" He intended to destroy the temple. A county official said, "This is a temple of the god, which can help with the growth of crops through rain and wind. If you destroy the temple, it would only cause trouble for the people of the county." Thus, Zhao did not ultimately destroy the temple.

○卢嗣宗 In Puzhen, there is a shrine dedicated to Shun, and nearby, there are shrines dedicated to Ehuang and Nüying. The clay figures in these shrines are quite exquisite and beautiful. During the Kai Cheng period, Lu Sizong of Fanyang held a temporary post in Puzhen. One day, he visited the Shun shrine with several friends, and they went to the Ehuang and Nüying shrine. Lu Sizong joked, "I wish to be a servant of the imperial sons, may I?" He bowed twice and prayed for a long time. His friends all said, "How disrespectful and impure are your words to the gods!" Lu Sizong laughed even more heartily. From then on, he often visited the Ehuang shrine alone, drinking heavily and making many impure remarks. Suddenly, he fell ill and was carried home on a shoulder yoke, with a fearful and trembling face and profuse sweating. That night, he died. The household servants saw more than ten people drag Lu Sizong out of the door and head towards the Shun shrine. When they examined Lu Sizong's body, they found many red marks on his back, as if he had been struck. The people of Puzhen were all amazed by the event. ○高生 During the Tian Bao period, there was a man from Bohai whose name is unknown. He suffered from fever and emaciation, with unbearable chest pain. He called a doctor to examine him, and the doctor said, "There is a ghost in your chest, and medicine can reach it." So he boiled the medicine and drank it. Suddenly, he felt something moving in the darkness. After a while, he vomited several liters of saliva, which was solid and could not be dissolved. He cut it open with a knife, and a person rose from the saliva. Initially small, the person grew to several feet tall. Gao wanted to harm him, but the person rushed out and disappeared immediately. From then on, his disease subsided.

### Translation A Man from Xingyang V, a man from Xingyang, often rode alone on horseback. One day, he encountered a maid who was exceedingly beautiful in appearance. She bowed before him on the road and said, "Madam Cui invites you, V." Startled, V replied, "I have never met Madam Cui, and I am still unmarried. Why do you invite me?" The maid answered, "Madam Cui's daughter is quite beautiful and virtuous, and given her noble family and fine lineage, she is a suitable match for you." V realized that this was not an ordinary situation and wanted to refuse. Immediately, more than ten elderly men in yellow robes arrived and said, "Madam Cui urges you to come." They immediately controlled the horse, and the journey was very fast. V only heard the wind whistling in his ears as they suddenly arrived at a place with tall walls and high gates, surrounded by rows of willows and poplars. V stood at the door, and the maid announced him first. Soon, he was commanded to enter. He passed through several gates, and the buildings were grand and magnificent. The lady, dressed in a green silk skirt, was about forty years old and had an adorable appearance. She stood at the eastern steps. There were eight or nine maidservants, all fresh and tidy. V hurriedly paid his respects and bowed twice. The lady said, "It is not strange to invite you, as V comes from a noble family with fine talent, and we wish to form a marital alliance. Our daughter is not perfect, but if you could take notice, it would be greatly appreciated." Feeling pressured, V did not know how to respond and only nodded in agreement.

The lady then ascended the hall and ordered someone to lead Zheng Lang up the western steps. The entire hall was covered with flowered carpets, and on both sides were placed low tables, seven-treasure screens, golden knee rests, green curtains hanging at the doors, and silver hooks with pearl nets. A long banquet table was set with abundant and clean food. She then ordered him to sit. The lady was skilled in clear discourse, arranging priorities, and her abilities were hard to match in the world. After the meal, wine was ordered, stored in silver, amounting to about three dou, with an amber color. It was served in engraved cups by attendants, and the wine was extremely sweet and fragrant. As evening approached, a maid reported that the young lady had finished dressing. She then ordered. Zheng Lang was led out to the outer room, where he was bathed in a hundred-flavored fragrant bath, and his clothes, hat, shoes, and ornaments were presented. Ten beautiful maids helped him in, and they freely teased him. From the hall to the door, they led him with flowered candles, and then invited him into the tent. The girl was around fourteen or fifteen years old, her beauty was very striking, and her appearance had never been seen before. Her attire was splendid, and her beauty surpassed all of her time. Zheng then happily. Afterward, the ceremony was completed. The next day, the lady ordered the girl to go to the eastern hall. In the hall, there were red embroidered curtains, bedding, and mats, all of which were extremely fine. The girl was skilled at playing the konghou, and her songs and lyrics were new and unique. Zheng asked about the horse he rode when he came to marry, and where it was now, and she replied, "It has already returned."

For over a hundred days, V was deeply affectionate towards the woman, but his heart grew slightly jealous and suspicious. He then asked her, "Can we go home together?" The woman sadly replied, "I am fortunate to have met you by chance and to be able to serve you, but there is a separation between the living and the dead. How can we be together?" She began to cry and sob. V realized the strange nature of the woman and reported to her superior, saying, "I have been lost in the family, and they are quite suspicious. I request to be returned." The superior said, "Just now, I received your visit, and I am deeply grateful and admiring. However, the paths between the living and the dead are different, and it is reasonable to be temporarily separated. At the moment of separation, how can one not shed tears?" V also wept. They then held a grand banquet and said their farewells, with the superior promising, "In three years, I will come to fetch you." V bowed and took his leave. The woman went out the door, waving tears and shaking hands, saying, "Even though there is a future meeting, the years still extend. Our joyous meetings are still short, and the separation is painful and long. Take care of yourself!" V also felt sad. The woman gave him a red robe and a pair of gold hairpins as gifts, saying, "If you do not forget me, keep this in mind." They parted ways, and the superior ordered the escort of V, giving him a green horse with a very fine saddle and bridle.

V rode out on his horse, and in the blink of an eye, he returned to his home. His servant then said, "The young master has been missing for a year." Upon examining the gifts he had left, they were all real and genuine. The family said, "After the young master left, his horse returned by itself, but no one was seen bringing it back." V then searched for the place where he had been, and he found only a large tomb, with a small grave beside it. The trees in front of the tomb were all withered, but the ones he had seen before were lush and provided shade. Those around him said, "These are the graves of Cui's wife and her son." V was particularly struck by this. He calculated that his three-year mourning period was coming to an end, and he must die. When the time came, he indeed saw the servant he had sent before, riding in a carriage to greet him. V said, "Life and death are determined by fate. If I can find happiness, what else do I have to worry about?" He then divided and settled all family affairs, preparing for the end. The next day, he died.

During the Tang dynasty, Li Linfu had served as chancellor for a long time. Believing that his hidden misdeeds were numerous, he found the world to be quite resentful, leading to ghostly calamities. He then summoned practitioners of esoteric arts to dispel them. Later, he found a sorcerer who said, "The chancellor has been wealthy and powerful for a long time, and there are many who bear resentment against him. The foundation of his calamities is not the result of a single day or night. Although, perhaps what can be avoided is the daily calamity." Linfu asked, "What should be done?" The sorcerer replied, "One could seek a skilled archer in the capital to guard against it." Linfu then recruited one in the western market. The man said, "I used to serve in the military, and was known for my archery. Recently, I have been ill, and know nothing else." Linfu then provided him with clothing and food, and supplied him monthly. One evening, Linfu held a banquet in the courtyard. The musicians and dancers were performing, and the music had not yet finished when it suddenly stopped. The skilled archer listened in surprise, but heard nothing.

Then he silently planned and said, "The night is not yet over, suddenly like this, is there some other reason, or is it the words of a fortune-teller?" He then took his bow and arrows and climbed over the wall to enter, waiting to see. Suddenly, he saw something fall from the south side of the wall, and another person climbed over to come. The skilled archer hit it with one shot, and then fled in surprise. He then went to Lin Fu's place of pleasure, where he saw singers and dancers who could not sing or move their sleeves. They were silent and motionless like wooden dolls. He then looked at the object that fell from the south side of the wall, which was a bag tied up. He opened it, and found hundreds of slips inside, all with the names of Lin Fu and his servants. Then he called out the names, and each one responded, so they resumed their feast as before. The next day, the fortune-teller came and congratulated him, saying, "Thanks to this person, otherwise, I would have been harmed. It would have been a wrongful death. You have long been in charge of important affairs, accumulating countless grievances. From now on, for ten years, it is beyond my knowledge." Later, Lin Fu was dispossessed, and indeed, it was ten years.

He silently pondered and said, “The night is not yet over, yet something has suddenly happened—could this be something else, or is it the words of the fortune-teller?” Then he took up his bow and arrows, climbed over the wall, and entered to observe. Suddenly, he saw something fall from the south side of the wall, and another person climbed over. The skilled archer shot once and struck it, and the figure then fled in alarm. He then went to Liu Linfu’s place of pleasure, where he saw singers and dancers, yet they were silent and unable to utter a sound, motionless and unable to raise their sleeves, utterly still as if they were wooden puppets. He then looked at the object that had fallen from the south side of the wall, which was a bag tied shut. He untied it and found within it hundreds of slips of paper, each bearing the names of Liu Linfu and his attendants. He then called out each name in turn, and each person responded accordingly, and so the feast and drinking resumed as before. The following day, the fortune-teller came and greeted him, saying, “Thanks to this person, otherwise I would have been ruined, and would have died unjustly. Your Excellency has long held the reins of power and has accumulated countless grievances. From now on, ten years—this is beyond my knowledge.” Later, when Liu Linfu’s family was seized and their property confiscated, it indeed turned out to be exactly ten years.

The Ghost of Sin Yu During the reign of Emperor Daizong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a scholar named Sin Yu, who came from the Huaihai region. After failing the imperial examinations, he intended to travel to Chengdu. Upon reaching Yangzhou, he died suddenly without any illness. Sin Yu had been friends with Shen Sheng, the magistrate of Huaiyin from Wu Prefecture, but they had lost contact for many years, and Shen Sheng did not know where Sin Yu had gone. Shen Sheng was transferred from Huaihai to serve as the magistrate of Jintang and arrived in Yangzhou, where he stayed at an inn. That night, the moon was bright and the wind was clear. Just as midnight approached, Shen Sheng felt as if he had lost something and could not sleep. Soon, he saw a man in white robes walking towards the door, reciting poetry and sighing, as if filled with sorrow and unable to express it. After a while, the man recited: "Home rests by the shores of the Chu River, Body lodged in a pavilion of Yangzhou. Gazing at the moon, I think of you alone, My sleeves wet with tears." Shen Sheng recognized the man as resembling Sin Yu. He stood up and tried to speak, but the man disappeared. Shen Sheng sighed, "I have been separated from my friend Sin Yu for so long, could he have become a ghost?" The next day, Shen Sheng left the inn and traveled a few miles when he saw a funeral pavilion on the side of the road. A passerby informed him, "This is the funeral pavilion for the scholar Sin Yu." Shen Sheng was shocked and hurried back to the inn to ask the innkeeper. The innkeeper replied, "There was a scholar named Sin Yu from the capital who traveled to Sichuan and died suddenly here. The prefect ordered his burial two miles south of the inn, and this is the funeral pavilion on the left side of the road." Shen Sheng paid his respects, bowed, wept, and then left.

○Xunyang Li Sheng Li Sheng, during the Zhenyuan era, failed the imperial examination for the jinshi degree and returned to Xunyang, passing through Shangluo along the way. Coinciding with the Southern Han envoy's audience with the emperor, he was pursued by the official's horsemen. Looking around, he saw only countless green mountains and did not know where to go. It was evening, and his horse was poor, and he had no servants or attendants. Seeing the deep thorns, he noticed a burial place nearby and hid there. After the envoy had passed, he was about to continue his journey, but he did not know the distance or direction of the road. He sighed and said, "That I should find refuge here, is it not fate?" He then stopped at the burial place. First, he bowed and prayed, "I am from Mount Lu, returning south after failing the examination. I have been forced to stop here by the advance guard of the governor. I cannot proceed nor retreat, so I have come here. If your spirit is aware, I wish for a night's rest." Afterward, he leisurely gazed around, and at that time, the wind and moon were clear and bright. The surrounding countryside for several miles could be clearly seen. There was another burial place a hundred steps away. He saw a person approaching, who turned out to be a woman adorned with fine and beautiful decorations, short, not reaching a full foot in length. She went to the south of the burial place and entered a cave. Li Sheng listened, and he heard her say, "Lady Jinhua conveys to Lady Cui: The wind and moon are beautiful tonight, and we can enjoy the view. If we can meet again, I hope you will remember me." The woman in the cave responded, "There is a distinguished guest staying in my house, and I cannot leave. Missing a night of joy is not very significant." She then left and returned to the burial place. The next day, Li Sheng went to an inn and asked about the woman. Someone told him that she was a daughter of the Boqing Cui family, who followed her father to serve as a military officer in Jiangnan and had died there, and was hastily buried. Li Sheng was moved and offered a sacrifice with wine and food before leaving.

Lu Qiao In the early years of the Yuanhe reign, there was a scholar named Lu Qiao, who was fond of composing poetry and was widely praised for it. He lived in Danyang, where his residence featured a terrace and pond, known as a scenic place. Lu Qiao was wealthy and enjoyed entertaining guests. One evening, on a clear and moonlit night, someone knocked on his door. Upon opening it, he saw a man with impressive attire and a graceful, refined appearance. Lu Qiao invited him in and engaged in conversation, which was clear and smooth, surpassing his expectations. He held the man in high regard, thinking no one could match him. He then asked for his name and surname, to which the man replied, "I am Shen Yue. I heard you are good at poetry, so I came to visit." Lu Qiao was surprised and said, "I am just a lowly person, never expecting to be visited by you. I wish to keep you for a short while to enjoy your company." Afterward, he offered wine, but Shen Yue politely declined, saying, "I have never drunk alcohol in my life, but it is not because I am refusing you." He then mentioned his friend Fan Puye, a high-ranking official during the Liang Dynasty, asking if Lu Qiao knew him. Lu Qiao responded that he had read the *History of the Liang Dynasty* and was familiar with Fan Puye's name.

约曰：“吾将邀之。”乔曰：“幸甚。”约乃命侍者邀范仆射。顷之，云至，乔即拜，延坐，云谓约曰：“休文安得而至是耶！”约曰：“吾慕主人能诗，且好宾客，步月至此，遂相谈谑久之。”约呼左右曰：“往召青箱来。”俄有一儿至，年可十岁余，风貌明秀。约指谓乔曰：“此吾爱子也。少聪敏，好读书。吾甚怜之，因以青箱名焉，欲使传吾学也。不幸先吾逝，今令谒君。”即命其子拜乔。又曰：“此子亦好为诗，近従吾与仆射同过台城，因命为感旧，援笔立成，甚有可观。”即讽之曰： 约说：“我要邀请他。”乔说：“那太好了。”约于是命令侍者去邀请范仆射。不久，范仆射到了，乔立刻行礼，邀请他坐下。范仆射对约说：“休文怎么会到这里来呢！”约说：“我仰慕主人能写诗，而且喜欢宾客，所以步行月下到这里来，于是我们聊了很久，谈笑风生。”约叫来左右的人说：“去把青箱叫来。”一会儿，有一个孩子来了，大约十岁左右，相貌俊秀。约指着孩子对乔说：“这是我的爱子。他小时候聪明，喜欢读书。我非常疼爱他，因此给他取名青箱，希望他能传承我的学问。不幸的是，他在我之前去世了，现在让他来拜见您。”随即命令他的儿子向乔行礼。又说：“这孩子也喜欢写诗，最近我陪着他和仆射一起经过台城，于是让他写了一首《感旧》，他提笔立成，非常出色。”随即吟诵道：

The ancient rivers and landscapes of the Six Dynasties, through which dynasties rose and fell over several hundred years, Now lie in desolation, where once bustling markets and streets echoed with life. Under the moonlight, the water shimmers like glass, and the sky in spring is a pale, hazy hue. Lamenting the present and yearning for the past, I shed tears at the gate of the nation. Qiao admired these lines for a long time. He then asked Yao, saying: "I often read the *Wenxuan* compiled by Zhaoming, and saw that the poems it collected were not bound by rhyme or meter, and were called the 'Qi-Liang style.' It was not until the Tang dynasty, with Shen Quanqi and Song Zhiwen, that regulated verse became popular. Why do the poems of the modern era follow this new style?" Yao replied: "If we write today, we naturally follow the modern style. What is there to be surprised about?" Yun then said to Yao: "In the past, I and you, along with Xuanhui and Yan Sheng, all frequented the literary circle of Jingling. We spent our days and nights laughing and playing games, and those times of joy cannot be recaptured. When Emperor Xiao of Sui peacefully took over the throne, you and I both became loyal ministers of the new dynasty. Though our positions were high and the emperor's favor was even greater, our hearts were often filled with anxiety, and we no longer had the joy of those past days."

Zhuge Changmin once said: "Those in poverty often aspire to wealth, but those in wealth often face crises." Is this saying not true?" Yue also sighed deeply for a long time. He then said, "From the Liang Dynasty to the present, it has been four hundred years. The landscape and moonlight are no different from those of the past, but the people have changed silently. How could one not feel sad?" He then said to Yun, "I served as the literary secretary of Cai Gong in Jingzhou. I often dreamed of a person who told me, 'My master will eventually reach the highest position, but will not surpass the Three Excellencies.' When I became the Grand Secretary and Minister of the Central Secretariat, those who discuss it mostly agree with this, but I ultimately did not achieve it. Thus, I understand that human affairs are not without destiny." It was already late at night, and Yun said to Yue, "It is time to go home." They then parted ways and told Qiao, "There will be a military uprising here, but it will not last more than two years." Qiao saw them off to the door, and before they had taken a few steps, they both disappeared. Qiao told his friends and relatives about this. A year or so later, Li Qi rebelled. Another year passed, and Qiao died.

During the Yuanhe period, there was a man named Guo Zhu who was appointed as the magistrate of Wuchang, Ezhou. He was close friends with Liu Zhiqian from Pei State, and they often lamented that the living and the dead could not communicate. They made a pact that whoever died first would come to inform the other. After Liu Zhiqian passed away several months later, Guo Zhu was living in Huayin. One night, while he was alone, he heard someone outside sighing and weeping. After a while, the voice said, “Have you heard that Guo Jun is still well?” Guo Zhu recognized the voice as Liu Zhiqian’s. He said, “Can we meet?” The voice replied, “Please extinguish the candle, and I will talk with you.” Guo Zhu immediately blew out the candle and pulled the sleeve of the figure, who entered with him. They lay down together on the same bed and recounted their past in great detail. The figure also spoke about the afterlife, where good and bad deeds are clearly judged and cannot be deceived. As the night progressed, Guo Zhu suddenly noticed a foul odor emanating from the figure. The stench became unbearable, and he reached out to touch the figure, only to find that it was much larger than Liu Zhiqian. Realizing that this was a different spirit, Guo Zhu, who was strong, grabbed the figure’s sleeve and pressed his body against it, holding it firmly in place. He covered his nose and lay down. The figure then said it was time to leave, and Guo Zhu pretended to talk to it, trying to keep it there. As dawn approached, the figure became increasingly insistent on leaving, saying, “Dawn is approaching. If you do not let me go, disaster will befall you.” Guo Zhu did not respond. Soon, the figure stopped speaking altogether. Just as the sun rose, a foreigner appeared, over seven feet tall, looking as if he had been dead for several days. It was summer, and the foul smell was unbearable. Guo Zhu ordered the body to be taken outside the city. Suddenly, several villagers saw the figure and rushed over. They were shocked to see that it was their brother, who had been missing for several days. They took the body away.

### Translation of the Source Text --- **Original Text:** ○利俗坊民 长庆初，洛阳利俗坊有民行车数辆，将出长夏门。有一人负布囊，求寄囊于车中，且戒勿妄开。因返入利俗坊，才入，有哭声。受寄者因发囊视之。其口结以生绠，内有一物，其状如牛胞，及黑绳长数尺。民惊，遽敛结之。有顷，其人亦复，曰：“我足痛，欲憩君车中行数里，可乎？”民知其异，乃许之。其人登车，览囊不悦，顾谓民曰：“君何无信？”民谢之。又曰：“我非人，冥司俾予录五百人，明历陕、虢、晋、绛，及至此，人多虫，唯得二十五人耳。今须往徐泗。”又曰：“君晓予言‘虫’乎患赤疮即虫耳。”车行二里，遂辞：“有程，不可久留。君有寿，不复忧矣。”忽负囊下车，失所在。某年夏，诸州人多患赤疮，亦有死者。 **Translation:** The People of Lisu Fang In the early years of the Changqing era, there were several vehicles traveling through the Lisu Fang district of Luoyang, preparing to leave through the Changxia Gate. A man carrying a cloth bag asked to store it in one of the vehicles, warning the others not to open it. After returning to the Lisu Fang district, he heard crying upon entering. The person who had stored the bag then opened it to look inside. The mouth of the bag was tied with a rope, and inside was an object that resembled a cow's bladder and a black rope several feet long. The people were startled and quickly tied the bag shut again. Shortly after, the man returned and said, "My feet hurt; may I rest in your vehicle for a few miles, if that is acceptable?" The people, recognizing something unusual, agreed. The man climbed into the vehicle, looked at the bag unhappily, and turned to the people, saying, "Why are you not trustworthy?" The people apologized. He then said, "I am not a human being; the underworld has ordered me to record the names of five hundred people, passing through Shan, Guo, Jin, and Jiang, and up to this point, many people have been afflicted with insects, but I have only managed to record twenty-five. Now I must go to Xu and Si." He added, "Do you understand what I mean by 'insects'? It refers to the red sores." After the vehicle had traveled two miles, he bid farewell, saying, "I have a schedule to keep and cannot stay long. You will live a long life and no longer need to worry." Suddenly, he carried the bag off the vehicle and disappeared from sight. In a certain summer, many people in various states suffered from red sores, and some even died.

○太原部将 During the Longqing era, Pei Du served as the governor of the northern region. There was a Surname Zhao, a military officer, who was suffering from a severe fever. His son was boiling medicine in the room. After placing the medicine in the pot and lighting the fire, Zhao saw a man in yellow robes enter through the door and stop beside the medicine pot. He carried a bag containing medicinal powder, which was as clean as wheat flour. The man then added the powder to the pot and left. Zhao informed his son, who said, "Isn't this a ghost trying to worsen my father's illness?" They discarded the medicine. Zhao then saw the same yellow-robed man return and add more medicinal powder to the pot. Zhao was displeased and ordered the medicine to be thrown away. On another day, Zhao was napping during the day, and his son again boiled the medicine. When the medicine was ready, Zhao woke up and drank it. A few days later, Zhao indeed passed away. ○成公逵 When Li Gong Yan was stationed in the northern capital, there was a military officer named Cheng Shao Yi. His son, Gong Kui, often dreamed of a man in white robes who said, "The underworld has ordered me to summon you." Gong Kui refused. The messenger said, "The officials of the underworld have been sent to summon a person born in the year of the dragon. Since you are a dragon, how can you escape?" Gong Kui replied deceitfully, "I am not a dragon, why would you slander me?" The messenger seemed to understand and said, "Now we will send you back. We will summon the person born in the year of the dragon." Gong Kui woke up in shock. He then told his father, Cheng Shao Yi, about his dream. Cheng Shao Yi had over ten soldiers under his command. The next day, one of his soldiers died suddenly without any apparent illness. Cheng Shao Yi asked about the soldier's birth year, and the soldier's father said, "He was born in the year of the dragon." This confirmed Gong Kui's dream.

**Translation:** **Dong Guan (Part One)** Dong Guan was a man from Taiyuan, skilled in the art of divination and observing omens. During the Yuanhe period of the Tang Dynasty, he traveled with a monk named Ling Xi to the Wu and Chu regions. Ling Xi died on the journey, and Dong Guan returned to Bingzhou. During the Baoli period, Dong Guan traveled to the areas around the Fen and Jing rivers and arrived at Niyang County, where he met at Longxing Temple. The temple's architecture was grand and beautiful, and it housed thousands of scriptures. Dong Guan decided to stay and read them all before returning. Previously, a room in the east wing of the temple, located in the north, was empty and locked. Dong Guan requested to live there, but the monks refused, saying, "Those who live in this room often fall ill or die. Moreover, there are many strange and supernatural occurrences." Despite this, Dong Guan, a young man who relied on his strength, said, "I wish to have it." He then occupied the room. After more than ten days, at night when he slept, he would often see ten or more foreigners bringing music and wine, singing and laughing within the room as if it were empty. This continued for several nights. Although Dong Guan was afraid, he still did not tell the monks about his experiences. One day, after finishing his studies, it was already dusk, and Dong Guan was very tired, so he closed the door and went to sleep. He had not yet fallen asleep when he suddenly saw Ling Xi standing by the bed, saying to him, "The master has left." Dong Guan was both surprised and angry, saying, "The master is a ghost. Why have you come?" Ling Xi smiled and said, "Your destiny is exhausted and your fate is over, so I am able to meet you." He then led Dong Guan away from the bed. Dong Guan looked back and saw that his body was still lying down, as if he were sleeping soundly.

The speaker sighed and said, "Alas! My family is far away, and my parents are still alive. Now, if I were to die here, who would protect my body?" Xue replied, "Why are your words so mistaken and why are you so deeply worried? What makes us human is our ability to move our limbs and perceive the world through our senses. It is the spirit that sustains us, not a natural state. When the spirit departs from the body, that is called death. Thus, the limbs cannot act, the senses cannot function, and even a body six feet tall is of no use. Should you be concerned about such a thing?" Guan Xie listened to this and asked Xue, "I have often heard that according to our teachings, there is an intermediate state between life and death. Who experiences this state?" Xue said, "I and you consider death as a state without rebirth." They then set off together. Their path was unobstructed even by the most severe barriers. They emerged from the western part of Niyang City. The land was covered with lush, dense grass that was a mixture of red and green, resembling a soft, colorful carpet. After traveling more than ten miles, they came across a river that was not very wide and flowed southwest.

Guan asked Xi, who replied, "This is what people commonly call the River of Oblivion. Its source comes from the Underworld, does it not?" Guan looked at the water, which was all blood, and its foul smell made it unbearable to approach. He also saw hundreds of people on the shore, dressed in various garments, and Xi said, "These are the clothes of the deceased. They are following the path to the Underworld." Looking west of the river, Guan saw two cities, stretching for more than one li north to south, overgrown with grass and trees, and houses closely connected. Xi said to Guan, "Let us go there together. You will be born into the Xu family in the southern city as their second son; I will be born into the Hou family in the northern city as their eldest son. After ten years, we will again leave our homes and return to the Buddhist path." Guan said, "I have heard that when a person dies, they should be pursued by officials of the Underworld, who register their sins and merits. As long as one's life has not been marred by major faults, they can be reborn into the human world. But now that I have not even finished dying, how could I be like this?" Xi replied, "Not so."

The Underworld is no different from the world of the living. If one does not act unrighteously, why should shackles ever reach one's body?" Having spoken, Xixi immediately led the observer by the hand and leaped across. As the observer was about to descend from the bank, the water suddenly opened up, revealing a space over ten feet wide. The observer was startled and confused. Suddenly, someone pulled at the observer, who looked back and saw a person covered in fur, resembling a lion in appearance but with a human face. After a long while, the person said to the observer, "Master, where are you going?" The observer replied, "To the southern city here." The person said, "I command you to read the *Dazangjing*. You should return quickly, and you should not stay long." Then he held the observer's arm and hurriedly pointed east and west towards the city, guiding him back. Not having reached a few miles, the observer saw another person, resembling the one who had called him before. The person shouted loudly, "You can take him away. Will there be no record?" Soon after, they arrived at the temple. By then, the sky had already dawned. The observer saw his living quarters, with dozens of monks surrounding the door. He looked down and saw himself on the bed. Two people pushed the observer inside, and suddenly water poured over his body, and he woke up. The temple monk said that the observer had died that night. Then the observer recounted everything to the monk. After several days, in the temple, he saw two clay figures serving as attendants, which were the ones he had seen before. The observer then vowed to think deeply and stayed to read the *Dazangjing*, persevering even through cold and heat. After several years, he returned. It was the 15th day of the fifth month in the second year of Baoli. During the Huichang era, an imperial edict abolished all Buddhist temples, and the observer was also dismissed. Later, he went to Chang'an, traveling among the officials to predict auspicious signs, and his predictions were often accurate. He often served as the magistrate of Linyi County in Yizhou. I was in the capital, and I heard this story from the observer.

A Tale of Wu Ren Sheng Wu Ren Sheng, a man from Wu Prefecture, was skilled in seeing ghosts and lived in a thatched hut on Dongting Mountain. He always looked like a child, and the customs of Wu and Chu could not determine his age. During the Bao Li era, a former magistrate from Kunshan named Yang, who had settled in Wu Prefecture, was traveling with a few others by boat one day to visit Tiger Hill Temple. At the time, Wu Ren Sheng was on the boat, and they discussed matters concerning ghosts and spirits. Yang said, "Humans and ghosts have different paths, so ghosts cannot be seen." Wu Ren Sheng smiled and said, "There are many ghosts; it's just that people cannot recognize them. I alone can." Then he glanced at a woman dressed in green, holding a small child, walking along the shore. He pointed and said, "That is a ghost. The one she is holding is the living soul of an infant." Yang asked, "Then how can you tell it's a ghost?" Wu Ren Sheng replied, "Just watch me talk to her." He then shouted loudly, "You, you are a ghost, stealing the child of the living?" The woman heard and was frightened, quickly retreated, took a few steps, and suddenly disappeared. Yang was both amazed and astonished. By evening, they returned, several miles from the city, where a family had set up a feast by the riverbank. A female witch was dancing and drumming to her left, performing an offering to the gods. Yang and Wu Ren Sheng asked her about it. The witch said, "Today, someone in the village had a baby die suddenly, and now the baby has revived, so we are setting up a feast to express our gratitude." She then ordered the baby to be brought out to see, and it was indeed the true baby held by the woman. The guests were amazed, and they thanked Wu Ren Sheng, saying, "You are truly a master of the Dao; I did not know."

Hu Ji, from Anding, lived in Hedong Prefecture and was renowned for his literary talent. In the spring of the seventh year of the Dazhong era (833 CE), he passed the imperial examination to become a Jinshi. At that time, Jia Fu served as the Vice Minister of Rites. Two years later, Emperor Wenzong promoted Jia Fu to the position of Chancellor. In the tenth month of that year, a disturbance erupted in the capital region, and Jia Fu, along with other ministers, fled. An edict was issued to capture them urgently. The influential eunuch Cao Shiliang, who protected the Left Guard, ordered his subordinates to take arms to track them down. The subordinate said to Cao Shiliang, "Hu Ji received favor from Jia Fu, and now he should be hiding at Hu Ji's place. I wish for five hundred brave and strong soldiers to surround his residence and capture him." Cao Shiliang approved his request. Then, the subordinate led troops to Hu Ji's door, called Hu Ji out, and spoke sternly, "Jia Fu is in your house; you should come out immediately. Otherwise, you will share the same punishment as Jia Fu." Hu Ji considered the situation and realized he could not argue logically, so he defiantly refused. The subordinate became angry and took Hu Ji to Cao Shiliang, where he was killed outside the main gate. At that time, Hu Ji's brother Xiang was in Hedong Prefecture. That day, Xiang and his family saw a person, headless, wearing green clothes with bloodstains, who entered through the door and walked into the courtyard. Xiang was greatly frightened, ordered his family to chase him, but he suddenly disappeared. Three days later, news of Hu Ji's death arrived.

Xin Shenyong, a subordinate official (従事) and censor (御史) of the Pinglu region, was assigned to the capital during the winter of the fifth year of the Tahe era (831 AD). He had previously served as the magistrate of Bai Shui County. At that time, there was a hired worker named Liu Wanjin and a household servant named Ziqin who lived in the same room. Ziqin had been ill for several months and was on the verge of death. One day, Liu Wanjin went out, and Ziqin lay down on his bed. Suddenly, a man appeared, dressed in purple, wearing a tall hat and broad sleeves, with a gaunt appearance, a large nose, and a long beard, entering through the door. He approached Ziqin's bed and said, "Rise up, your illness will soon improve." He then helped Ziqin sit against the wall. Before this, there was a food table under the eastern wall with several dishes. The purple-clad man reached into his sleeve, took out a handful of something that looked like green rice grains, and placed about ten grains into the food dish. He told Ziqin, "I am not from the human world; I have been sent to summon Liu Wanjin, who will die after eating. Do not reveal my words, or else you will suffer the same fate." After speaking, he left. That day, Liu Wanjin returned home, looking flushed and breathing heavily, and said, "I am suffering from abdominal heat, which is almost untreatable." He then ate from the dish until it was nearly finished. Ziqin recovered from his illness, and Liu Wanjin indeed died.

○Tang Yanshi Tang Yanshi, from Jinchang, loved reading and lived a reclusive life on Jiuhua Mountain. One evening, after the rain had cleared, Tang Yanshi walked up the mountain under the moonlight. As night deepened, a pack of wolves blocked his path, preventing him from returning home. Fearing greatly, he hid in the deep forest. Soon, a man in white appeared, wearing a silk scarf, with a solitary and handsome appearance, nearly fifty years old. He walked along the stream, reciting poetry calmly, and stood still for a long time before reciting: "Under the moon, the stream murmurs on, Across the fields, wildflowers bloom. Comes and goes, unseen by all, Returns alone, facing the moonlit mountain." Tang Yanshi, who often wrote seven-character poems and was well-regarded by his contemporaries, was amazed by this. He wanted to speak to the man but did not get the chance as the man disappeared. The next day, Tang Yanshi returned home and asked the locals about the man's appearance. Someone recognized him and said, "He was a son of the Wu family, who passed the imperial examination and was skilled in poetry. He died several years ago."

A Tale of Liang Jing During the Kai Cheng period, there was a man named Liang Jing who, on his way from Changsha to take the examination for filial piety and integrity, stopped at an inn in Shangshan. It was the evening of the 15th day of the eighth month, and after a fresh rain, the wind and moonlight were clear and bright. Liang Jing lay down but did not sleep. At midnight, he suddenly saw three men with very ancient attire, all adorned with jade-green robes, walking slowly towards him. They reached the courtyard, where they paused to recite poetry and admire the scenery, accompanied by several attendants. Liang Jing knew they were spirits, but being naturally bold and courageous, he descended the steps to greet them. The three men showed no fear and introduced themselves as Xiao Zhonglang, Wang Bubing, and Zhuge Changshi. They immediately arranged seats in the courtyard and said, "We did not expect to meet you on such a fine night." They then called out to their attendant, "Yushan, bring the wine." When the wine arrived, they passed the cup around the table. Soon after, Wang Bubing said, "With such a beautiful night, and such distinguished guests present, we cannot go without composing some poetry." They began to write verses together, praising the autumn scenery. Wang Bubing started with, "The autumn moon is round like a mirror." Xiao Zhonglang followed with, "The autumn wind is sharp like a knife." Liang Jing added, "The autumn clouds are light as cotton." When it came to Zhuge Changshi, he remained silent for a long time. The other two urged him, "Please act quickly and simply." After pondering for a moment, Zhuge Changshi finally said, "The autumn grass is fine as hair." The other two burst into laughter, saying, "It may be simple, but why so slow?" Zhuge Changshi replied, "This is just Zhonglang's way. He gets stuck on obscure rhymes and delays his quick wit." Soon, Xiao Zhonglang said, "Such a gathering cannot lack wine to enhance the joy." He ordered Yushan to bring Hui Nü over. Yushan left to fetch her.

After a while, a beautiful woman entered, dressed in fine clothes, and approached the guests with a smile, bowing to them. The high-ranking official, Zhuge Changli, playfully addressed the woman, saying, "You've come in response to the Zhonglang's summons; what business do you have with me?" The woman replied, "How can I be sure I'm not here for everyone?" The Bubing officer suggested, "To clarify yourself, there is no better way than to sing a song to accompany the wine of the Changshi." The woman named Huinian stood up and said, "I would like to sing the song 'The Song of the Phoenix Tower.'" She began to sing, her voice clear and melancholic. Jing listened to her without getting tired. After a while, the song ended. The Zhonglang then sang. When his song concluded, he said, "The mountain light is gradually brightening; I wish to compose another piece to prolong our enjoyment." He began, "The tall trees cast long shadows." The Bubing responded, "The quiet fragrance of mountain flowers." He then pointed to the Changshi and said, "The previous rhyme was awkward; indeed, it was a mistake by the Zhonglang. Now, I wish to continue this, to see who is more quick-witted." The Changshi replied, "The distant mountains and sky are clearly visible." Everyone laughed, saying, "Slow and unable to be quick, and also clumsy; is this the quick-witted one?" The Changshi's face could not remain calm. Next, Jing contributed, "The rushing water in the mountains." The Zhonglang generally praised the poem. He then asked Jing, "Aren't you someone who has taken the Jinshi examination?" Jing replied, "I am about to take the Xiaolian examination." The Zhonglang laughed and said, "How can a Xiaolian know about poetry?" Jing, angered, scolded him. The Changshi also stood up with raised sleeves, and the guests were startled and dispersed. They then disappeared, and the cups and plates also vanished. Jing then suffered from illness and became confused, often dreaming of the Zhonglang and the Bubing coming, and greatly disliked it. Later, he went to Chang'an and met a magician named Li, who gave him a charm to ward off ghosts, and it was finally cured.

**Translation:** The Imperial Censor Cui In Guangling, there was an official residence that spanned several hundred steps and was grandly constructed. In the neighborhood, it was said that a ghost inhabited it, and anyone who stayed there died suddenly the same night. It had been locked up for years. There was an imperial censor named Cui, who was stationed in Guangling. Upon arriving, he opened the door and said, "Evil does not arise on its own. If I move in, how could it bring disaster?" He immediately informed the governor and moved in. That night, there was a light rain. Cui ordered his servants to stay in other rooms and slept alone in the main hall. He woke up with a start, and his clothes were completely soaked. He got up and saw that his bed was in the courtyard. He went back to sleep, and before he had eaten, his bed moved again to the courtyard. This happened three times. Cui said, "I thought there were no ghosts in the world, but now I see that there are indeed." He then put on his official cap and robes, poured wine, and made a toast, saying, "I have heard that many who live here die suddenly. Moreover, humans and spirits follow different paths. They should stay in their own place and not harm living people. Even if they appear in form or make sounds, it is because they have grievances and are about to make a complaint, or they might be seeking a meal for a sacrifice. If they appear to people, people will be frightened and die. It is not that the spirits harm them. I am very foolish and have no fear. If they truly have something to complain about, just tell me. I can fulfill your trust, even if it means facing fire and water." He made this toast three times. Soon, he heard a voice from the air saying, "You are a human; I am a ghost."

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Translation: It is not appropriate to disturb people with ghosts; I will instead sincerely inform you. Cui replied, "Just speak." The ghost said, "I am a woman. My three sisters and I, all unmarried, died young, and our parents buried us in the north of the prefectural city for a long time. Later, the prefect built high walls and city houses here, and workers cut down almost all the trees around our graves, then moved us to the northeast corner of this hall. Our restless souls had no place to rest. Unexpectedly, I met an enlightened gentleman tonight, so I can express my grievances. If you, with a benevolent heart, bury me in a coffin and lay me to rest in the wilderness, that would be a great kindness indeed." She then wept and sobbed, and added, "I have been here for ten years. Those who lived here before all wanted to report this matter, but since then, the residents have died in fright. I am just a young woman, and I do not harm anyone." Cui said, "I said before that I would do as such. However, why can't I see you?" The ghost replied, "I am a ghost, how dare I appear to you with my dark and obscure nature? Since you have promised my request, even if I am in a state of darkness, I should still be grateful for your kindness, how could I just leave it at that?" After saying this, she departed. The next day, Cui called the workers, dug up the northeast corner of the hall, and indeed found dry bones. He buried them in the vacant land of Chanzhi Temple. The villagers all worshipped there, calling it the Trio of Girls' Grave. Since then, the place became peaceful.

Poet Cao Tang, renowned for his poetry in his time, had long failed to pass the imperial examinations. He often resided in the garden of a Buddhist temple in Jiangling, where the scenery was very secluded and beautiful. Whenever he came here to enjoy the view and compose poetry, he would write two lines: "Underwater there is a sky, spring is vast and boundless; on earth there is no path, the moon is vast and mysterious." After reciting these lines for a while, he believed that his usual works were not as good as this one. One day, while sitting by the pond, he was immersed in the pleasure of reciting his poem, when suddenly he saw two women dressed in white clothes, very beautiful and graceful, walking slowly and reciting poetry. It was exactly the two lines he had written. Cao Tang, thinking that he had composed these lines only the previous day, wondered how they could have gotten hold of it so quickly. He asked them about it, but they did not respond and left. Within a dozen steps, they were no longer seen. Cao Tang was greatly puzzled and surprised. He had always been on good terms with the temple monk Fahzhou and told him about the incident. Fahzhou was startled and said, "Two days ago, a young man visited me, holding a greenish paper, and showed me this poem. I was just about to mention it." He then showed the poem to Cao Tang, who felt somewhat bewildered. A few days later, Cao Tang died in his room.

Xing Qun, an official in the Ministry of Justice, lived in Luoyang in the second year of the Dazhong era, suffering from a severe illness. He had always been on good terms with Censor Zhu Guan, who had served as an official in Huaihai and was now retired to Yiluo, where he had died of illness, though Xing Qun did not know this. One day, while resting during the day, Xing Qun suddenly heard someone knocking on the door. He ordered someone to look, and saw Zhu Guan riding his horse. Xing Qun immediately invited him in to sit. Previously, Xing Qun had heard that Zhu Guan was ill, and when he saw him come, he was very pleased, saying, "I heard you were ill before, but there's no need to worry." Zhu Guan replied, "I was once ill, but I have recovered now. Your illness is also nothing to worry about; it will be fine in a day or two." They talked and laughed for a while, and then Zhu Guan left. When Zhu Guan came to visit Xing Qun, he had already died.

In the fifth year of the Tai Zhong era, Li Zhong, the Inspector of the Salt and Iron Department at the Yellow River Institute, was dismissed from office and lived in the region east of the Yellow River. He suffered from an illness that worsened day by day, and he lay quietly on his bed. One night, he told his servant, "I will not recover from this illness." He then ordered the door to be locked. Suddenly, he heard a subtle sound in the courtyard. Li Zhong looked up and saw a man wearing red, who was none other than Cai Xingji, the magistrate of the region west of the Yellow River. There was another man, wearing white layered clothing, following him. Li Zhong was familiar with Cai Xingji and was startled, exclaiming, "Here comes Cai the Imperial Censor!" He then invited them to sit down, and both men sat together. Soon, Li Zhong noticed that Cai Xingji's body gradually grew larger, and his hands, feet, mouth, and nose also enlarged. Upon closer inspection, he realized that this was not Cai Xingji himself. Li Zhong was amazed but continued to address him as the Imperial Censor. Li Zhong then felt that he could slightly move his body and leaned against the wall to sit. He asked, "I have been ill for a month now, and it has worsened. Could it be that I will not recover?" The man replied, "Your illness should soon subside." He then pointed to the man in white clothing and said, "He is my youngest brother, skilled in divination."

Then he ordered the divination for Zhong. The white-clad one emerged from the center with a small wooden monkey, placed it on the couch, and then the monkey jumped around left and right, several times before settling. The white-clad one said, "The divination is complete. The illness of the medical officer is indeed not something to worry about, but he will reach sixty-two, and there will also be disaster." Zhong asked, "Shall the imperial physician drink?" The white-clad one replied, "How dare I not drink?" Zhong then ordered wine and placed the cup in front of him. The red-clad one said, "I have my own drinking vessel." He then took out a cup from his clothing, which at first seemed like silver, but when filled, the cup wobbled, and upon closer inspection, it was made of paper. The two of them finished two cups each. As they were leaving, they warned Zhong again, "After you recover, be careful not to drink alcohol, for disaster will follow." Zhong thanked them and promised to comply. After a long time, they left. In the courtyard, there was nothing to be seen. Looking at the outer door, it was locked as before. He also saw that there was wine on the floor in front of the couch, which the two ghosts had drunk. From then on, Zhong recovered from his illness. Later, he drank as he used to. That year, he was demoted to the position of Prefect of Hangzhou.

○Wang Kun In the spring of the fourth year of Da Zhong (850 CE), Wang Kun, a Doctor of the Imperial Academy, was residing in Taiyuan. He had a servant girl named Qingyun who had passed away several years ago. One night, Qingyun suddenly appeared at his bedside. Wang Kun was greatly frightened and asked her about her appearance. Qingyun said, "I have not been among the living for several years now. I often remember my past, and I have not completely forgotten it. Tonight, I am able to visit you, which is a fortunate encounter." Wang Kun was dazed and did not realize that she was a ghost. Qingyun led Wang Kun out of the room. The door was already locked, but she guided him through a gap, and he passed without any hindrance. They walked down the street, strolling under the moonlight for a long time. Wang Kun suddenly felt hungry and told Qingyun. Qingyun said, "Is there anyone in the village who is friendly with you? You can go to their house to find food." Wang Kun had always been close friends with a scholar named Shi Guan, who also lived in the same village. He decided to go to Shi Guan's house. However, when they arrived, the door was locked. Qingyun knocked on the door, but after a while, the gatekeeper opened it and said, "I just heard the knocking, but now I see nothing. What is going on?" He then closed the door. Qingyun knocked again. This happened three times. The gatekeeper became angry and said, "How dare a ghost knock on my door!" He spat and cursed at her. Qingyun explained to Wang Kun, "Shi Guan is already asleep, so we cannot visit him. I suggest you go to another place." At that moment, a minor official from the Imperial Academy, who was also from the same village, was passing by. Wang Kun and Qingyun went to his house. The door was open, and they saw someone pouring water from a container into the street.

Light Cloud said, "You may enter with me." After they entered, they saw the small official dining with several others. Initially, Kun stood in the courtyard, thinking that the small official would certainly descend the steps to greet and bow, but the small official did not show respect. Soon, a servant girl carrying a bowl of soup and bread climbed the steps, and Light Cloud immediately struck the girl's back, causing her to suddenly fall on the steps, spilling the soup and bread. The small official and his wife and servants all stood up,惊呼道：“中了邪！”随即急忙召来巫师。巫师说：“有一人穿着红绶带和银印，站在庭院前。”于是进行了祭祀。Kun and Light Cloud both sat down, and after finishing the meal, they left together. The female witch accompanied them to the door, burning paper money at the side of the door. Light Cloud told Kun, "Sir, you can go with me." Kun followed her out of the neighborhood, heading towards Qixia. They reached a remote area of the wilderness, and saw a tomb. Light Cloud said, "This is where I reside. Sir, you can follow me inside." Kun bowed his head and entered the tomb, which was dark and indistinguishable at the entrance. Suddenly, he woke up in a state of fear, sweating on his back and trembling in his thighs. By then, it was already dawn, and he disliked the dream, not daring to tell anyone about it. That day, he summoned Shi Guan, who sat down, and Shi Guan said, "Last night, a ghost knocked on my door three times. I sent someone to look, but nothing was seen." In the morning, when passing by the small official's house, there were traces of burned paper money. He immediately summoned the small official to inquire about the matter. The small official said, "I was having a meal last night when suddenly a servant girl fell ill. The witch said it was due to a ghost causing trouble. Therefore, we set up a sacrifice in the courtyard and burned paper money here!" Everything matched Kun's dream. Kun became even more frightened and informed his family. That winter, he indeed died.

### Translation ○杨慎矜 During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Yang Shenjing served as the Vice President of the Censorate. One day, as he was about to enter the court, his servant opened the outer gate. After unlocking it, the door was tightly sealed and could not be opened. Yang Shenjing was both surprised and puzzled. By dawn, his attendants saw a night demon standing outside his door, about ten feet tall and extremely unusual in appearance. The night demon stood under the eaves, locking the door with its hands, and had a flaming mouth and lightning eyes, glancing left and right. The attendants, frightened and scattered, fled in all directions. After some time, as more people and carriages filled the street, the night demon turned south and departed. Passersby, seeing this, scattered and fell to the ground in fear. Yang Shenjing heard about this event and was greatly alarmed. A month later, he was falsely accused by Li Linfu and his brothers were all executed. ○江南吴生 There was a man named Wu from Jiangnan who once traveled to Kuaiji and married a woman named Liu as a concubine. Several years later, Wu became the magistrate of Yanmen County and took Liu with him to his new post. Initially, Liu was known for her gentleness and submission. However, after several years, she suddenly became reckless and self-willed, beyond control. Whenever someone went against her will, she would become furious. She would beat her maids and servants, sometimes biting their flesh until it bled, and her anger could not be appeased. Wu began to realize that Liu was violent and domineering, and he started to distance himself from her. One day, Wu and several generals from Yanmen went hunting in the wild, capturing many foxes and rabbits, which they brought back to the kitchen. The next day, when Wu returned, Liu secretly entered the kitchen, took the foxes and rabbits, and ate them all raw. Wu asked where the foxes and rabbits were, and Liu remained silent and dejected.

Wu was enraged and questioned his maid, who replied, "Liu ate it all." Wu began to suspect that Liu was some kind of monster. A few days later, a county official presented a deer, and Wu ordered it brought to the courtyard. Soon after, Wu started to say he would be going far away. After leaving the house, he hid himself and secretly watched. He saw Liu with loose hair and bare arms, her eyes all torn open, and her appearance suddenly changed. Standing in the courtyard, she held the deer with her left hand and pulled from its thigh to eat with her right. Wu was greatly frightened, fell to the ground, and could not get up for a long time. Then he called for several dozen officials and soldiers, armed with weapons, to enter. When Liu saw Wu coming, she removed her outer garments, stood upright in the courtyard, and transformed into a night fork. Her eyes were like electric light, her teeth like spear blades, her muscles and bones twisted, and her body entirely blue. All the officials and soldiers were terrified and dared not approach.
