The night fork looked around, as if afraid of something. After a meal, she suddenly turned east and ran, very fast, and eventually disappeared. ○ Zhu Xian's Daughter There was a pagoda temple in Wuling Commandery, towering hundreds of feet high, overlooking the great river below. Whenever the river waters surged, the pagoda seemed to sway. Thus, no one dared to climb to the top. There was a merchant named Zhu Xian, whose family was extremely wealthy, and he had a daughter who suddenly disappeared without a trace. Her family searched for her, but after a few days, they could not find out where she had gone. One day, after the rain stopped, the townspeople saw someone standing at the top of the pagoda, wearing a patterned robe. They thought it was some kind of monster. Zhu Xian heard about it and went to investigate. Upon seeing her attire, he realized it was very similar to his daughter's. He immediately ordered someone to climb up and retrieve her. Indeed, it was his daughter. Zhu Xian was shocked and questioned her, to which she replied, ...

Once, when I was alone, a nightmarish creature, about 10 feet tall and very eerie, leaped from the roof and entered my room. It told me not to be afraid. I quickly put on my clothes and ran to a pagoda. Afterward, I felt dazed and as if drunk. It took several days for me to gradually recover. Because of my fear, I was even more terrified. The nightmarish creature usually came down from the pagoda in the early morning to get food and drink for me. One day, as the nightmarish creature was leaving, I looked down and saw it walking through the streets, where it encountered a white-robed figure. The nightmarish creature backed away 100 steps and dared not look at the white-robed figure. When it returned at dusk, I asked it, "Why are you afraid of the white-robed figure?" The nightmarish creature replied, "In the past, the white-robed figure has never eaten the meat of cattle, so I cannot approach." I asked why, and the nightmarish creature explained, "Cattle are used for farming and are essential for human life. If people do not eat their meat, they are protected by the gods. Therefore, I cannot approach." I silently reflected, "I am human, and it is sad to associate with creatures of a different kind." The next day, as the nightmarish creature was leaving, I vowed, "I will not eat the meat of cattle." I repeated this vow three times. Then, the nightmarish creature suddenly appeared from the city, came to the pagoda, and spoke to me, "Why have you changed your mind and abandoned me? Now I will never be able to approach you. From now on, we must part ways." After finishing speaking, the nightmarish creature walked east and disappeared without a trace. I was very happy and returned home via the pagoda.

Tchen Yue Shi, from Yingzhou, originally named Huang Shi, lived in a suburban area near Mount Wangwu. He had a concubine named Zhang. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, in the year 806 AD, Yue Shi and Zhang were eating together at night when they suddenly heard a breathing sound behind the candlelight, which was very unusual. Soon after, a hand appeared in front of Yue Shi. The hand was bluish-black in color, with short fingers and long, slender claws. There were yellow hairs connected to the arm, resembling the appearance of someone begging for food. Yue Shi deeply knew that this was strange, and he felt both disgust and fear. After a while, he heard a voice from behind the candlelight say, "I am hungry, so I have come to pay my respects. I wish to have a little meat placed in my hand. Please do not stop me." Yue Shi immediately threw a small piece of meat on the ground, and the hand took it and left. The hand then said, "This meat tastes very delicious." After finishing the meat, the hand appeared again in front of Yue Shi. Yue Shi angrily shouted, "What are you doing here, you demon? Leave quickly, or I will hit you. Will you not regret it?" The hand then retreated, as if it was afraid. A short while later, the hand appeared again in front of Zhang and said to her, "Madam, can you give me a little meat?" Yue Shi told Zhang, "Be careful not to give it to her!" Zhang refused to give any meat.

After a long time, suddenly a face appeared beside the candlelight, and it turned out to be a yaksha. The yaksha had red, unkempt hair, eyes like lightning, and four teeth resembling sharp blades, which was extremely frightening. It struck Zhang with its hand, causing him to fall to the ground immediately and become unconscious. Yue Shi, who had courage and bravery, immediately got up and chased it. The yaksha then fled, not daring to look back. The next day, Yue Shi traced its trail and found traces on the wall. He said, "This creature will come again." Therefore, at night, he stood with a staff by the northeast wall to wait for it. Shortly after a meal's time, the yaksha indeed came. Having jumped over the wall, before its feet touched the ground, Yue Shi immediately struck it with his staff dozens of times. When the yaksha left, Yue Shi looked under the wall with a candle and saw a lot of blood. There was also a piece of skin over a foot long, on the ground, which had fallen off from being struck. From then on, Zhang recovered from his illness. At dusk, he heard someone calling from several miles away, saying, "Why hasn't Chen Huangshi given back my skin?" The calling kept going without stopping. Only about a month later, every night, he often heard the calls. Yue Shi thought he could not prevent it and disliked being called out. So, he moved to a new residence to avoid it. He therefore changed his name to Yue Shi. In the 15th year of Yuanhe, he passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi. In the 2nd year of Huichang, he died as the magistrate of Lantian.

A Girl from the Zheng Family In Tongzhou, there was a recluse named Wang who possessed supernatural abilities. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (841-846), the local governor of Tongzhou, Viceroy Zheng, had a young daughter whom he greatly cherished. She had been ill since childhood, as if her spirit was lacking. Viceroy Zheng then invited Wang to inquire about her condition. Wang said, "This girl is not suffering from an illness, but rather her soul has not returned to her body." Viceroy Zheng asked him to explain further. Wang replied, "A certain magistrate from a certain county, who is the previous incarnation of this girl, should have died several years ago. Due to his lifetime of good deeds, he was protected by the supernatural and thus lived longer than expected. He is now over ninety years old. On the day he passes away, this girl will recover." Viceroy Zheng immediately sent someone to verify this. Indeed, the magistrate was over ninety years old. A month later, the girl suddenly woke up as if from a drunken stupor, and her illness was cured. Viceroy Zheng then sent someone to verify again, and it was confirmed that the magistrate indeed died without illness on the day the girl was cured.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong, a villager from the Lujiang region was gathering firewood in the mountains. As dusk fell, he suddenly saw a tramp over ten feet tall emerge from a mountain ravine, wearing black clothes and holding a bow and arrow. The villager was greatly frightened and rushed to hide behind an ancient tree, peering out to observe. The tramp stood and gazed for a long time, then suddenly shot an arrow eastward. The villager followed his gaze and saw an object a hundred paces away that looked like a person, covered in several inches of yellow fur, wearing a black headscarf, and standing still. The arrow hit its abdomen, but it did not move. The tramp laughed and said, "Indeed, it is beyond my ability." He then left. Another tramp, also over ten feet tall and even more robust than the first, also held a bow and arrow and looked eastward, shooting. The arrow hit the object's chest, but it still did not move. The tramp said, "Only a general can do it." He then left. Soon, dozens of tramps in black clothes, with bows on their arms and arrows in their belts, arrived, acting as vanguard. Then, a giant several dozen feet tall, wearing purple clothing and with an extremely strange appearance, slowly approached. The villager saw him and unconsciously felt a chill. The giant tramp looked eastward and told the vanguard, "Shoot at its throat."

A group of Huns wanted to compete to shoot it, and the great Hun warned, saying, "Only Xiong Shu is suitable." The other Huns all stepped back. One Hun approached, drew the bow fully and shot once, and hit its throat. The creature was also not afraid, and slowly pulled out three arrows with its hand, carrying a large stone and coming from the west. The Huns all showed signs of fear, and approached the great Hun, saying, "The situation is urgent. It would be better to surrender to it." The great Hun immediately commanded and called out, "General wishes to surrender." The creature then threw the stone on the ground, removed its own headscarf, and looked like a woman, without hair. It approached the group of Huns, completely took away all the bows and arrows they were holding, and broke them all. Then it ordered the great Hun to kneel on the ground, and struck his cheeks with his hands repeatedly. The Huns begged in a pitiful manner, calling out the number of dead several times, only then released him. The other Huns stood with their hands raised high, and were afraid to move. The creature slowly covered its head with the scarf, looked east, and left. The Huns congratulated each other, saying, "Thankfully today is the day of Jiazi. Otherwise, we would all be dead!" Afterward, they all knelt before the great Hun, and the great Hun nodded. For a long time, then led them into the mountain pass, it was almost dark. The people returned sweating, finally, they did not know what it was.

○谢翱 谢翱，陈郡人，曾经参加过进士考试，喜欢写七言诗。他先祖曾暂时居住在长安升道里，他家的院子里种了许多牡丹。一天傍晚雨后天晴，谢翱走出家门，向南走了百步，远眺终南山的山峰。他久久站立在那里，忽然看见一匹马从西边飞驰而来，马上的女子身着绣花衣服，近看原来是两个鬟髻，高高的发髻，妆容美丽，容貌非常娇艳。那女子来到谢翱面前，停下马说道：“郎君不是来等我吗？”谢翱回答说：“我只是来散步，顺便看看山景而已。”那女子笑着下马，向谢翱行礼说：“希望郎君能回你的住所。”谢翱感到非常困惑，立刻回头望向自己的家，只见几个穿着青衣的人站在门外，谢翱更加惊讶和疑惑。他走进屋内，那些青衣人都上前向他行礼。进入屋内后，只见厅堂中铺设了坐垫和地毯，悬挂着帷幔，锦绣交相辉映，异香弥漫整个房间。谢翱感到非常惊讶和害怕，不敢多问。

A man stepped forward and said, "Why are you afraid? It won’t harm you." Soon after, a golden carriage arrived at the door. A beautiful woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, with an elegant and graceful appearance, was seen. She had never been seen before. She got out of the carriage and entered the house, meeting with Aow. They sat in the western pavilion, and she said to Aow, "I heard that there are famous flowers here, so I came to drink with you." Aow's fear gradually eased. The beautiful woman then ordered food to be prepared and ate together. All the utensils and objects were precious and abundant. She took out a jade cup, poured wine, and passed it to drink. Aow then asked, "What kind of person are you? Are you not some kind of strange being?" The beautiful woman smiled and did not answer. Aow persisted in asking her, and she replied, "You just need to know that I am not a human; why ask further?" Late at night, she said to Aow, "My home is very far away, I am going back now, and cannot stay here for long! I heard that you are good at writing seven-character poems, I wish to have one as a gift." Aow felt sad and then wrote a poem, saying:

**Translation:** "The reunion on the Yangtai Terrace is uncertain when, amidst deep green trees and dense mist, the jade-drip clock moves slowly. At midnight, a fragrant breeze fills the courtyard under the moonlight, and in front of the flowers, the sorrow of King Chu is unexpectedly evoked." The lady read it, and tears streamed down her face. She said, "I have also learned to write poetry and wish to compose a reply, hoping you will not find fault with it." Aro was pleased and asked her to proceed. The lady requested a red writing sheet, and Aro looked in his box, finding only a green one, so he gave it to her. The lady wrote: "There is no way to express longing, so do not long; flowers bloom in the wind, but only for a moment. Lamenting the place of return in the golden palace, the morning oriole's song breaks the green willow branches." Her writing was very skillful, and Aro admired it for a long time. The lady then looked around and dismissed the curtains, ordered the candle to be lit, and mounted the carriage. Aro saw her off to the door, and they parted with tears. Not even a dozen steps away, the carriage and the horse and rider all vanished from sight. Aro was amazed by the event and stored the lady's poem in his box.

The following spring, having failed the imperial examinations and returned eastward, I arrived in Xin Feng and stayed at an inn at dusk. Walking and gazing at the moon, I reflected on past events and then composed a poem, saying: "A sheet of fine paper like a green cloud, the lingering fragrance and fresh ink remain. Only adding to the sight of sorrowful events, I do not see the ethereal people of the Three Mountains. The slanting moon illuminates tonight's dream, fallen flowers and crying birds from last spring. The red chamber has even more things to grieve over, cobwebs on the window and dust on the mirror." I then recited it aloud. Suddenly, I heard the sound of a carriage coming from the west, very urgent. Soon, I saw a carriage accompanied by several horsemen. Looking at the attendants, they were the two maids from before. Surprised, I asked them, and the two maids quickly went forward to report. The carriage stopped immediately. The attendant said to Aow, "In the main street, I could not help but wish to see you." Aow asked her to stay at the inn, but she firmly refused. He then asked where she was going, and she replied, "I am going to Hongnong." Aow said, "I am also returning to Luoyang today, would you like to go east with me?" She replied, "My journey is very urgent, I cannot." She immediately lifted the carriage curtain and said to Aow, "I am moved by your kind intentions, so I only have a brief meeting." After speaking, she wept uncontrollably. Aow also wept with sadness and recited the poem he had composed. The woman said, "I did not expect you to remember me so well, how generous you are." She then said, "I wish to repay you with another poem." Aow immediately gave her paper and a pen, and in a short while, she finished and said: [The content of the second poem is not provided in the source text, so it is omitted here.]

Melancholy, the beautiful time was but a dream; the spring scenery of the Five Mausoleums has all turned to nothing. To understand parting is especially worth hating, only because news cannot reach each other. Sorrowful expression congeals on the eyebrows, light green in hue; tear stains stain the face, falling like light red. The wheels of the carriage temporarily pause with the noble youth, but tomorrow they will ride westward and then eastward again. Aowei thanked him. After a long time, he bid farewell and departed, only to see nothing after a hundred or so steps. Although Aowei knew it was strange, he could not forget it. By the time he reached Shaanxi, he took the road to Hongnong, where he stayed for several days, hoping to meet again, but there was no sign of him. When he returned to Luoyang, he recited the two poems to his friends. A few months later, bound by resentment, he died.

A monk named Fa Chang from Longmen Temple in Henan, who was from Yuanwu in Zhengzhou. During the Bao Li era, he once returned from Longmen to Yuanwu. His family had several acres of land, and the crops were ripe but not yet harvested. One evening, while riding his horse through the fields, the horse suddenly stopped, even if whipped or struck, it would not move, only staring eastward. At the time, the moon was bright, and following the direction of the horse's gaze, hundreds of steps away, there was an object like the color of an ancient tree, coming abruptly. Fa Chang was frightened and immediately turned his horse to run alongside the path for dozens of steps to observe. The object came closer, and it was white vapor, about six or seven feet tall, very foul-smelling, even more so than a fish market. There was a soft sound like a moan, and it looked westward and then departed. Fa Chang rode his horse to follow behind, always keeping a distance of dozens of steps. After traveling more than a mile, they arrived at the house of a villager named Wang, and the object suddenly entered the house. Fa Chang stopped his horse to wait, and after a while, he suddenly heard the family call out, "There is a cow dying under the carriage, come and see." Soon after, they heard the sound of a donkey falling to the ground, unable to be saved. Then, they heard cries of alarm, and someone came out. Fa Chang pretended to pass by and asked what happened. They said, "The master has a ten-year-old son who suddenly died." Before the sentence was finished, they heard more cries of alarm and exclamations, continuing without end. After midnight, the sounds gradually diminished, and by dawn, they had stopped. Fa Chang was greatly surprised and reported the incident to his neighbors, who came together to investigate the Wang family's house. Inside, everything was silent, and when they opened the door, they found that the Wang family of over ten people had all died, and there were no chickens or dogs left.

**Translation:** **Zhen Sheng** According to folk belief, after a few days of death, a bird will emerge from the coffin, and this bird is called "kill" (殺). During the Daxi period, there was a man named Zheng Sheng who was hunting with a local official in Xichuan. They caught a large bird with a grayish color, about five feet tall. The commander ordered it to be released and examined, but suddenly there was nothing to be seen. Zheng Sheng was surprised and immediately went to ask the villagers. One villager replied, "There was someone in our village who had died several days ago. The diviner said that today the ‘kill’ would leave. The family watched for it and saw a large bird with a grayish color emerge from the coffin. Is what you captured indeed that bird?" Zheng Sheng was amazed and returned. During the Tianbao period, the Prefect of Jingzhao, Cui Guangyuan, often encountered a strange bird while traveling, and the incident was similar to this.

The Old Man of Qingjiang Prefecture During the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, an old man from Qingjiang Prefecture often herded cattle in the fields south of the prefecture. One day, he suddenly heard a strange sound coming from the ground. The old man and several herding boys all fled in fright. After this incident, the old man suffered from a severe fever. After only ten days, his illness somewhat improved, and he dreamed of a man wearing a green robe. The man turned and said to the old man, "Move me to the Kaiyuan Temple." The old man was startled awake but did not understand the meaning. A few days later, he happened to hear the same sound again in the countryside. He immediately reported this matter to Prefect Feng. Prefect Feng said angrily, "Is this not foolish and delusional?" and scolded and sent him away. That night, the old man had another dream in which the man in the green robe told him, "I have been buried underground for a long time. You must quickly dig me up, or else you will fall ill." The old man was greatly frightened. By morning, he went with his son to the southern part of the prefecture, examined the area, which was about a zhang wide, and found a bell, which was green in color, just as the man in the dream had been dressed. He then reported this again to Prefect Feng, who placed the bell in the Kaiyuan Temple. On that day at the third watch, the bell suddenly rang without being struck, and the sound was extremely loud and resonant. The people of Qingjiang all gathered and exclaimed in amazement. Prefect Feng reported this event to Emperor Xuanzong, who ordered Chancellor Linfu to write down the design of the bell and announce it throughout the empire.

### Final Translation ○东莱客 In Donglai County, there was a pavilion where strange occurrences frequently happened in the western pavilion. Guests who slept below it often heard faint barking at night. When they looked alone, they saw nothing. This continued for many years. Later, the county magistrate ordered the pavilion to be locked and secured as a storeroom. One night, when the moon was bright, a storekeeper saw a very small gray dog circling the courtyard and running. The storekeeper found it strange that it was different from ordinary dogs and threw a stone to hit it. The dog barked and ran away, entering the western pavilion. The next day, the storekeeper reported the incident to the county magistrate, who ordered an investigation in the western pavilion. They found many grayish hairs on the door, which matched the color of the gray dog the storekeeper had seen, and everyone finally understood. ○交城里人 Several miles south of Jiaocheng County, strange phenomena often appeared to people at night, causing many to become terrified, fall ill, and even die. The villagers had been troubled by this for a long time. Later, a villager walking with a bow and arrow at night in the southern part of the county saw a tall and large figure like a giant, wearing red clothing and a silk scarf covering his head, walking slowly and leaning as if very drunk. The villager was afraid and immediately drew back and shot, hitting the figure. The strange phenomenon then retreated. The villagers' fear somewhat eased, and the villager walked north to an inn, telling the story. The next day, he reached the county town and saw the red osmanthus tree west of the city wall, with an arrow piercing through it, which indeed belonged to the villager. He took the arrow back, and the arrowhead had a lot of blood. He reported this to the county magistrate, who ordered it to be burned. From then on, there was no more trouble in the southern part of the county.

**Translation:** **Fengli** During the Yuanhe era, there was a man named Cui Fengli from Boling, who came from Ruyang and Zheng and lived temporarily in the Yinfu Lane of Chang'an. One day, while reading under the window, he suddenly saw a child, no more than a foot tall, bareheaded and wearing yellow clothes, who came running from the northern wall and approached his bed. The child said to Fengli, "May I entrust myself to your study? Is that acceptable?" Fengli did not respond. The child then said, "I am still strong, and I wish to be at your service. Why do you reject me so strongly?" Fengli still did not pay attention. The child then climbed onto the bed, stood up gracefully for a long time, and took out a small piece of writing from his sleeve and presented it to Fengli. It was a poem, with tiny letters like millet, clearly distinguishable. The poem read: "Once I benefited from the kindness of Meng Tian, Later I was thrown away by my brothers. If you do not give me tasks, where can I find a silver hook?" After reading it, Fengli laughed and said, "Since you have agreed to follow, will you not regret it?" The child then took out another poem and placed it on the table, which read:

“Knowledge is inherited from you, but poetry and books are passed down through my own efforts. You must know Wang Xizhi, whose fame and value have endured for a thousand years.” He then said, “I lack the skill of Wang Xizhi, so even if I get you, what good will it do?” Soon after, he threw another piece of writing:知 “It can enable communication over great distances, and it can express complex ideas. I am sad that Jiang Sheng does not appreciate my good work, but I take pride in my writing.” He playfully said, “I regret that you are not colorful.” His servant laughed and got off the bed, then quickly moved to the northern wall and entered a hole. He immediately ordered his servant to uncover the hole and found a tube of writing brush. He then took the brush, which was as sharp as new, and used it for over a month without any other strange occurrences.知 ○张秀才 In the Taohua neighborhood of Dongdu, there was an empty house. During the Dazhong era, a scholar named Zhang borrowed it to study. He often felt restless and uneasy, thinking that as a man, he should have a bold and generous spirit, and should not be timid and weak. He then moved into the central hall to stay. Late at night, leaning on his pillow, he saw fifteen Taoist priests and Buddhist monks each emerge from the hall. They all had similar appearances, arranged in six rows, with dignified and respectable demeanor. The scholar thought they were a gathering of immortals and did not dare to breathe a sound, so he pretended to sleep and watched them. After a while, two other objects were moving on the ground, each with twenty-one eyes, four of which were bright like fire, chasing each other, and their eyes were spinning, making a sharp cracking sound.

In the meantime, thirty monks and Taoist priests were seen moving chaotically, some running, some walking, some going east, some west, some south, and some north. A single Taoist priest stood alone in one place, only to be struck and removed by a monk. The two objects wandered among the monks and Taoists, never taking a moment's rest. In this way, they competed to strike and gather, sometimes separating, sometimes coming together. Suddenly, one person shouted, "Outstanding!" After speaking, the monks and Taoists all fell silent. Then, the two objects spoke to each other, saying, "Earlier, the group of monks and Taoist priests had extremely high and wonderful methods, but they all depended on us two to complete their teachings. Otherwise, how could they be called outstanding?" The scholar then realized that they must be ghosts or spirits, and threw a pillow at them. The thirty monks and Taoists, along with the two objects, fled in fright at once, saying, "Do not delay in leaving, we are almost controlled by a scholar." And so they all disappeared. The next day, upon searching, in the corner of the wall, they found a tattered bag containing thirty longhanging dice and a pair of dice.

During the reign of Emperor Wenzong in the Kai Cheng era, there was an official in Hedong Prefecture who often patrolled the streets at midnight. One night, under clear skies and a bright moon, he reached the front of Jingfu Temple. There, he saw a person sitting cross-legged, arms hugging the knees, with an entirely black body, completely motionless. Frightened, the official scolded the person, but the figure pulled and did not look back. After scolding for a long time, the official struck its head. Suddenly raising his gaze, he saw that the figure had an extremely strange appearance, several feet tall, pale and thin, and its appearance was very frightening. The official was initially so frightened that he fell to the ground, but after a while, he gradually managed to stand up and looked around, but the figure had already disappeared. From then on, the official was even more terrified and hurried back home to tell others about his experience. Later, when the gate of Jingfu Temple was rebuilt and the ground was excavated, a lacquered bucket was found, deep several feet, with white clay sealing its head. Indeed, it matched what the street official had seen.

During the late Jianzhong era, there was a man named Du Gu Yan who once traveled as a guest in the region between the Huai and Si rivers. One day, a sudden strong wind made it impossible for his boat to proceed, so he anchored it at the shore. Under the moonlight, he walked to the shore and arrived at a Buddhist temple. All the monks had gone to a village gathering, so Yan walked around the courtyard. Soon, two men came. One man was very tall and wore black clothes, calling himself Jia Qinjie, the fifth generation. The other man was broad but short, wearing green clothes, calling himself Zeng Yuan. They bowed to Yan and spoke with him. Their discourse was profound and subtle, beyond the level of ordinary people. Yan, who always sought out strange and profound things, had often discussed with reclusive scholars for many years. As for the esoteric teachings of Daoism and Buddhism, nothing escaped their understanding of the essence. When he met these two men, he felt that he could not surpass them. He secretly admired them and intended to learn from them. Therefore, he bowed twice and said, "I am someone who seeks out the strange. Today I have the good fortune to meet you, sir. I wish to become your disciple. Is it possible?" The two men replied, "How dare we?"

The man inquired about the origin of his lineage. The black-clothed one said, "My ancestors originally belonged to the Lu family. When I was young, I was known for my firmness and strength. Generally, anything that is stuck or not flowing must be provoked to be enlightened, and at the time, everyone called me invasive and accusatory. Because of this, people named me. Later, when I went to the wilderness, I met an enemy who broke me. I then changed my surname to Jia and escaped from my suffering. Additionally, I have always been skilled in medical arts and once held the position of a doctor. It is not that I could not become highly skilled, but when it comes to ascending and descending, I rely on others. Due to old age and weakening strength, the ruler wanted to appoint me as a low-ranking official, but I firmly declined the offer and retired to the countryside. I have an uncle who was my colleague, and his actions and daily routines were never different from mine. But since I was discarded, I often think of my uncle. It is because our usage and placement were different, leading to our separation. Now that the gentleman is asking me, I can talk about my life story, how fortunate I am!"

After the conversation, Zeng Yuan said: "My ancestors are descendants of the Taotang dynasty. Among those who were granted the surname Yao-Zeng by the officials of Taotang, my family adopted the character '曾' as our surname, hence we are the Zeng clan. I am a descendant of this lineage. I once served under Lord Lai, holding an important position, which required me to handle critical matters. I was naturally narrow-minded and impulsive, and I often acted out of pride or anger against my superiors. As a result, I faced slander and criticism from the lower classes, and eventually resigned from my post. This was due to my sense of loyalty and bravery, which led to my downfall. I have since been living a humble life, almost forgotten, and have been in this state for many years. I am content to be like broken tiles and rubble, and I do not have high aspirations. However, when I recall the events of the past with my father, I remember that he had a strong and upright character. He was willing to face even the most severe punishments, such as being boiled in a cauldron, and he always helped others in times of need, even going through fire and water. People respected him for this. Now, I am confined to my old position, feeling trapped like a prisoner. Because of my father's neglect, I have distanced myself from him, and this has been the case for several years. If you have any questions, how could I remain silent?"

As the words were still uncompleted, the temple monks all returned, and the two people saw them, as if they were afraid of something, and immediately fled away. After dozens of steps, they were no longer seen. Yan questioned the monks, and they said, "We have lived in this temple for a long time, and we have never seen them before. We are afraid they might be strange." Yan found their talent extraordinary and found it strange. Therefore, he asked for their names. After a long while, he understood and said, "So-called Zeng Yuan, isn't it the steamer? The character '文' with '瓦' attached to '曾', is the character for 'steamer'. The name 'Yuan' is because the picture inside the '瓦' is placed above the '瓦' character, which conveys its meaning. The person named Jia Qinjie, isn't it the iron pestle? And '午' wood is the character for 'pestle'. The surname 'Jia' is the wood of the east, 'Jia' and 'Yi'. The fifth, also uses the '午' character. By this reasoning, it is the 'pestle' character. The name 'Qinjie' is because the reverse of the word is 'gold cut'. By attaching 'cut' to 'gold', it is the character for 'iron'. In summary, isn't it the steamer and the iron pestle?" The next day, he ordered to investigate their traces. Indeed, in the decayed remnants, he found an iron pestle, and another steamer that was split in half, which was the remaining part used. Yan was greatly surprised, and everything matched his interpretation.

A Scholar-Official Named Lu Yu A scholar-official named Lu Yu, originally from the northern region, moved his family to Chang'an. He once traveled north to the states of Yan and Zhao and then stayed as a guest in Neihuang. The local governor housed Lu Yu in his official residence. Previously, the house had no occupants. When Lu Yu arrived, he saw an old woman with completely white hair, short and fat, wearing a plain white robe. She greeted him, saying, "I have been living here for a long time, so I came to greet you." Afterward, she left. That night, Lu Yu was alone in front of the hall, and it was cold with wind and snow. The old woman came again and said to Lu Yu, "How can you, a distinguished guest, entertain yourself alone?" She invited him to sit and talk. The old woman said, "I am surnamed Shi, and I come from Huayin County. I followed a certain Censor Wang here for nearly forty years. Our family is very poor, so please take pity on me, a distinguished guest." Lu Yu then offered her food, but the old woman did not look back as she left.

Yù asked her, "Why don't you eat?" The old woman replied, "I am very hungry, but I do not eat grain, so I live long and am at peace." Yù, being curious, thought it was very good and believed she had some kind of Daoist art or skill. He then asked, "Since you don't eat grain, how do you fill your stomach? Do you often take elixirs of immortality?" The old woman said, "I live in Huayin. My ancestors loved Daoist immortals, and we lived in a hut on Mount Taihua. I also often hid in the mountains, learning the methods of longevity from a Daoist. The Daoist taught me to swallow fire, and since then, I have abstained from eating. Now I am ninety years old, and I wonder if there will be a day when I get sick from cold or heat." Yù then said, "I once met a sage when I was young, who taught me the technique of breathing exercises. I thought it was wonderful. Later, I pursued fame and profit, traveling to the capital to pay tribute. During the day, I hurried; at night, I rested. I did not expect to meet you tonight. We talked about our lifelong interests."

### Translation How can one not know that swallowing fire is the essence of immortality?" the fairy maiden said. "Have you not heard that a perfected being is not affected by heat or cold? When entering fire, fire cannot burn them; when entering water, water cannot drown them. If so, then swallowing fire is indeed appropriate." Xu said, "May I watch you swallow fire, please?" The fairy maiden replied, "What is there that is not possible?" She then took fire from the furnace and swallowed it. As the fire was nearly gone, her complexion remained unchanged. Xu was both surprised and amazed, so he stood up, fastened his belt, and bowed twice, thanking her, "A rustic person like me has never heard of immortality. Tonight, I met a fairy maiden and witnessed the extraordinary act of swallowing fire, which is truly something I have never experienced before." The fairy maiden said, "This is just a minor trick, what is there to value in it?" After speaking, she announced her departure. Xu then descended the steps to see her off. After parting, Xu returned to his bedroom.

After a while, a servant informed Yu, saying, "There is a fire under the western veranda!" Yu was startled and rushed to look, and indeed, the western veranda was already burning. The people in the neighborhood all arrived and rushed to pour water on it, and it wasn't until dawn that the fire was finally extinguished. Upon investigating the origin of the fire, they found a stone fire starter in a pit under the veranda, with a large amount of firewood inside. There had been piles of rotten grass on top of it, which had caught fire and spread. Yu then realized that the old woman was indeed the source of this fire starter. It was indeed the person known as Mr. Shi, who lived in Mount Hua. Yu then questioned the Prefect of Justice Lu, and an old official from the prefecture told Yu, "Prefect of Justice Lu was an official from Wei, and he has lived in this house for forty years now." Everything matched what the old woman had said. Another stone tiger in the north of Pingling City in Jinan, Qingzhou, one night moved to the east-south side of the Shanzhigou area, and there were traces of over a thousand wolves and foxes following it. The tracks all formed a path.

A Man Named Zhu Jizhen In the region between Chen and Cai, there lived a man named Zhu Jizhen, who had passed away more than ten years ago. Later, a villager named Zhao Zihuo also died, and several days after his death, he suddenly awoke and immediately rushed out of the door. His wife and children were startled and approached him, asking what had happened. Zhao Zihuo said, "I am Zhu Jizhen. How do you know me? I am returning to my home now." However, his voice was no longer that of Zhao Zihuo. His wife and children followed him, and when they arrived at Zhu Jizhen's house, they saw Zhao Zihuo coming and thought he was mad, scolding and driving him away. Zhao Zihuo, now speaking as Zhu Jizhen, said, "I am Zhu Jizhen. I died eleven years ago and have now returned. Why are you rejecting me?" The family listened to his words and realized it was indeed Zhu Jizhen; they verified the circumstances and confirmed it was him. His wife and children were both astonished and bewildered. They questioned him, and Zhu Jizhen explained, "Since I left the mortal world, it has been a full decade. I have been living in the underworld, constantly thinking of returning to see my wife and children, never forgetting for a single day. However, in the underworld, every thirty years, the souls of the deceased are given a chance to be reborn, and their deeds are judged. Last time, I presented my case to the underworld officials and was granted permission to be reborn. The underworld official then asked me, 'Your house has been ruined for a long time. What shall we do?' My case was presented, and it was suggested, 'There is Zhao Zihuo, a villager of the same area, who has just died. He wishes to lend his body to Zhu Jizhen's soul.' The underworld official agreed and sent someone to escort me to Zhao Zihuo's house, which is why I have returned." He then recounted past events, and everything he said was clear and understandable. His wife and children finally believed him and accepted him. From then on, Zhu Jizhen stopped eating meat and alcohol, wore coarse and short clothing, and went begging in the regions of Chen, Cai, Ru, and Zheng. Whenever he received money, he used it to support the poor and hungry, and he never returned home, and he is still alive to this day.

During the reign of Emperor Daizong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man from Shan Yang named Qie Huilian, who initially lived in Si. Because his father had once served as an official in the northern regions, he followed him to live in Qing He. After his father's death, Huilian became known for his intense grief and emaciation. The governor sent officials to pay condolences and gave him grain and silk. After completing the mourning period, he was appointed as the magistrate of Zhangnan by imperial edict. One year later, one night he was alone in his hall when he suddenly saw a person dressed in purple and carrying a sword, rushing forward and saying to Huilian, "The Heavenly Emperor has a command, appointing you as the chief of the Life and Death Office to establish Yanbola King with a document." The person then presented a brocade box containing documents to Huilian, saying, "This is the Heavenly Emperor's command." The scroll was adorned with jade and gold, marked with patterned silk. Additionally, he was given ceremonial regalia including an ivory tablet, a purple sash, a golden turtle, and a jade belt. Huilian was both joyful and fearful, very confused and anxious, with no time to ask questions, and thus accepted it. He stood in the front hall when a servant rushed in and said, "The palace officials and guards are about to arrive." Soon after, hundreds of people dressed in embroidered clothes with red foreheads, wearing weapons on both sides, rushed in and arranged themselves in several rows, bowing twice. One person stepped forward and said, "I am fortunate to be your servant, dare I express my gratitude." After finishing his words, he bowed again. After the bow, they stood in front in separate rows. The servant then said, "The commanders of the Five Peaks' guards are here."

Again, there were over a hundred people hurrying in, arranged in five rows, dressed in the colors of the five cardinal directions, all bowing twice. The official said, "Officials responsible for ritual vessels and musical instruments, officials in charge of musical ensembles, officials managing vehicles, carriages, and horses, officials handling seals, documents, and records, officials managing treasury, storage, and food services." Another group of several hundred people hurried in. After a while, the official said, "The guards of the Five Sacred Mountains and the ritual vessels, musical instruments, chariots, and horses, please allow me to personally inspect them." Huilian asked, "Where are the guards of the Five Sacred Mountains?" The official replied, "They have their own place, their own place." Huilian immediately ordered the carriage, and thus a white horse was controlled and arrived, adorned with gold and jade, and the attendants who guided, controlled, and managed the horses were all the same as before, wearing embroidered robes. Several riders preceded along the path on both sides, leading Huilian northeastward. The shouts and calls were very strict. After traveling several miles, there were over ten thousand soldiers, some riding and some walking, all armored in metal and holding spears, lined up on the road. Spears, lances, banners, and flags, all adorned with intricate embroidery and shining brightly.

Soon after, outside the red gate, there were dozens of people, all dressed in green robes holding ceremonial tablets, bowing deeply in respect. They said, "These are the officials." Inside the gate, all the curtains, screens, and furniture were arranged, as if it were the residence of a king. Huilian ascended the steps and sat on the table. Soon, ten officials in green robes arrived, each carrying official documents, requesting Huilian to approve and sign them. The guide then led Huilian to a courtyard on the eastern side of the building. In front of the courtyard, there were many carriages, carts, and horses. There were also musical instruments, seals, and keys, all placed on the table and covered with yellow-patterned cloth. The table was surrounded by the four walls. There was also a jade tablet, inscribed with purple gold in ancient script, intricately shaped like dragons and phoenixes. An official said, "This is the document of Yama, the god of death." A man dressed in ceremonial headgear came to pay respects, and Huilian treated him as an equal. After sitting down, the man said to Huilian, "The supreme deity has recognized the virtue of the Buddhist monk Haiwu from the monastery in Nanyang, Anyang County, Hebei Province, and has created a record for him, which is highly honored by Yama."

Because the official has high moral conduct, I was appointed as the chief of the God of Fate, overseeing the registration and appointment of officials. I am fortunate to be among the attendants, thus I can serve at your side. Huilian asked, "Where does King Yanbola reside?" The official replied, "He is a highly esteemed figure in the underworld, distinguished among mountains and rivers, and oversees the affairs of the underworld. Those without extraordinary conduct are not chosen." Huilian thought, "I am conducting the registration ceremony in the underworld. Isn't this because I have already died?" He also thought of his wife and children, looking unhappy and discontented. The official noticed his intention and said to Huilian, "The official looks worried. Isn't it because you are concerned about your wife and children?" Huilian replied, "Yes." The official said, "The ceremony will take place tomorrow. You can temporarily return to take care of your family. But your position is very high. I hope you do not regret the difference between the living and the dead." After speaking, he stood up. Huilian immediately ordered a carriage to leave, and he felt drowsy, as if drunk. He leaned on the desk and took a nap. When he woke up, he was already in the county, and it was just dawn.

Astonished for a long time, Huilian considered the will of the Heavenly Emperor and concluded that it was inevitable. He immediately informed his wife and children and prepared for his fate. He also informed the county magistrate, but Magistrate Mr. Cao did not believe him. Huilian then bathed, dressed in official robes and hat, and lay on the bed. That night, several county officials all heard a sound like wind and rain in the air, coming from the north and directly entering Huilian's room. After a meal, Huilian died. They heard the sound go away to the north. They were astonished and sent messengers to the south of Neihuang County in Yeh Prefecture to inquire. It turned out that it was Chan Master Haiwu of Lanruo Temple, who had recently passed away. ○ Liu Xian When Shangshu Li Huan was stationed in Pingyang, there was a military officer named Liu Xian, from the He River region. He was of a straightforward and brave nature. One night, he saw a white-clad figure come to his house. The figure said to Xian, "The officials of the governor's office have urgently summoned you; you must go quickly." Xian angrily said, "I am a deputy general in the army, never having committed any fault; how could the officials of the governor's office summon me?" The white-clad figure said, "Just go, do not refuse. Otherwise, disaster will befall you." Xian shouted at him with a loud voice, and the white-clad figure galloped away, disappearing after taking only a few steps. Xian then realized it was a ghost. Late at night, it came again, calling Xian. Xian privately thought, "I have heard that life and death are predestined; how can one escape it?" He immediately went with it. They went out of the city for several miles, arriving at a public office. They saw a judge of the underworld in the hall, with dozens of officials standing on either side.

The underworld official heard that Xian had arrived and tidied his cap, descending the steps to greet him with full courtesy. He then invited Xian to sit and said, "I have heard of your bravery and valor, so I have sent you this mission." Xian replied, "I do not understand the reason for your summons, sir." The underworld official said, "In the underworld, there are patrol officers who inspect the roads of the mountains and rivers. If anyone does not follow the rules, they can be investigated. This is a significant task, one that can only be entrusted to someone who is brave and resolute. I hope you will accept this responsibility." Xian declined, saying, "I have no other talents; I would rather you choose someone else who is more suitable." The underworld official said, "Why are you so reluctant to accept this task?" He then ordered the case official to immediately summon the county clerk Wang Xin from Hongdong County and sent a clerk to escort Xian back home. Xian woke up in a state of shock. A few days later, the governor ordered Xian to go to the northern capital. On the way, he passed through Hongdong County and discussed matters with the county officials. The officials said, "There was a clerk named Wang Xin in the county who died several days ago."

**Right Attendant Yang Qian, who once served as a Shangshu Lang and was appointed to govern Xihé Prefecture, had a notable encounter with a local official named Zhang Wen in Pingyao County. Zhang Wen, without any apparent illness, suddenly died and awoke several days later. Initially, he saw his deceased brother visit his door. Zhang Wen was greatly startled and asked, "Are you a ghost? Why have you come?" His brother replied, "Since I left the world, I have often thought of my friends and relatives. It is like being blind and unable to forget what I see. Can I relive the joys of our past lives? Now, the underworld official has sent me to visit you." Zhang Wen asked, "Who is this underworld official?" His brother replied, "He is an official in the underworld, holding a very high position. I am now one of his subordinates and often carry out missions in the village. Because the realms of the living and the dead are different, I could not visit you before. Now, the underworld official has summoned you. You should come quickly." Zhang Wen was afraid and tried to decline, but his brother insisted, taking Zhang Wen's sleeve and leading him away.**

After walking for several miles, the road became so dark and indistinguishable that I could only hear the sound of carriages and horses rushing by, and the voices of people speaking. I also heard my wife, children, and brothers calling out and weeping, all saying, "Let us discuss the funeral arrangements." I walked alongside my brother, but I had no idea how far we had come. Then I thought to myself, "I am now dead, but I have often heard that when one dies, one should see all one's deceased friends and relatives. Now, if I call out to them, how can I know it is impossible?" I had a cousin by marriage named Wu Jilun, who had passed away several years ago, and we were close. I called out to him, and indeed, I heard Jilun respond, "Yes." We both wept together, and I then said to him, "Now that you are here, where do you live? Why is it so dark here?" Jilun replied, "This is the path of the dead, dark and obscure, without the light of the sun or moon." He added, "Regret that we cannot meet fully, now I must leave."

They traveled for several miles, the road dim and dark, indistinguishable. They could only hear the galloping of carriages and horses, the clamor of people speaking, and also the cries and calls of his wife, children, and brothers, all saying, “We are now discussing funeral arrangements.” Wen walked forward only with his older brother, knowing not how far the road ahead extended. He then reflected to himself, “I am now dead, yet I have often heard that when a person dies, they should meet all their deceased relatives and friends. If I call out to them now, how can I know it is not possible?” Wen had a cousin by marriage named Wu Jilun, who had passed away nearly a decade ago, and who had been close to Wen. He called out to him, and indeed, he heard Wu Jilun reply, “Yes.” Soon after, both of them wept together. Wen then said, “Now, where is your dwelling? Why is it so dark here?” Wu Jilun replied, “This is the冥途, a dark and obscure path, without the light of sun or moon.” He added, “I regret that we cannot speak at length, for I must now depart.”

"Wen said, 'Where are you going?' Ji Lun replied, 'My life has been filled with countless sins. Since I have been consigned to the underworld, I am daily subjected to humiliation and punishment. I heard your words earlier and came to speak with you, but I cannot stay.' He then wept for a long time and parted ways. He called out the names of dozens of deceased relatives, and they all came as if responding to his call, speaking of their torment and suffering, their words filled with sorrow and lamentation. Though I have gone ahead, I do not know where I will stop, but I often hear the cries of my wife, children, and brothers, as well as their voices, clearly present on all sides. When I called out their names, it was as if they did not hear me. After a while, someone shouted angrily, saying, 'The official of Pingyao County, Zhang Wen.' Zhang Wen replied, 'Yes.' Then another person angrily questioned Zhang Wen, asking how many sins he had committed in his lifetime. Zhang Wen firmly denied it. Then they ordered the clerk to bring out Zhang Wen's record. Soon, the clerk announced, 'Zhang Wen is not dead, and we wish to send him away.'"

The Underworld Official became angry and said, "Wen did not deserve to die, why did you summon him?" The clerk replied, "My brother Wen is now this official. Previously, he spent a long time in the underworld as a laborer and suffered greatly. I requested to take his place, although my request was not granted. Now, he has summoned me here himself." The Underworld Official rebuked his brother, saying, "Why did you summon a living person without regard for my laws?" He immediately ordered him to be imprisoned and sent Wen back. Wen thanked and left. He then traveled alone, as the road was dim and obscure, and he was extremely anxious and confused. In a short while, he suddenly saw a candle several dozen miles away, with a very faint light. Wen said happily, "Is this candle not from a human dwelling?" He ran quickly, following the shadow. After traveling over a hundred miles, he finally felt that the shadow was getting closer. He approached it and saw himself lying on a bed, with a candle in the room, which was indeed what he had seen. From then on, he woke up. Wen immediately recounted what he had heard in the underworld about his wife, brothers, and sisters crying and discussing funeral arrangements. He inquired at home, and there was not one thing that was different. He knew.

### Translation **Cui Jun** During the time when Cui Ning was stationed in Yizhou, Cui Jun, the governor of Jianwei, having already opened the stone box of Immortal Yin, that night, Cui Jun was summoned by the officials of the underworld. The underworld official was none other than the former prime minister Lü Yan, who was a close friend of Cui Jun. They met and wept together. Soon after, Lü Yan said to Cui Jun, "Immortal Yin has a stone box in your prefecture. Why did you open it without permission? Now, I am here on the command of the Heavenly Emperor to summon you for verification. What shall we do?" Cui Jun apologized, saying, "The ignorant and blind people of this age do not understand the affairs of immortals, so I rashly opened the box of the Immortal. My sin is indeed grave. However, according to the Threefold Pardon Principle, those who are ignorant or unaware may be pardoned. If you could show mercy, perhaps I might be able to reform myself." Lü Yan replied, "The command of the Heavenly Emperor is extremely strict, and the officials of the underworld are lowly. How dare we disobey?" He then summoned the recorder to bring out Cui Jun's record. Shortly after, the recorder arrived and reported, "Cui Jun has five remaining official positions and fifteen years left of his life. Now, the Heavenly Emperor has decreed that his lifespan will be reduced by thirteen years, and all his official positions will be taken away." Cui Jun apologized again, saying, "I have been your friend all my life. Today's crime is truly self-inflicted, but can an old friend not show leniency?" Lü Yan said, "Reducing your lifespan and stripping you of your official positions is inevitable, but I can grant you a temporary position for two years and provide generous stipends to fulfill my friend's trust." Cui Jun then repeatedly bowed and expressed his gratitude.

The speech was roughly concluded, when suddenly a cloud-like vapor appeared, with red light descending from the sky. Yin and the court officials and servants all got up in fear, saying, "The heavenly symbol has descended!" Yin then led Cui into a room. Cui immediately peeked through a crack and saw Yin with his headgear and笏 (a tablet used in court), leading the court officials to stand in rows in the courtyard. All of them bowed and clasped their hands. In the cloud, there was a person in a purple robe with golden fish decorations, holding a scroll, and announcing the emperor's command. Yin and the court officials bowed twice and received the scroll, and were lifted up by the cloud and soon disappeared. Yin ordered Cui to come out and sit, and opened the heavenly symbol to look at it, sighing and crying, and said to Cui, "Do you know Yuan San?" Cui replied, "He is just an old acquaintance from my days as a commoner." Yin said, "No blood relations are alike. Alas, how sad! Although I am friends with Yuan San, by now I can no longer save him, only accumulating grief and sighs." After finishing speaking, he wept again. Then he ordered a clerk to send Cui back. Cui bowed twice and left, accompanied by the messenger, entering the government office in the county town. He was already lying on a bed, with his wife and children crying around him. The messenger led Cui to bow before the bed, and his spirit and body merged, and he woke up. His family said, "He has been dead for three days." The county reported this to the censor. Cui immediately packed his belongings, and with his entire family went to Shu, fully informing Ning. Ning then appointed Cui as acting deputy governor, giving him a monthly salary of 200,000 coins. At that time, Yuan Zai was in control of the state affairs, and Ning had a good relationship with him, sending many letters. Upon hearing Cui's words, he feared being implicated and ordered his close officials to carry 500 gold pieces, bribing Yuan Zai's attendants, buying and burning over a hundred scrolls. Two years later, Cui also died.

○ Liu Gai Liu Gai, from Pengcheng, served as the magistrate of Hancheng during the reign of Emperor Dezong. He died in office, leaving his family in poverty. They then lived in a temple in Hancheng. Before the end of the year, the county magistrate Sin also died and woke up after three days. At first, Sin slept during the day and dreamed of a clerk leading him westward, passing through highlands and large lakes for hundreds of miles, and reaching a city. After entering the gate, the guiding clerk disappeared. Sin was very afraid and immediately left the city gate. There were guards at the gate, holding swords and standing in formation. Sin asked them, and the guards raised their swords and pointed south, saying, "This is the way to the path of life." Sin then knew he was dead, his back was sweaty, and his thighs trembled. He immediately went south, even though he was exhausted, he dared not rest. Soon, he saw more than ten people standing on the side of the road, one person called out to Sin, took his hand and wept, looked at him carefully, and it was Liu Gai. He said, "What are you doing here?" Sin told him everything, and Liu Gai said, "Since we parted ways, as if I had fallen into a pit, thinking about my life, how could I have it again?" He then wept. Sin then asked about the afterlife, and Liu Gai wept but did not speak. After a long time, he said, "Where are my wife and children, are they safe?" Sin said, "Your son is living in a temple in Hancheng for almost half a year." Liu Gai said, "You will go now, to ask for news. I am in a deep and shameful place, far removed and unable to escape. Every time I think of my wife and children, as if they were close but I never forget to walk. The sorrow of being in the afterlife, how can it be fully expressed?" After parting, Liu Gai said to Sin, "I have a poem to give to you, it says:

The Path of the Dead is far and unknown, people do not know; there is no need to speak of it to cause sorrow. Gladly met you to deliver family news, the future meeting is uncertain and hard to predict. Afterward, he wept again. Dong bid farewell. Not even ten miles away, he heard the sound of the bell ringing very loudly, awoke due to fear. Knew that Dong was the cousin of Shixi, his nephew, Cui's son, often told others about the matter. Master Kou Qianzhi, a person who attained enlightenment during the Northern Wei period, often carved stones as records, stored in Mount Song. At the beginning of the Shangyuan era, a resident of Gaocun County in Luochuan, while gathering herbs in the mountains, found it and presented it to County Magistrate Fan Wen. Reported to the prefecture, the prefecture reported it to the imperial court, Emperor Gaozong ordered it to be stored in the imperial treasury. The inscriptions on the stone are numerous, deep and difficult to understand. Roughly says "wood and son will rule the world"; also says "the one who stops the sword is the dragon"; also says "the Li family will never change its lineage"; also says "in the middle of the tripod, the true form will be revealed"; also says "the foundation will last for a thousand years." What is meant by "wood and son will rule the world," it refers to the Tang dynasty being granted the mandate of heaven. "The one who stops the sword is the dragon," it refers to Empress Wu Zetian. "The Li family will never change its lineage," it means the Tang dynasty's continuity. "In the middle of the tripod, the true form will be revealed," it is the temple name of Emperor Zhenzong, "Zhen" is the posthumous title of Emperor Zhenzong, how can this not be believed? "The foundation will last for a thousand years," "Ji" is the name of Emperor Xuanzong, "a thousand years," indicating a long lineage. After Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne, Fan Wen's son, Qinben, presented the original stone inscription to the court, the emperor ordered it to be compiled into the national history.

Wei Dajing, a man renowned for his literary achievements, was from Jieyang. He avoided the vulgar and often shut himself off from worldly matters. Born with keen insight, he fully understood astronomy and calendar systems, seeking profound truths. Later, he died of old age and was buried in the wilds of Jieyang. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (Tang Dynasty), a great flood occurred, and Jiang Shidu, following an imperial decree, dug the Wubian River to irrigate the salt fields. This project involved demolishing many houses and tombs, which the people of Jieyang resented. Upon reaching Wei Dajing's tomb, Jiang Shidu excavated the foundation and found an inscribed stone with an epitaph, which was the words of the master. It read: "Jiang Shidu, move it three or five steps to the south." The workers found this and reported it to Jiang Shidu. Jiang Shidu was amazed by the incident and exclaimed and praised for a long time. Turning to his officials, he said, "Wei Dajing is truly a remarkable man." He immediately ordered the workers to move the river several dozen steps away from the master's tomb.

**Wu Junzai** During the Kaiyuan era, a great flood struck the southern regions, drowning thousands of people. The local government reported this disaster to the central government, and Emperor Xuanzong ordered the imperial censor Wu Junzai to inspect the area. Upon arriving, Wu Junzai suddenly saw an ancient tomb by the roadside, whose entrance had been eroded by the water. He ordered the remains to be moved to high ground. After opening the tomb, they found a stone with inscriptions carved on it, which was a record of the tomb. Following the inscriptions, there were twenty words, which were the words of the person who chose the burial site. The words said: "Thousand years after, this place will turn into a spring. Thanks to meeting the imperial censor Wu, we were moved to high ground." Wu Junzai read this and found it remarkable. He checked the year and confirmed that it was indeed a thousand years later. **Han Yu (II)** South of Quanzhou, there is a mountain. The mountain rises abruptly and stands vertically. Below it is a deep pool, whose depth is unknown, covering an area of about ten mu. There are dragons and snakes in the water, which have been a problem for the people. Anyone who accidentally approaches or even animals that come to drink are often swallowed. The people of Quanzhou have suffered from this for many years. As a result, those living near the mountain have all moved their families to other cities to escape the danger. In the fifth year of the Yuanhe era, one night, there was a sudden and violent thunderstorm in the south of the mountain, shaking the earth for hundreds of miles, as if the mountain itself was collapsing. The entire city was in fear. The locals, along with their cattle, horses, chickens, and dogs, all fell to the ground, sweating profusely. The tiles on the roofs clashed, and the trees were uprooted. From 7 PM to 1 AM, the thunder and lightning finally ceased. The next morning, they went to see what had happened. The mountain had collapsed, and the stone cliffs, hundreds of ren high, were almost completely destroyed, filling the pond.

A flood overflowed, spreading to fill the four fields, and the blood of the dragon and the serpent spread like black and yellow. On the stone wall, there were carved words, nineteen in number, with an ancient style. The scholars and commoners of the county could not understand them. Since then, the residents no longer suffered. Those who had been afraid ceased to fear, and those who had moved also returned, building houses and huts, connecting their land. The prefecture governor named the place "Shi Ming Li" because of it, as a memorial to the characters and to recognize its uniqueness. Later, a guest from the spring was able to transmit the characters, bringing them to Dong Luo. At that time, the former Ministry of Personnel Deputy Secretary Han Yu, who was a Prefect of Henan from the position of Secretary of the Ministry of Rites, saw and recognized them. The text reads: "Edict to the black water carp, Heaven's deity humbles the ox-slaughtering man, the divine book of Ren and Gui is urgent." Thus, examining its meaning in detail, it seems to be a command from the Heavenly Deity to punish the dragon and serpent for their harm. The characters are in the form of seal script, so the people of the spring did not recognize them.

In the autumn of the ninth month of the first year of the Yuanhe era, Wu Shaocheng, the military governor of Huai West, died. His son, Wu Yuanji, defied imperial orders and rebelled. The emperor then ordered neighboring regions to attack Huai West from all sides with their armies, but after several years, they were unable to defeat him. In the thirteenth year of the Yuanhe era, the chancellor, Duke of Jin, Pei Du, was summoned to lead the campaign. Upon his arrival, Pei Du ordered the workers to deepen the moat and fortify the area, and he also ordered the digging of the ground. One of the workers found a stone with inscribed text, which he presented to Pei Du. The text read: "A bamboo pole stands at the bottom of a well, its color deep green. The chicken is not yet fat, the wine not yet brewed, the boys blocking the carriage should retreat." Pei Du received the stone and showed it to his subordinates, asking them to interpret its meaning. None of them could fully understand it. As Pei Du was pondering the inscription, a soldier suddenly leaped forward from the ranks and exclaimed: "Wu Yuanji defies the emperor's command, allowing his troops to plot rebellion. Thanks to the emperor's divine authority and the chancellor's virtue, it will not be long before the rebel is captured. I dare to congratulate the chancellor on his victory."

Dù was surprised and asked about it. The response was: "A seal engraver found an inscription on a stone, which is an omen. Moreover, 'A bamboo pole at the bottom of a well, with deep green color' refers to Wu Shaocheng rising from a common soldier to command ten thousand troops, becoming a leader of one region, and symbolizing his glory. 'The chicken is not fat' means there is no meat. By removing 'meat' from 'fat,' we get the character '己.' 'The wine is not ripe' means there is no water. By removing 'water' from 'wine,' we get the character '酉.' 'Obstructing the carriage' refers to soldiers or guards. 'And should retreat' means one should withdraw and defend one's position. From this, it can be deduced that on the day of Jǐ Yǒu, there will be a victory. If it has not occurred by the expected time, it can still happen later." Dù was pleased and turned to those around him, saying, "The soldier is a discerning person." He sighed and found it remarkable. In the tenth month of that year, the Chancellor Li Shuo led his troops into Huai West, capturing Yuan Ji and all the rebels. Dù then checked the date and found it to be indeed Jǐ Yǒu. Thus, Dù further admired the soldier's discernment and promoted him to the rank of deputy general.

In the shadow of Heishan Mountain lies the Temple of Li Weigong. During the Bao Li era, Zhang Weiqing, the Protector General of the Xiongnu, had a clerk named Lu Li who once dreamed of a tall man dressed in black. The man told Lu Li, "I have lived in the temple of Wei Gong for a long time. Please move me to the military city." Afterward, he disappeared. Upon waking, Lu Li was confused and immediately reported to Zhang Weiqing, saying, "Wei Gong has great merit for the state. Now the temple is in ruins, severely affected by wind and rain. I wish to renovate its structure." Zhang Weiqing was pleased and agreed to his request. Previously, the Xiongnu government had praised Zhang Weiqing's good governance, described his achievements, and requested General Luo Zhong to report to the emperor. An edict was issued for the Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat, Gao Gong Tai, to write about the matter and carve it on a stele. The edict had arrived, but there was no stele. Zhang Weiqing then sent someone to collect stone from Yunzhong Prefecture, who had not yet returned. When repairing the temple of Wei Gong, the western part was excavated, and a stone was found, square and long, with a clearly visible "Zhang" character carved on it. The workers presented it to Zhang Weiqing. Zhang Weiqing was delighted and said, "Heaven has given me the stele stone." He then summoned the clerk to see it. Lu Li was both surprised and amazed and stood up to congratulate, reporting the previous dream. Thus, the stone was used as a stele, and Gao Gong Tai's writing was carved on it.

During the Dàhé period, Wang Fán, while investigating Dan-yáng, had a moat dug around the city. When they dug several feet deep, they discovered a stone with inscriptions that read: "There is a mountain with a stone, the stone contains jade, the jade has a flaw, and thus it ceases to be." The workers found the stone and reported the matter to Wang Fán in detail. After studying the meaning, they could not understand it. He then ordered his officials to interpret it, but none could explain the reasoning. Several days later, an old man asked to see Wang Fán’s secretary and privately said, "I heard that Master Wang found an inscribed stone. Is there anyone who can interpret it now?" The secretary replied, "Master Wang is still thinking about it. How could you possibly understand it right away?" The old man said, "This is an ominous sign. The phrase 'There is a mountain with a stone, the stone contains jade, the jade has a flaw, and thus it ceases to be' describes Master Wang’s lineage. Your ancestor was named Jīn, who gave birth to Chu. From the text, 'There is a mountain with a stone' refers to this. Chu gave birth to Fán, which means 'the stone contains jade.' Jīn’s son was named Xiá Xiū, which means 'the jade has a flaw and thus it ceases to be.' 'Xiū' is a sign of the end of the lineage. Based on this interpretation, it seems the lineage will come to an end!" The secretary thanked him. The old man finished speaking and left. In the ninth year of the Dàhé period in winter, Wang Fán died, and his clan was destroyed, just as the old man had predicted.

During the Tahe era, there was a man named Liu Guang who traveled south. As he walked along a mountain path, the evening approached, and he mistakenly entered a deep mountain ravine. The pines wound and twisted, and he walked several miles before reaching a stone chamber. Clouds and water surrounded the chamber, and clear springs intersected within it. The chamber contained a couch, as if it were inhabited by a person. In front of him was a colorful sunset, indicating that it was certainly not a place for human habitation. Liu Guang stood by the stream, gazing fixedly, when he suddenly saw a jar lying on the ground. He immediately approached it, and beneath the jar was a spring, no more than a foot wide. The water was clear and refreshing. He lifted a cup to drink, and it tasted like sweet liquor. After drinking more than ten cups, he became extremely drunk and lay down on the couch. By morning, he awoke and saw that the stone wall was covered with numerous inscriptions. He wrote down the characters and placed them in his sleeve. The inscriptions read:

During the year of the rabbit, in the reign of Emperor Gaozu of Tang, who took the title "Emperor of Tang" (唐王) for eight years, I abandoned my palace and left my throne. Deep and high, I was not known nor recognized by others. From this point onward, more than two hundred years later, the light shone brightly and harmoniously at its beginning. In the east, there was a rabbit with a small head and a long tail, passing through my path and arriving at my village. It drank from my spring and became intoxicated, climbing onto my bed to sleep. Carved into the wall, the profound meaning was difficult to discern, and who could understand the message left by Dongpingzi? Guang read it and found it unusual. He then walked out a few dozen steps and looked back at the room, but it was nowhere to be seen. Guang could not understand it. A man named Lü came and examined it, explaining shortly afterward: "I have understood everything. This is the speech of one who has attained the Dao. At the beginning of the Tang dynasty, the title 'Wude' was established, and in the second year of Wude, which was the year of the rabbit (己卯), '武之在卯' refers to the second year of Wude. '尧王' refers to Emperor Gaozu, who took the title 'Emperor of Tang' (唐王). '八季' also refers to eight years. '我弃其寝，我去其扆' means that he left, and indeed, he left in the second year of Wude. '深深然，高高然，人不吾知，人不吾谓' means that he was hidden and unknown by others. '由今之后，二百余祀' means that the ruler will come. And indeed, the Tang dynasty has now lasted for more than two hundred years."

The bright and intense light, and the gentle and harmonious beginning—this refers to the year of the Goat, "丁未." The flame is fire, is it not the Southern fire of the Heavenly Stems, and "未" is also the position of fire. The "gentle and harmonious beginning" refers to the current emperor who has established the era name "Taihe," and "its beginning" is the first year. The phrase "in the east there is a rabbit, with a small head and a long tail" describes the emperor's name and surname. "The east" is the position of wood in the Heavenly Stems, "rabbit" is the sign of the rabbit, and "卯" is attached to "wood," which forms the character "柳." "Small head and long tail" refers to the light. "Traveling along my path" and "coming to my village" describe the emperor's arrival. "Drinking from my spring to get drunk, and sleeping on my bed" describe the emperor's stay. "Inscribed on the wall, profound and hidden in meaning. Who can interpret it? It is the East Pingzi," meaning that the meaning is profound and hidden, and only I can interpret it. "East Pingzi" is my hometown, and thus it is more credible. Thus, it is determined that this is indeed a record left by one who has attained the Dao.

During the Yuanhe period of the Tang Dynasty, Li Shidao occupied the regions of Qing and Qi, maintaining a large and brave army, controlling a territory of a thousand miles, and accumulating millions in wealth. He refused to pay tribute or visit the emperor. Emperor Xianzong ordered generals to attack him, but the imperial army was unsuccessful, and Li Shidao became even more arrogant. He then built a new palace modeled after the emperor's main hall and chose a day to move in. That night, the sky suddenly turned dark, the wind and thunder shook violently, and it was struck by lightning and collapsed. Soon after, it was destroyed by a fire from heaven, leaving nothing standing. The people of Qing and Qi looked at each other and said, "Those who rebel against their sovereign as subjects certainly deserve punishment. Now the divine retribution is evident; how can they escape their punishment?" A few days later, Li Shidao was indeed executed.

A Son of the Xiao Family During the Longqing era of the Tang Dynasty, a son of the Xiao family from Lanling was renowned for his bravery. Traveling as a guest in the regions of Xiang and Chu, he arrived at Changsha Prefecture and stayed at Yanshan Temple. One evening, as he was alone and had lit a candle, a sudden thunderstorm shook the eaves, lasting for a long time. Soon, he heard a rustling sound under the western wall. Xiao, relying on his physical strength, was not afraid. By the bed, there was a large mallet. He carried it to the wall and bent down to strike the source of the sound. With one blow, he struck it, and a loud cry was heard, as if it was moaning. He then struck it dozens of times in succession, and the sound finally ceased. The rain and wind also stopped. Xiao was pleased and said, "The monster must be dead." By dawn, under the western wall, he saw a very strange ghost: its body was entirely green, it was hunched and short, and it had a golden axe and wooden wedge, tied with hemp threads around its body. It blinked and panted, as if very exhausted. He then reported it to the temple monks for them to see. One of them said, "This is a thunder ghost, it is the messenger of God. Why do you insult God? Disaster will soon befall you." The villagers prepared sacrificial animals and wine to worship it. Soon, clouds and mist became dark and dim, emerging from the room, it went out the door and ascended to heaven, and the ghost also followed. Afterward, thunder sounded again, lasting for a few meals before stopping. Xiao's courage grew even stronger, and the villagers all called him a brave warrior.

The mountains of Dongyang Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty bordered the Zhejiang River, surrounding several hundred li. The river bent and encircled the mountains, obstructing boats and rafts, causing great trouble for the people. During the Dazhong period, the high-ranking official Jing Xin was sent to govern Dongyang. One night, the mountains were shrouded in dim clouds and mist, and a severe storm with strong winds, thunder, and lightning shook houses and buildings. The river water overflowed, and no one was not alarmed and confused. Until dawn, the storm finally cleared up, and people went to see what had happened. The mountains were already split in half, separated by several hundred steps, and the river flow was guided straight through, filling all the curved sections with stones. Thus, the trouble of winding around was no longer a problem.

The Monk Zhikong During the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk named Zhikong at Jianyuan Temple in Jinling Prefecture. He was a native of the prefecture, and his spiritual practice was renowned in the local area. He was over seventy years old. One night, after closing the temple gates, a sudden strong wind and thunder arose as if from the meditation hall, rumbling continuously.
