The candles went out, and dust filled the air, making it very dark and murky. The eaves and buildings shook. Suddenly, he thought to himself, "I abandoned my family to become a monk, and I have been a monk for over forty years. Such a violent thunderstorm, could it be that the dragon is angry with me? Otherwise, I deserve to be struck dead by lightning." As the sound became even louder, he sat down again and prayed, "When I was young, I studied Buddhism and have been a monk for over fifty years. Could my actions be contrary to Buddhist teachings? Otherwise, could I be offending the dragon? If that is the case, how dare I escape death! If not, then I hope for the storm to clear quickly, so that all the monks in the temple can be freed." After he finished speaking, a loud clap of thunder sounded, as if it came from all sides. The mats and beds were overturned and destroyed, and the darkness and confusion were overwhelming. He was so shocked that he fell to the ground. Only a short time later, the sound finally stopped, and the sky cleared, revealing the moon and stars. But he noticed a foul smell, as if it were inside the room. He lit a candle to see and found a dragon under the wall, with a skin several zhang long, and blood covering the ground. It was a tree in the north of the meditation hall, tens of ren tall, which was struck dead by lightning, splitting along its grain, with traces of a dragon coiled inside.

In the eastern part of the Tang territory, about a hundred miles southeast of Hedong, there is a body of water called "Baizhang Hong." It is so clear that even the finest details can be seen. On the left side of the post road, it is surrounded by willows and elms, with smoke and shadows clustering together, making it a place for travelers to rest. In the fifth year of the Dazhong era during the summer, Xu Sheng traveled from Luoyang to Hedong. Upon reaching this water, he was extremely tired and exhausted, so he took a brief rest, reciting poetry and looking around. As noon approached, he suddenly heard a faint sound like the buzzing of flies coming from the water. Soon, a thin light appeared, and the sound grew louder, like the pounding of wheels, with the light resembling a rope being dragged. Xu Sheng was surprised by this, and as the sound grew more frequent, a thunderclap came from the water, with the light flashing like lightning and connecting with the clouds. He quickly told the story to his traveling companions, who replied, "This is Baizhang Hong. It never fails to rain at the predicted time during droughts. Now the drought is severe, and my teacher has ordered the officials to pray at this place. The shaman said, 'There will be heavy rain on a certain day.' And indeed, it happened on that day."

Imperial Censor Yang Xunmei's Nephews During the Tang dynasty, Imperial Censor Yang Xunmei lived in Guangling Prefecture. He had several nephews who were young and had just begun their studies. One night, there was a storm with wind, rain, thunder, and lightning. The nephews all went out to look and said, "We heard that there are ghosts in the thunder, but where are they? Can we catch them and kill them?" Soon, the thunder became even louder, and the trees were knocked down. Suddenly, there was a loud boom, as if it was right in the eaves. The nephews were very frightened, so they ran back into the house, stood against the wall, and dared not move. Then they heard the thunder as if the earth was roaring, and the houses were shaking. The nephews were even more afraid. After a meal's worth of time, the thunder and lightning stopped, and the sky cleared up. There was a large locust tree in the courtyard, and its roots were pulled out and split. The nephews felt pain in their thighs that they could not bear, and they told Yang Xunmei about it. He ordered a servant to bring a candle to look at them, and they all had red marks, spread across ten or so places, looking like marks from a stick, as if they were caused by ghostly thunder.

During the reign of Emperor Suzong (Baoying period), there was a man named Wei Si Xuan from Jingzhao who was temporarily residing in Luoyang. He had a nature that preferred the unusual and once admired the techniques of immortals. Later, he traveled to Mount Song and encountered a Taoist who taught him: "Those who consume elixirs of gold can extend their lifespan. You should first learn to refine gold, and if so, then you can shoulder the burden of Cesi and ride upon Guangchengzi." Wei Si Xuan thus sought to learn the art of refining gold. Over ten years, he met hundreds of practitioners but ultimately could not grasp the subtleties. One day, a recluse named Xin Rui appeared, looking very thin and with a sad, pale appearance, wearing tattered fur, and knocked on Wei Si Xuan's door. He said to Wei Si Xuan, "I am a sick man, destitute and without a place to go. I have heard that Master Wei enjoys antiquity and the extraordinary, gathering all kinds of strange people and magicians. I come to pay my respects, and I wish Master Wei would accept me." Wei Si Xuan immediately invited the recluse to stay in his house.

Later, the scholar fell ill, and his abscess burst, bleeding profusely. The entire We family despised him. Si Xuan once summoned several sages to a feast, but the scholar was not invited. After the meal was prepared, the scholar suddenly appeared before the guests and urinated on the banquet table, completely wetting it. The guests, angry, all stood up, and even the servants of the We family came to scold him. The scholar then left, walking to the courtyard, where he suddenly disappeared. Si Xuan and the guests were greatly puzzled. When he looked back at what he had urinated, it was purple gold liquid, with a brilliant, dazzling light, truly a treasure of the ages. Si Xuan was both surprised and sighed. Someone explained, saying, "The scholar is the essence of purple gold. Verifying his name and status, it is true. And 'Xin' is the Western 'Geng-Xin-Jin' (metal). The character '锐' contains '兑,' which follows metal, and '兑' is also the proper position of the West. Analyzing the meaning, then my explanation fits perfectly."

**The Tale of Li Yuan** Li Yuan, a scholar who had passed the imperial examinations, was from Hedong and lived in the Yan寿li district of Chang'an. In the early summer of the Yuanhe era, one night, Li Yuan was alone in his room, lying on his bed, not yet fully asleep. Suddenly, he heard a faint sound coming from the western corner of his room, which was both fine and distant, ringing like the music of metal and stone, lasting for a long time. Soon after, there was a singer whose voice was extremely clear and high-pitched, sounding crisp and lasting for a long time. Li Yuan secretly memorized the lyrics: "Divided into the blue-green leaves, The sound is like the chime of a stone. On the seventh day of the seventh month, I will show you my form." When the song ended, the sound ceased. Li Yuan was both surprised and puzzled. The next morning, he sent his servant to investigate the source of the sound, but they could not find anything. That night, Li Yuan was again alone, and he heard the same sound, which was both sorrowful and high-pitched, lasting for a long time. It was the same song as before. When the song ended, Li Yuan knew it must be a strange occurrence, and he remained silent, feeling very strange about it. This happened for several nights in a row, and he continued to hear the sound. Later, in the autumn, on the sixth day, there was a heavy rain at night, and the northern wall of his hall collapsed. The next day, he heard the sound again from the north side of the wall. Li Yuan was startled and looked, and he found a jar buried under the northern wall, about a foot long, made of gold, with an unusual and ancient shape, different from any golden jar he had seen. When he cleaned off the dirt and moss, he could see faint writing on it, though it was not visible at first glance. It was clearly a thousand-year-old artifact. When he tapped it, the sound was very long. He then cleaned it thoroughly and read the inscriptions, which were all in small seal script. They were the *Zuo You Ming* (Left and Right Admonitions) by Cui Ziyu. Li Yuan was amazed by this discovery, but he still did not know which dynasty it was from.

In the area of虞鄉, there was a mountain temple where a Taoist priest named涤阳道士resided. During the period of大和中, the Taoist priest once climbed the altar alone one night to observe. He suddenly saw unusual light in the courtyard, which originated from a well. A creature resembling a rabbit, with a color resembling pure gold, emerged with the light and circled the altar. After some time, the creature returned to the well. From then on, the creature appeared every night. The Taoist priest did not dare to tell anyone about the phenomenon. Later, when the well was cleaned, a small golden rabbit with a brilliant light was discovered. The Taoist priest placed the golden rabbit in a small box. At that time, the official Li Rong held a position in the area of蒲津 and was friendly with the Taoist priest. The Taoist priest sent the golden rabbit to Li Rong. After that, Li Rong moved from the position of county magistrate in奉先 to the position of prefect in忻州. The golden rabbit suddenly disappeared. After a month, Li Rong died.

During the Tang dynasty, during the Dali period, there was a man named Lü Sheng, who was a magistrate in Shangyu, Zhejiang, and was summoned to serve in the capital. Afterward, he temporarily resided in Yongchong Lane. One night, he gathered with several friends for a meal in his room. After the meal, as they were about to go to bed, suddenly an old woman appeared, her appearance and attire pure and white, about two feet tall, emerging from the northern corner of the room, walking slowly toward them. Her appearance was extremely unusual. The others looked at her and laughed among themselves. The old woman gradually approached his bed and said, "You have a gathering, yet you do not summon me? Why do you treat me so poorly?" Lü Sheng rebuked her, and she retreated, disappearing at the northern corner. He was both surprised and puzzled, unsure of where she had come from. The next day, Lü Sheng alone woke up in his room and saw the old woman again at the northern corner, hesitating to come forward, looking anxious as if she was afraid of something. Lü Sheng rebuked her again, and she vanished. The next day, Lü Sheng quietly thought to himself, "This must be a supernatural phenomenon. Tonight, if it comes, if I do not get rid of it, it will surely become a problem for me soon." He then placed a sword under his bed. That night, the old woman indeed emerged slowly from the northern corner, her face without fear, approaching the bed. Lü Sheng swung the sword at her. The old woman suddenly climbed onto the bed, thrusting her arm into his chest, while others leaped around him, lifting their sleeves and dancing. After a while, another old woman suddenly climbed onto the bed, again thrusting her arm into him. Lü Sheng suddenly felt his entire body turn cold, as if covered in frost. He swung the sword wildly, and soon several old women followed, dancing along with him.

The scholar kept swinging the sword, and he also turned into more than ten old women, each about an inch long. Although there were more, they all looked the same, and none could be distinguished, as they circled the four walls. The scholar was very afraid and could not think of a way out. One of the old women in the middle said to the scholar, "I will merge into one. Please watch." After finishing speaking, she retreated to the north corner and disappeared. The next day, the scholar told others about the incident.

There was a man named Tiansi who was skilled in using talismans to dispel strange spirits. His fame spread throughout Chang'an. When he was told about the old woman's strange behavior, he joyfully exclaimed, "This is my task. Removing her is as easy as picking up an ant with a claw. Tonight, I wish to go to your house and wait for her." That night, the man and Tiansi sat together in the room. Soon after, the old woman indeed came to the foot of the bed. Tiansi shouted, "Spirit, depart quickly!" The old woman boldly ignored those around her and slowly walked in, staying for a long time. She then said to Tiansi, "This is beyond your understanding." Suddenly, the old woman waved her hand, and it fell to the ground. Then another small old woman appeared, leaping onto the bed, and suddenly entered Tiansi's mouth. Tiansi was shocked and exclaimed, "Am I going to die?" The old woman said, "I have already said I would not harm you, but you did not listen. What is the state of Tiansi's illness now? But it will make you wealthy." After speaking, she left. The next day, someone told the man, "It is appropriate to dig at the northwest corner; you will see it." The man was delighted and returned home, ordering his servant to dig deeply at the spot. It was not even a foot deep when they found a jar that could hold several shi (a unit of volume), containing a large amount of mercury. The man finally realized that the old woman was a spirit of mercury. Tiansi eventually died of cold and fright.

○严生 严生，冯翊人，家住汉南。他曾经游览岘山，获得一件东西，形状像弹丸，颜色黑而大，有光泽，看起来洁净透明，如同轻冰一般。严生将它展示给别人看，有人说道：“这是珍珠。”严生因此以“弹珠”命名它，并常常将其放在箱中。后来，严生来到长安，在春明门遇到了一个胡人，胡人敲门说道：“我的袋子里有一件奇宝，希望能见到。”严生立即拿出“弹珠”给胡人看。胡人捧着它高兴地说：“这是天下独一无二的宝物，愿意以三十万的价格购买。”严生问道：“这件宝物有何用途，而您如此看重它的价值呢？”胡人回答：“我是西方国家的人。这是我国的至宝，国人称之为‘清水珠’，如果把它放入浑浊的水中，水会变得清澈透明。自从失去这件宝物已有三年，我国的井泉都变得浑浊，百姓都生病了。因此我越过山海，来到中原寻找它。现在终于在你这里找到了。”胡人随即命令将浑浊的水倒入罐中，然后投入珍珠，不久，水变得清澈明亮，连细小的东西都能看得清楚。严生于是将珍珠给了胡人，得到了报酬后离开。

○ The Three Treasures of the Jade Purity Wei Yan of Duling, courtesy name Jingzhao, passed the imperial examination for the jinshi degree during the Kaiyuan period of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang. He lived and traveled in Shu, a region known for its scenic beauty. As spring came to an end, Wei Yan and his friends held feasts and banquets, enjoying themselves well into the night. One day, someone invited them, saying, "Ten miles south of the city, there is a Zheng's Pavilion. It rises in a garden and is truly a place beyond the mundane world. I wish to go with you." Wei Yan, hearing this, was very happy, so he went with them.

Traveling south for ten miles, I arrived at Zheng's Pavilion. The pavilion stood tall and isolated, with a grand and imposing presence. Its doors were adorned with floral carvings, and its walls rose like smoke, creating an ethereal and mystical atmosphere. I gazed upon it, unable to look away, for it was truly a scene beyond the mundane world. A messenger bowed and invited me to enter. Once inside, I saw a dozen or so deities on the pavilion, all of them resplendent in their beauty. They stood still, as if in contemplation, their sleeves fluttering like clouds, creating a sense of ethereal movement. There were also a dozen or so attendants standing on either side of them. The intricate patterns and decorations were faint and almost indistinguishable, adding to the mysterious and otherworldly quality of the scene. One of the deities, looking at me, said, "Wei Scholar, you have arrived." They commanded their attendants to invite me to ascend the pavilion. I ascended through a series of steps and bowed upon reaching the top. The deities greeted me with joy, saying, "Have you not heard of the story of Liu and Ruan? Today, you are treated to the same experience. We wish to share a drink with you and enjoy the spring scenery. What do you think?" I responded with a polite thank you, saying, "I did not expect to be treated like Liu and Ruan today. How fortunate I am! But what are these female deities, and what is their purpose? I would like to know." The deities replied, "We are daughters of the Jade Clarity, and we have lived here for a long time. This is the Jade Clarity Palace. We had heard that you were a scholar who had failed the imperial examination and were traveling here. We wished to invite you, but we were afraid you might not come, which would be an embarrassment. Therefore, we used Zheng's Pavilion to invite you, and you have indeed fulfilled our wish."

Although this is a fairy realm, and it is said that mortals cannot linger here, you can live here without any harm. Please do not doubt it. They immediately ordered a feast and music in the pavilion. All the string and wind instruments were played, floating and cool, transcending the mysterious and the profound, not of earthly tunes. When the wine had been drunk deeply, the immortals said, "I have heard that the Tang emperor reveres immortals. I have a new piece of music, called 'Purple Cloud,' and I wish to present it to the Holy Emperor. You, being a Tang person, can convey it to the emperor once, can you do this?" I replied, "I am just a scholar. In Chang'an, I am merely preoccupied with trivial matters, and I cannot even see the gates of the emperor's palace, nor is there anyone who understands music. How can I bring this about?" The immortals said, "Since you cannot, I will convey it to the emperor through a dream." They also said, "I have three treasures, which I will give to you. These can make you as wealthy as kings and dukes. Please accept them." He then ordered his attendants to bring the treasures. First, he brought out a cup, green in color, and brilliantly transparent, and looked at me, saying, "This is a green jade cup." Then he brought out a pillow, resembling jade, slightly red, and said, "This is a red phoenix pillow." Then he brought out a small box, purple in color, also resembling jade, but even more transparent, and said, "This is a purple jade box." Afterward, he gave all of them to me. I bowed in gratitude and left.

Yan had not walked a mile when he looked back at the pavilion, but it was nowhere to be seen. Astonished by this, he still did not know where it was. So he took the treasure back to Chang'an. The following year, he failed the examination and traveled east to Guangling. He then gathered his treasure in the Guangling market. A foreigner saw it and bowed, saying, "This is a rare treasure in the world. Even after a thousand years, no one has obtained it. How did you obtain it?" Yan told him, and the foreigner asked, "What kind of treasure is this?" Yan replied, "It is the Three Treasures of the Jade Clear Truth." So he exchanged it for several million in value. Yan then built a grand mansion and lived in Guangling as a wealthy and influential person, but he ultimately died as a commoner. ○ Three Treasures Village In the southwest of Fufeng County, there is a village called Three Treasures Village. An old man from the Hu people said, "When the village was founded, a foreign monk told the villagers, 'This place has a treasure aura, but it cannot be obtained now. The time for its revelation will come.' The villagers asked, 'What kind of treasure is this?' The monk replied, 'It is the Three Treasures of Jiaozhi.' So they named the village 'Three Treasures' to commemorate the event."

In the spring of the first year of the Kai Cheng era, a villager dreamed of a man at night. The man wore a black headband and cap, a robe with wide sleeves, and carried a long sword at his waist. His appearance was dignified and ancient. He said to the villager, "I once served in the Eastern Han Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Guangwu, alongside the Flying General Ma Gong in a campaign against Jiaozhi. I once obtained treasures from the southern people. Later, Ma Gong was slandered for allegedly looting goods from the south and bringing them back. Emperor Guangwu became angry and ordered a search of Ma Gong's home. I feared that I would also be implicated in the scandal, so I buried the treasures here." He had not finished speaking when the villager woke up. The villager then told his neighbors about the dream. That summer, on a cloudy and dim evening, a young herdsman saw a bright light in the western fields, like a long piece of silk being dragged, and it remained for a long time without disappearing. The herdsman was surprised and told his father, who rushed to see it. The light was even brighter. The next evening, it was the same. The villagers then went out at night to follow the light, and they saw it emerging from the ground, like a flame of burning wood. The villagers planted a stake to mark the spot. The next day, they brought shovels and tools, dug down to the marked spot, and found a golden turtle about two cun long, with a design that was intricate and unusual, unlike anything seen before. They also found a treasure sword about 2.4 chi long and an ancient mirror with a diameter of more than one chi. All the items were covered in dust. The villagers took them to the county office.

During the time of the county magistrate of Pei State, Liu Sui, he obtained it. When drawn from the whetstone, the sword was as calm as water waves in color, though sharp enough to cut through jade, there was nothing sharper. Its length of 24 inches, according to the ancient standard of 8 inches per foot, equates to three ancient feet. The mirrors were intricately patterned with mythical creatures surrounding the handle, and they were of ancient origin, perfectly preserved. They were then polished, resulting in a clarity as pure as clear water, truly rare treasures of the world. The county magistrate Liu said, "These are ancient treasures suitable for the royal treasury, comparable to the Heavenly Ball and the Jade Bi, shining in the upper hall." They sealed the matter and reported it to the commander of Qiyang, wishing to present it to the emperor. At that time, Chen, who was also the military governor of Qilong, obtained and cherished these treasures, thus possessing them. From then on, no one knew about them. Known. The Jade Dragon Ointment In An Nan, there is the Jade Dragon Ointment, which the people of the south use to transform silver into liquid. Some say, "This ointment should not be brought north, for anyone who does so will bring disaster upon themselves." During the Great Harmony period, Han Yue, the protector of An Nan, obtained the ointment and took it back with him. Someone told him, "The people of the south say this ointment should not be brought north, and you are taking it away. Will you not regret it later?" Han Yue did not listen and eventually took it back. Later, Han Yue became the chief of the Gold Bearer, and that year, the capital was in turmoil. Han Yue, associating with Zheng Zhu, ultimately had his entire clan executed. Is it because of the Jade Dragon Ointment that brought the disaster? Therefore, those going south dare not bring it north.

A Fleshy Fungus Underground A man from Lanling, known as Xaio Yi Ren, whose name has been lost to history. Once he took the imperial examination but failed, so he burned his books and retired to live by a lake, following a Taoist to learn the ways of immortality. He abstained from food and breathed deeply, bending and stretching his limbs every morning, hoping to extend his life. After more than ten years, his hair turned completely white, his complexion became dry, and his back hunched; some of his teeth had fallen out. One day, looking at himself in the mirror, he suddenly became furious and said, "I abandoned fame and profit, hid myself in the fields, abstained from food and breathed deeply, hoping to achieve immortality. Now I am as thin and weak as this, is this what I intended?" He then returned to live in Ye, learning to trade for a profit of one-tenth. Over several years, he accumulated great wealth and became a wealthy family. Later, while repairing his garden and digging the ground, he found something resembling a human hand, fleshy and moist, slightly red in color. Xaio Yi Ren was startled and exclaimed, "Isn't this a sign of impending misfortune? I have heard that where the Tai Sui resides, earthworks cannot be undertaken. If someone were to violate this, there would be a fleshy object emerging from below, certainly an omen of bad luck."

--- ### Final Translation Now that it exists, what should be done? I heard that if one gets to eat it, perhaps one can avoid [some misfortune]. So they cooked and ate it, and the taste was extremely delicious, almost finished. From then on, the hermit's hearing and vision became clear, his strength grew stronger, his appearance younger, his bald hair turned black and grew again, and his fallen teeth grew back in pairs. The hermit silently wondered at these changes and dared not tell others. Later, a Taoist came to Ye and met the hermit, amazed and said, "Have you ever taken immortal elixir, Master? Your spirit and appearance are so clear and bright." The Taoist then examined the hermit's pulse. After a while, he said, "You must have eaten lingzhi mushrooms. Lingzhi mushrooms resemble human hands, are fat and moist, and have a slight red color." The hermit realized the truth and told the Taoist, who congratulated him, saying, "Your lifespan can match that of turtles and cranes. However, you should not live in the mundane world; you should retire to the mountains, abandon worldly affairs, and achieve immortality." The hermit was delighted and followed the advice, leaving and disappearing without a trace. In Donglu, there was an old house with a grand hall and high steps. However, many people who lived there died suddenly, so it had been vacant and locked for a long time. Right Censor Lu Qian, during the Tang Dynasty, was responsible for inspecting the eastern region and often wanted to buy the house. Someone warned him, "This house is haunted and not suitable for living in." Lu Qian confidently said, "I can dispel the supernatural phenomena."

One night later, Ruan and his followers slept together in the hall, ordering all servants to stay outside the door. The followers were brave, fierce, and skilled in archery, so they held bows and arrows and sat under the front veranda. As the night deepened, they heard someone knocking on the door. The followers immediately asked who it was, and the response came: "General Liu has sent a letter to Magistrate Lu." Ruan did not respond. Soon after, a sheet of paper was thrown under the veranda, and the writing appeared as if it had been written with a wet brush, delicate and fine. Ruan ordered the followers to look at it, and the writing said: "My family has lived here for many years. The inner rooms and verandas are all my dwelling; the door gods and household spirits are all my servants. And you have suddenly entered my home—how is that right? If you have a place, can I enter yours? Since you are not afraid of me, are you not ashamed in your heart? Leave quickly, and do not bring upon yourself the shame of defeat." After reading the message, the paper scattered in the air, like flying ashes. Soon after, they heard someone speaking again: "General Liu wishes to see Magistrate Lu." Then, a ghost appeared, towering dozens of fathoms tall, standing in the courtyard, holding a ladle. Ruan's follower immediately drew the bow and shot, hitting what the ghost was holding. The ghost retreated, dropping the ladle. After a long time, it came back, bending over the veranda and standing, pulling its head and peering at it, looking very strange. The follower shot again, hitting its chest. The ghost was startled, seeming to be afraid, and then turned east and left.

Until dawn, the official ordered the traces to be investigated. They reached the eastern empty space of the house, where they saw a willow tree over a hundred feet tall, with a single arrow piercing through it. This is what is known as the Willow General. The official cut down the wood of the tree. From then on, the occupants of the house were unharmed. After more than a year, the house was rebuilt, and a ladle about ten feet long was found under the roof tiles, with an arrow piercing through its handle. This was the ladle held by the general. --- During the Dahe period, there was an official named Jiangxia. His official residence once had strange occurrences. Every night, he saw a giant. The entire body of the giant was black and very bright. Seeing it caused fear and led to illness and death. Later, there was a man named Xu Yuanzhang, skilled at seeing ghosts. The official ordered Xu Yuanzhang to use talismanic arts to summon and investigate. One night, Xu Yuanzhang sat under the western veranda of the hall, and the giant suddenly appeared. Xu Yuanzhang threw a talisman at it, which hit the giant's arm, making a loud sound, and it fell to the ground. The giant then left. Xu Yuanzhang examined the fallen arm and found it to be a dried branch of wood. By the next day, a servant boy told Xu Yuanzhang, "There is a dead tree in the eastern corner of the hall. Your talisman is now on it." He went to look at it. The tree had a slightly broken branch, indeed the arm cut off by the giant. He then cut it down and burned it. The house was then free of strange occurrences.

○窦宽 唐扶风窦宽者，家于梁山。大和八年秋，自大理评事解县推盐使判官，罢职退归。因治园屋，命家仆伐一树。既伐，而有血滂溜，汪然注地，食顷而尽。宽异之，且知为怪。由是闭门绝人事。至明年冬十一月，郑注、李训反，宽与注连，遂诛死于左禁军中。 **Translation:** ○Duan Kuan Duan Kuan, a man from Fufeng, lived in Liangshan. In the autumn of the eighth year of the Dahe era (834 AD), he retired from his position as an administrative judge for the salt distribution office in Jie County, which he had held under the title of "Dali Pingshi." After leaving his post, he returned home and began renovating his garden and house. He ordered his servant to cut down a tree. After the tree was felled, blood flowed from it, pooling on the ground and disappearing within a short time. Duan Kuan found this strange and realized it was a supernatural occurrence. He then closed his doors and cut off contact with others. In the eleventh month of the following year (December 835), Zheng Zhu and Li Xun staged a coup, and Duan Kuan was implicated in the conspiracy and was executed in the Left Forbidden Army. ○吴偃 有厉泉县民吴偃，家于田野间。有一女十岁余，一夕，忽遁去，莫知所往。后数余日，偃梦其父谓偃曰：“汝女今在东北隅，盖木神为崇。”偃惊而寤。至明日，即于东北隅穷其迹，果闻有呼吟之事，偃视之，见其女有一穴内。口甚小，然其中稍宽敞。傍有古槐木，盘根极大。于是挈之而归，然兀若沉醉者。会有李道士至，偃请符术呵禁。其女忽瞬而语曰：“地东北有槐木，木有神，引某自树腹空入地下穴内，故某病。”于是伐其树。后数日，女病始愈。 **Translation:** ○Wu Yan Wu Yan, a man from Liquan County, lived in the countryside. He had a daughter who was over ten years old. One night, she suddenly disappeared, and no one knew where she had gone. Several days later, Wu Yan dreamed that his father told him, "Your daughter is now in the northeast corner; it seems that a tree spirit has caused this." Wu Yan woke up in shock. The next day, he went to the northeast corner to investigate the traces and indeed heard sounds of calling and moaning. He saw his daughter inside a hole. The entrance was very small, but inside it was somewhat spacious. There was an ancient locust tree nearby with very large roots. He then carried her back home, but she seemed dazed as if drunk. Later, a Taoist named Li arrived, and Wu Yan asked him for a talisman and exorcism. His daughter suddenly opened her eyes and spoke, "There is a locust tree in the northeast; the tree has a spirit, it led me into the hollow inside the tree to an underground hole, and that's why I am sick." He then cut down the tree. After several days, his daughter's illness began to improve.

A Tale of Dong Guan (Part Two) There was a man named Dong Guan who had once been a monk and lived in a Buddhist temple in Taiyuan. In the summer of the seventh year of the Taihe era, he traveled south with his cousin Wang Sheng to Jingchu. Later, they planned to enter Chang'an, and on their way, they passed through Shangyu. One night, they stayed at a mountain inn. Wang Sheng had already fallen asleep, but Dong Guan remained awake. Suddenly, he saw something emerge from under the candlelight, then cover the candle. It resembled a human hand but lacked fingers. On closer inspection, there seemed to be something outside the candlelight. Dong Guan quickly called out to Wang Sheng, who got up, and the hand then disappeared. Dong Guan warned Wang, saying, "Be careful not to sleep. The demon will come again." He held a spear and sat waiting for it. After a long time, Wang Sheng said, "Where is the demon? Brother, you are mistaken." They then went to bed. Soon after, a creature more than five feet long stood blocking the candlelight, without hands or a face. Dong Guan became even more afraid and called out to Wang Sheng again. Wang Sheng was angry and did not get up. Dong Guan struck it with the spear, and its body seemed to be pierced by grass, with the spear following into it, but it could not be taken out with force. Soon, it withdrew. Dong Guan worried it would come again and, until dawn, dared not sleep. The next day, he visited the innkeeper, who said, "A few miles west there is an ancient pine tree that often becomes a demon. I suspect it was what you saw." Immediately, Dong Guan and Wang Sheng went to search, and indeed found the ancient pine tree, with a spear piercing through its branches and leaves. The innkeeper said, "People say this has been an evil spirit for a long time, but never seen its true form. Now, we believe it." They quickly took an axe and cut it down completely.

In the west of JinYang, there was a temple called Tongzi Temple, located beyond the outskirts of the pasture. During the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Deng Gui who temporarily resided in the temple. One autumn, he gathered with several friends for an overnight stay. After closing the door, they suddenly saw a hand enter through the window. The hand was yellow and very thin. The others looked at it with fear, but Deng Gui was not afraid. He opened the window and heard a sound of singing or whistling. Deng Gui did not find it strange. He asked, "Who are you?" The hand replied, "I have been living in seclusion in the mountains for many years. Tonight I am indulging in the pleasures of the wind and moon. I heard that the master is here, so I came to pay respects. I truly do not deserve a seat at the master's table, but I would be content to sit under the window and listen to the master and guests talk." Deng Gui agreed. They sat down, and they talked and laughed happily. After a while, the hand said goodbye and was about to leave, saying to Deng Gui, "I will come again tomorrow night. I hope the master does not reject me." After it left, Deng Gui and his friends discussed, "This must be a ghost. If we do not trace its tracks, it will surely cause trouble." So they wove silk into hundreds of fathoms of rope and waited for its return, intending to bind it. The next night, the hand appeared again, entering through the window. Deng Gui immediately bound its arm with the rope, which was firmly and could not be untied. The hand asked from outside the window, "What crime has led to my being bound? Where is the discussion? Could there be regret?" Then it pulled the rope and left. The next day, Deng Gui and his friends traced its tracks to a grapevine about a hundred steps north of the temple. The grapevine was very lush and abundant, and the rope was tied to its branch. There were leaves that resembled human hands, indeed what was seen in the window. They then dug up its roots and burned them.

Duan Zhao In the south of Lingshi County, there were often supernatural phenomena at night, so no one in the village dared to pass through that area after dark. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong (Yuanhe era), Dong Shujing was the governor of Hexi. At that time, Liu Zhao from Pengcheng held a temporary position as a magistrate in Xiaoyi. Liu Zhao once wrote a letter to Dong Shujing, which greatly angered him, leading Liu Zhao to abandon his post. He entered the Fen River Pass and arrived at the south of Lingshi County at night. There he encountered a person standing by the roadside, who had an extremely unusual appearance. Liu Zhao's horse was startled and he fell off, taking a long time to get up. The person by the roadside then took off Liu Zhao's clothes and put them on himself. Liu Zhao thought he was being robbed and did not dare to resist. He then walked westward, covering about ten miles, and arrived at an inn, where he told the story. The innkeeper said, "There are supernatural phenomena in the south of the town at night, but they are not robbers." The next day, someone who came from the south of the county said to Liu Zhao, "There are weeds in the fields south of the county, which look like a person, wearing a green robe, isn't that strange?" Liu Zhao went to see it and found that it was indeed his own robe. The villagers finally realized that the supernatural phenomena were caused by the weeds. As a result, all the weeds were burned, and the supernatural phenomena ceased.

○梁生 In the west of Xingping during the Tang Dynasty, there was a villa belonging to Liáng Shēng, and in his garden, there were more than ten pear trees. In the tenth month of winter in the fourth year of Dàhé, after the snow had melted, the pears suddenly began to bloom, fragrant and luxuriant. Liáng Shēng found it very strange and considered it a good omen. Wéi Shì said to Liáng Shēng, "Trees bloom in spring and flourish, but wither in winter, which is their natural cycle. Now it is the opposite; how can this be considered a good omen?" Liáng Shēng was not pleased when he heard this. After a month, Liáng Shēng's father died. ○赵生 During the Tianbao era, there was a man named Zhao Shēng, whose ancestors were renowned for their literary achievements. Zhao Shēng had several brothers, all of whom entered officialdom through the imperial examinations. However, Zhao Shēng was simple-minded and slow-witted. Although he read, he could not understand the meaning of individual sentences. As a result, even when he was grown, he had not yet been recommended as a candidate for the provincial examination. He often attended banquets with his brothers and friends, where the room was filled with red and green attire, but only Zhao Shēng wore plain white clothing, and he was very unhappy. When the wine flowed, someone mocked him, and Zhao Shēng became even more ashamed and angry. The next day, he abandoned his home and fled, hiding in Jinyang Mountain and building a thatched cottage. Zhao Shēng had over a hundred volumes of books, which he carried to the mountain, where he studied during the day and rested at night. Despite the cold and heat, he ate millet and wore coarse cloth, and he did not fear hard work. But Zhao Shēng remained dim-witted, and the harder he worked, the less he achieved. He became increasingly angry, but he never changed his resolve.

After more than ten days, an old man dressed in coarse clothes came to visit him. He then said to the man, "My son, living in the deep mountains and reading ancient books, do you have aspirations for official positions? Although, the longer you study, the more you cannot even parse sentences or discuss them in detail, how severely are you obstructed?" The man replied, "I am not intelligent; I consider myself old and useless, so I entered the deep mountains to read books for my own pleasure. Although I cannot grasp their subtleties, I still wish to die for my aspirations and not disgrace my ancestors. How could I even think about official positions?" The old man said, "Your aspirations are very firm. Although I have no skills, I can be of some help to you, just visit me once." He then asked where the man lived. The old man said, "I am a descendant of the Duan family, living under a large tree in Shanxi." After he finished speaking, he suddenly disappeared. The man was surprised and thought it was a ghost, so he went straight to Shanxi to find his traces. Indeed, there was a large, flourishing paulownia tree. The man said, "Is this not the descendant of the Duan family?" He then took a spade and dug under it, finding a ginseng root over a foot long, which greatly resembled the old man he had met. The man said, "I have heard that ginseng can transform into strange things and can cure diseases." He then boiled it and ate it. From then on, he was suddenly enlightened, and he could understand all the books he read. After a year or so, he passed the imperial examination in the classics. He held several official positions before he died.

The Dragon of Xingqing Pool Emperor Xuanzong of Tang once lived in the Xingqing Palace, where he was known as the "hidden dragon." After ascending the throne, a small dragon appeared in the Xingqing Pool, wandering outside the palace moat. It had an extraordinary and winding form, displaying a posture of leaping and escaping. The palace ladies and eunuchs all watched in awe. Later, when Emperor Xuanzong traveled to Shu, his imperial carriage was about to depart. On the eve of his journey, the dragon emerged from the pool, riding on a white cloud, leaping into the sky and heading southwest. The court officials gathered around to witness this miraculous event. When the emperor reached the Jialing River and was about to cross on a boat, he saw the small dragon flying alongside the boat. All the court officials saw it. The emperor shed tears involuntarily, turning to his attendants and saying, "This is the dragon from my Xingqing Pool!" He ordered wine to be poured as an offering and personally prayed to it. The dragon then shook its mane and flew away from the water.

The Former Minister of War Xiao Xin, who served as the Governor of Jingzhao, was known for his administrative skills. During a severe drought in the capital, the oppressive heat and sickness were widespread. Emperor Daizong ordered the high officials to perform rituals to the mountains and rivers, but after over a month, the heat only intensified. At the time, the Indian monk Fokong Sanzang, a renowned practitioner of Buddhist rituals, resided at Jingzhu Temple. Known for his ability to summon rain through spiritual practices, Fokong was approached by Xiao Xin, who said, "For months now, the sun has been scorching, and the emperor is deeply concerned. He has stopped music and reduced food consumption, worrying about the poor harvest and the suffering of the people. I hope, Master, that you will perform the ritual to bring rain." Fokong replied, "It's easy. However, summoning rain through dragons may cause harm to crops due to the thunder and lightning, and it may not benefit the fields." Xiao Xin argued, "Even if the rain cannot nourish the crops, it will at least cool the heat and alleviate the people's suffering. I beg you not to refuse." Reluctantly, Fokong instructed his disciples to prepare a small piece of fragrant wood and a silk dragon, about a foot long, and placed an incense burner with incense water in front. He then recited spells and made loud invocations. After a short while, he handed the silk dragon to Xiao Xin and said, "Throw this into Qujiang. Do it quickly and return without being caught in the storm." Xiao Xin followed the instructions and threw the dragon into the river. Soon, a small white dragon, about a foot long, emerged from the water, shaking its mane and scales. It quickly grew to several zhang in length, appearing like a white ribbon stretching across the sky. Xiao Xin urged his horse and rode away, but before he covered even a few dozen steps, dark clouds gathered and a heavy rain began to fall. By the time he reached Yongchongli, the rain had turned into a flood, as if a dam had broken.

Ren Xu of Le'an, during the early Jianzhong period of the Tang Dynasty, loved reading and disliked worldly affairs. He resided in a deep mountain with the intention to live there until the end. One day, he closed the door and sat during the day. An elderly man wearing a yellow robe, with a very handsome appearance, walked with a cane and came to visit. Ren Xu invited him to sit and talk. After a while, Ren Xu noticed that the old man's speech was slow and his face was dejected, indicating he was in distress. Ren Xu asked, "Why is your face dejected? Is there illness in your family, and you deeply worry about it?" The old man replied, "Indeed so. I have been waiting for you to ask this question for a long time. I am not a human; I am a dragon. One mile to the west, there is a large pond, my home for hundreds of years. Now, I am troubled by one person, and disaster is imminent. Only you can save me from death, so I have come to report this. You are fortunate to ask me, so I can speak now."

○ Ren Xu In the early years of the Jianzhong era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man from Le'an named Ren Xu, who loved reading and disliked worldly affairs. He lived deep in the mountains, with the resolve to remain there until the end of his days. One day, he was meditating in solitude with the door closed, when an old man came knocking at his door to visit. The old man wore yellow robes, had an exceptionally refined appearance, and arrived dragging a staff. Ren Xu invited him to sit and conversed with him. After some time, Ren Xu was surprised by the old man's hesitant speech and dejected expression, sensing that he was troubled by something. He then asked the old man, "Why do you look so dispirited? Is it not because you have something to worry about? Or perhaps someone in your family is ill, and you are deeply concerned for them?" The old man said, "Indeed it is so. My worry has been waiting for your question for a long time. And I am not a human, but a dragon. One li to the west is a great spring, my home for hundreds of years. Now, I am being tormented by a man, and calamity is about to befall me. Only you can save me from death, so I have come to inform you. You have now fortunately asked me, so I am able to speak."

Xu replied, "I am just a commoner, knowing only about poetry, books, rites, and music; other arts I cannot understand. But how can I save the old man from his misfortune?" The old man said, "Just teach me the words; not relying on other arts, only requiring dozens of words." Xu said, "I wish to learn." The old man said, "Two days later, I wish you to come to the spring in the morning. At noon, there will be a Taoist priest coming from the west, this is the one who will harm me. The Taoist priest will drain the water from my spring and will kill me. You wait until the spring water is drained, then loudly call out: 'Heaven has a decree, those who kill the yellow dragon will die.' After saying this, the spring will fill up. The Taoist priest will surely use another art, you should call out again. Three times in this manner, I will be able to save my life. I will surely reward you, so please don't worry about anything else." Xu agreed. Later, he prayed and thanked very earnestly, for a long time before leaving.

Two days later, Xu went to Shanxi and indeed found a large pond. He immediately sat by the pond to wait for it. At noon, suddenly a cloud drifted down from the west onto the pond. A tall and slender Taoist emerged from the cloud, about ten feet tall, and stood on the shore of the pond. He took out several black talismans from his sleeve and threw them into the pond. Soon, the water in the pond completely dried up, revealing a yellow dragon lying flat on the sand. Xu then shouted sternly, "Heaven has decreed, whoever kills the yellow dragon shall die." After he finished speaking, the water in the pond overflowed again. The Taoist became very angry and immediately took out several red talismans from his sleeve and threw them into the pond. The water in the pond dried up again. He then shouted loudly, repeating the same words, and the water in the pond overflowed again. The Taoist became even angrier and, for the duration of a meal, took out more than ten red talismans, threw them into the air, and they all turned into red clouds, entering the pond and causing the water to immediately dry up. He shouted the same words again, and the water in the pond overflowed once more. The Taoist turned to speak to Xu, "I have been searching for ten years to find this dragon to eat. Why are you, a scholar, saving this strange creature?" He angrily scolded a few words and then left. Xu also returned to the mountains.

That evening, a dream came to the old man from before, who came to thank him, saying, "I was saved by your virtue, otherwise I would have nearly died in the hands of a Taoist priest. Deeply moved by your sincerity, what can I say in return? Today I present you with a pearl, which you can find at the bank of the pool, to express my deep gratitude." Xu went to look for it, and indeed found a pearl the size of an inch in the grass at the bank of the pool, shining brightly and clearly, almost impossible to recognize. Xu later went specially to Guangling City, where a foreigner saw it and said, "This is truly the treasure of the black dragon. And no one in the world can obtain it." He bought it for several million as the price. ○Lu Yuanyu The former governor of An Prefecture, Lu Yuanyu, before he entered officialdom, once set up banners, flags, and images during the Zhongyuan Festival, placing the Ullambana Festival among them. Soon he heard a chirping sound in the pot, and Lu Yuanyu looked, and saw a small dragon about an inch long, with graceful and unique appearance, lovely and charming. Then he washed it with water, and the dragon stretched its legs and shook its mane, growing several feet long. Lu Yuanyu was greatly frightened. White clouds rose from the pot, and the dragon followed the clouds and departed. Lu Yuanyu was the father of Han. ○Li Xiu Li Xiu, the Tang Dynasty observer of Zhexi, was the governor of Jiang County in the seventh year of Yuanhe. That year, there was a dragon seen in the county of Longmen under his jurisdiction, and at that time there were thousands of spectators. The county reported the situation to the Taifu. At that time, Zhang Hongjing, the chancellor of Hedong Prefecture, was the military governor of Hedong, and his son, the former governor of Shuzhou, once wrote an article to praise the event.

○Lu Jun Chang Formerly, when Lu Jun Chang, the Cishi (censorial) official of the Eastern Capital and Langzhong (counselor) of the Sacrificial Department from Fanyang, was a commoner, he resided as a guest in the Han River region. One day, he drove alone into the countryside and saw two white dogs with long waists and full chests, floating as if they were falling, both running through the fields. Lu was surprised by their unusual appearance compared to ordinary dogs, so he stopped his horse to observe. Soon, the dogs both jumped into a pool. Shortly after, the waves of the pool surged, and two white dragons rose from the pool, filling the sky with thick clouds and causing a great storm with wind and thunder. Lu was greatly frightened, whipped his horse, and returned home. Before he had traveled several miles, his clothes were soaked. He then realized that the two dogs were actually dragons. ○Fa Xi Temple In the southeast of Zhengyang Prefecture, there is Fa Xi Temple, which is 100 li away from the prefecture but located on the western bank of the Wei River. At the end of the Yuanhe era during the Tang Dynasty, the temple monks frequently dreamed of a white dragon coming from the Wei River, stopping at the western pillar of the Buddha hall, coiling and twisting for a long time, then moving directly eastward, and it would rain the next day. This happened several times. The monks found this strange and told others about it. Someone said, "This blessed place is where gods and deities reside, certainly the dwelling of dragons. And the Buddhist temple is also a place where dragons rest. Therefore, in Buddhism, there are the Eight Classes of Celestial Beings, including dragons, whose meaning is clear. Moreover, a temple in the suburbs with clear and spacious halls is an appropriate place for dragons to rest. Originally, a clay dragon should be placed between the temple pillars to remember the dream." The monks summoned workers, mixed soil to make a clay dragon, described its appearance in detail, and placed it at the western pillar of the Buddha hall. When the work was completed, the clay dragon greatly resembled the appearance of a cloud, winding with scales and fins, fully expressing its wonder, even surpassing the best paintings.

In the early years of the Changqing era, a resident of the Changqing Temple, lying down outside the temple gate, saw a creature emerge directly from the west pavilion, floating up like ascending clouds, and fly out of the temple, disappearing towards the water. Just before dawn, it finally returned to the west pavilion. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a white dragon. The next day, he informed the temple monks, who were surprised by it. Several days later, the monks went to a village gathering for a communal meal and did not return until noon. When they entered the temple to check, the dragon statue had disappeared. The monks, both sighing and marveling, looked at each other and said, "This is a dragon, even though it is made of clay, it can still change in countless ways. It goes without anyone knowing where it is going, and it returns without anyone knowing where it came from. Is it truly a divine creature?" Later that evening, dark clouds arose over the Wei River and soon approached the temple. Suddenly, something leaped out from the clouds, pointing towards the west pavilion and entering. The temple monks were frightened and looked at it, seeing the dragon statue on the west pillar. They approached to look at it, and the dragon's curly mane, scales, and horns appeared as if it had been thoroughly wet. From then on, they fastened it with an iron chain. Later, there were droughts and floods in the village, and they prayed to it, and it responded as if to an echo.

At the beginning of the Changqing era, a resident of the temple was lying down at the outer gate when he saw an object emerging directly from the western pavilion, drifting as if ascending into the clouds, then swiftly flying out of the temple and appearing to head toward the water. As night was nearly over, it finally returned beneath the western pavilion. Upon careful observation, he realized it was indeed a white dragon. The next day, he informed the temple monks, who were greatly amazed. A few days later, all the monks went to a village gathering for a communal meal and returned at midday. Upon entering the hall, they found the dragon image already missing. The monks, both sighing and marveling, looked at one another and said: “This dragon, though made of earth, still has the power to change form without limit. Its departure is unknown where it goes, and its arrival is unknown where it comes from—could it truly be a supernatural being?” By evening, dark clouds arose over the Wei River. Soon, they began to approach the temple. Suddenly, something leapt from the clouds, pointed toward the western pavilion, and entered. The monks, frightened and startled, looked and saw the dragon image already on the western column. They approached to examine it and saw that its curling mane, scales, and horns seemed all to be wet. From then on, they secured it with an iron chain. Afterwards, whenever there were droughts or floods in the village, they prayed to it, and it responded as swiftly as shadow follows form.

○Dragon Temple The Fen River flows through Taiyuan and runs southward, with two bridges along its course. Once, a dragon was seen beneath the southern bridge, leading to the construction of a dragon temple under the bridge. During the time when the former chancellor Linghu Chu was in charge of the northern capital, a dragon emerged from the temple, causing all the people and women in the city to gather and watch. After a short while, the dragon struggled and flew away, and soon there was a thunderstorm. The following autumn, the Fen River overflowed, and a white snake emerged from the temple. As it emerged, the temple buildings collapsed, and the bridge also broke. This occurred in the early years of the Tahe era of the Tang dynasty. ○Li Zheng Li Zheng, a member of the imperial family from Longxi, lived in Guole. Zheng was young and widely learned, skilled in writing, and recommended by the state and county at the age of twenty. He was known as a famous scholar of his time. In the spring of the tenth year of the Tianbao era, he passed the Jinshi examination under the list of the Right Censor Yang Mo. After several years, he was appointed as the magistrate of Jiangnan. Zheng was arrogant due to his talent and could not lower himself to be a low-ranking official, often feeling depressed and unhappy. At gatherings with his colleagues, after they had drunk deeply, he would look around and say to his officials, "I am now among you low-ranking officials!" His subordinates all envied him. After resigning from his post, he closed his door and did not communicate with anyone for nearly a year. Later, due to the need for food and clothing, he prepared and traveled east to Wu and Chu to seek favor with the local officials. The people of Wu and Chu had heard of his reputation for a long time, and upon his arrival, they all opened their halls to welcome him. They feasted and played very happily. When he was about to leave, they all gave him generous gifts to fill his bags. Zheng stayed in Wu and Chu for nearly a year and received many gifts.

He was traveling westward to the region of Guo and Luo, but before reaching his destination, he stopped at an inn by the River Luo. Suddenly, he fell ill and went mad, whipping and beating his servants. The servants could not bear the suffering. For about ten days, his illness grew increasingly severe. Without delay, he ran wildly at night, and no one knew where he was going. His household servants followed his path and waited for him, but after a whole month, they finally did not return. Thus, the servants drove away his riding horse, took his bags and belongings, and fled far away. The following year, Chen Commandery's Censorial Inspector Yuan You was sent by imperial decree to the Lingnan region. He arrived at the boundary of Shangyu by riding in an official carriage. In the morning, he was about to depart, and his postal official said, "There are tigers on the road, fierce and eating people, so those passing by here do not dare to proceed unless it is during the day. It is still early, I wish to stop the carriage for now, definitely cannot proceed." Yuan You became angry and said, "I am an envoy of the Son of Heaven, with many horses, can wild beasts in the mountains and marshes cause harm?" He then ordered the carriage to depart.

After walking not quite one li, indeed, a tiger suddenly emerged from the grass. The narrator was greatly startled. Soon after, the tiger hid itself in the grass and spoke in a human voice, saying, "How strange! It almost hurt my old friend." The narrator recognized the voice as resembling that of Li Zheng. The narrator had once been a fellow candidate with Li Zheng in the imperial examinations, and they had a deep bond, but it had been many years since they last met. Upon hearing his voice, the narrator was both surprised and puzzled, unable to understand what was happening. He asked, "Who are you? Are you not my old friend from Longxi?" The tiger groaned several times, as if in lamentation, and then said, "I am Li Zheng. Kindly stay a moment and speak with me." The narrator dismounted his horse and asked, "Li Zheng, Li Zheng, why have you come to this?" The tiger replied, "Since our parting, communication has been interrupted for a long time. Are you glad to be unharmed? Now, why are you going to such a place? Earlier, I saw you with two officials pushing you forward, a postal servant carrying an official bag leading the way. Isn't it for the position of censor and a mission?" The narrator said, "Recently, I have been fortunate enough to hold the position of censor. Now, I am being sent on a mission to Lingnan." The tiger said, "You have established yourself through your literary talents, reaching a high position in the court. This is indeed a great achievement. Moreover, the Censorate is known for its high moral standards and strict discipline, and the wise ruler carefully selects those who serve in this capacity."

I am pleased that my old friend lives here, it is very fortunate." The official said, "In the past, I and you became famous in the same year, our friendship was deep and close, different from ordinary friends. Since the separation of sounds and appearances, time has passed like a stream, I often think of and admire your appearance and demeanor, both heart and eyes are broken. I did not expect to hear your words of remembrance today. However, why don't you see me, and hide among the grasses? Is this how the relationship of old friends should be!" The tiger said, "I am no longer a human, how can I see you?" The official immediately asked about the matter. The tiger said, "My former life was spent in Wu and Chu, last year I returned, on the road near your tomb, I suddenly fell ill and went mad, running into the mountains and valleys, soon I walked with my hands on the ground, from then on, I felt my heart became more fierce, and my strength doubled, when I looked at my thighs and upper arms, I saw hair growing. I also saw people wearing crowns and robes walking on the road, carrying and running, flying with wings, and galloping with fur, I wanted to eat them. After I reached the southern part of Han Yin, because of hunger, I encountered a man with fat and strong muscles, I captured and chewed him up immediately. From then on, I rarely acted normally. I did not forget my family and friends, but because of my actions, I was punished by the gods, one day I transformed into a strange beast, ashamed to be seen by people, so I no longer have contact with people. Alas! I and you passed the imperial examinations in the same year, our friendship has always been strong.

Today, I held the mandate of heaven, honoring my relatives and friends. Yet, I have hidden myself in the wilderness, forever bidding farewell to the world of humanity. I leap and cry to the heavens, pull and weep to the earth, ruined and not used—Is this truly fate?” Thus, I called out, sighed, and lamented, barely able to bear it, and then I wept. Gui asked, “Now that you have become an outsider, how can you still speak like a human?” The tiger replied, “Though my form has changed, my heart has greatly enlightened. Thus, there is collision, filled with fear and hatred, which is hard to fully express. Fortunately, old friends remember me and deeply forgive my outrageous guilt, which is also my wish. However, when you return from the south, I will meet you again, and I will surely forget my entire life. At that time, I will view your body as just an object on my machine. You should also strictly guard and prepare, so that it does not become my crime and be mocked by the wise and virtuous.” He then said, “I and you are truly friends who transcend form. And I will entrust something to you, is that acceptable?” Gui said, “Old friends from the past, how could it be unacceptable? I just do not know what matter it is, I wish to hear it all.” The tiger said, “If you do not allow me, how dare I speak. Now that you have allowed me, how could I keep it hidden? Initially, when I was in an inn, I became ill and went mad. After entering the wilderness, the attendants drove me away, taking my horse and clothes, leaving me empty-handed.

My wife and children are still in Guo and Luo, how could they think I have transformed into a different kind? If you return from the south, deliver a letter to visit my wife and children, just say I am dead, do not mention today's events. Please remember this. He also said, "I have no assets or business in this world, I have a young son, it is indeed difficult to manage on my own. You hold a position among the officials, you have always upheld your principles, the bond we shared in the past, how could anyone else surpass you? I hope you will consider his loneliness and weakness, to provide him with relief when he is in need, do not let him die of starvation on the road, this would also be a great act of kindness." After he finished speaking, he cried again. Guan also cried and said, "Guan and you share the same joys and sorrows. Then your son is also Guan's son. I will do my best to fulfill your trust, and why should I doubt that it will not happen?" The tiger said, "I have several dozen old writings that have not been published, although there are some remaining drafts, they are all scattered. You should transcribe them for me, I truly do not dare to place them among the works of others, however, it is valuable to pass them down to future generations." Guan immediately called his servant to get a pen, and wrote according to his dictation, about twenty chapters, the writing is very profound, and the reasoning is very far-reaching. Guan read and sighed repeatedly. The tiger said, "These are my lifelong aspirations, how could I dare to hope they will be passed down?" He also said, "You are on a mission from the emperor, you should be very busy, now you have stayed for a long time at the post station, feeling extremely anxious. To part ways forever, the regret of going different paths, how can it be expressed!" Guan also bid him farewell, after a long time, he finally left.

Gua returned from the south and then took the initiative to order the sending of books and a hat as a funeral gift, which he entrusted to Zengzi's son. A month later, Zengzi's son came from Guo and Luo to the capital, visiting Gua's door to request the coffin of his father. Gua, unable to refuse, wrote a detailed report on the matter. Later, Gua distributed his own salary equally to Zengzi's family, preventing them from starving or freezing. Gua later reached the position of Vice Minister of War.

○Hebei Fui There was a man named Fui who served as the governor of Hebei during the Zhenyuan era. Fui was greedy and harsh, causing much suffering to the people of Hebei. He frequently borrowed gold from a Buddha statue in a temple, amounting to several jin, but never paid it back. The monks dared not speak to the governor. Soon after, Fui died in his office. On that day, a cow in the temple gave birth to a calf. The calf's head had white hair like threads, and the characters "Fui" appeared on its forehead. The temple monks gathered to observe it and sighed, saying, "Fui often borrowed gold from this temple's Buddha statue but never returned it. Today's events, what do they signify?" Fui's family heard about it and immediately exchanged the calf for another cow. Once they got home, they ordered the characters to be cut off. However, they soon reappeared. When the calf reached Fui's house, despite being fed with hay and grain, it eventually refused to eat. Fui's family found this strange and finally returned the calf to the temple. ○Emperor Xuanzong's Dragon Horse Between the Hai and Dai mountains, there is a black and yellow stone. It is said that eating it can lead to immortality. Emperor Xuanzong once ordered the governor of Linzi to collect and present it annually. In the twenty-seventh year of Kaiyuan, Li Yong of Jiangxia was the governor of Linzi. That autumn, he went into the mountains to collect the black and yellow stone. Suddenly, he met an old man who was very handsome, with a dignified and refined appearance, thick beard, wearing a rough cloth robe, emerging from the left side of the path, and tapping Li Yong's horse, saying, "Lord, you personally collect herbs, isn't it to extend the life of the holy emperor?" Li Yong replied, "Yes." The old man said, "The holy emperor will obtain a dragon horse, then he will enjoy the kingdom for ten thousand years, no need to collect herbs." Li Yong asked, "Where is the dragon horse?" The old man replied, "It should be in the outskirts of Qi and Lu. If obtained, it will be a sign of peace."

Even the auspicious signs of the qilin, phoenix, turtle, and dragon are not sufficient to compare with its auspiciousness. Yuan ordered to drive the carriage behind it, but it suddenly disappeared. Yuan was greatly amazed and turned to say to his subordinate, "Is this not a divine being?" He immediately ordered the official Wang Qianzhen to search for the dragon horse between Qi and Lu. In the summer of the fifth month of the twenty-ninth year of Kaiyuan, Qianzhen indeed found a horse at the home of Ma Huien, a resident of Beihai Commandery. The horse had a white mane, scales on its sides, a mane and tail like the ridges of a dragon, and neighed with the sound of a flute. It could travel three hundred miles in a day. Qianzhen asked about its origin, and Huien said, "I had only a mare, which often bathed in the Zhi River, and then conceived and gave birth. Therefore, I called it the Dragon's Son." Qianzhen immediately reported this to Yuan. Yuan was very pleased and submitted a report detailing the matter to the emperor. The emperor was greatly delighted, ordered it to be kept in the imperial stables, and treated it differently and kept it specially. He also ordered artists to draw its likeness and distribute it to be shown both inside and outside the court.

In the early years of the Tianbao era, there was a man named Wang Xun who lived in the Yanshou Quarter of Chang'an. One evening, several people brought food to meet at Wang Xun's residence. After they had eaten, a large hand suddenly emerged from the shadow of the candle. Wang Xun and his friends were both frightened and watched it. The hand was black and had many hairs on it. Soon, a voice came from outside the shadow, saying, "You have a gathering, but you can't even invite me once? I wish to have some meat placed in my palm." Wang Xun could not understand the reason, so he gave it some meat. The hand then withdrew. A short while later, it extended its arm again and said, "Thank you for giving me meat; now it is almost finished. I wish you would give me more." Wang Xun placed more meat in its palm, and it withdrew again. They then discussed among themselves, "This must be a strange occurrence. We should wait for it to come again and cut off its arm." Soon enough, it returned, and Wang Xun drew his sword and cut it off. The arm fell, and the rest of the body disappeared. When he looked down, he saw that the arm was actually a donkey's leg, with blood flowing everywhere. The next day, following the blood trail, he found it leading to a house in the neighborhood. He asked the homeowner about the incident, and the homeowner said, "I have kept a donkey for about twenty years. Last night, one of its legs was cut off, as if by a sharp blade. I was very surprised by this." Wang Xun told the homeowner about the incident, and they killed the donkey and ate it.

#### Guo Zhao Guo Zhao, the Grand Controller, during the Dazhong period, was transferred from Zhitong to Xiliang. There was a gatekeeper who was very diligent and honest. Guo Zhao thought highly of him and entrusted him with many tasks. One day, Guo Zhao ordered the purchase of over a hundred pieces of fine silk fabric, but the price was double the usual amount, and he believed he had been deceived. The gatekeeper was imprisoned, and evidence was gathered to prove his guilt. Guo Zhao then ordered the gatekeeper to be flogged in the courtyard. Suddenly, more than ten dogs rushed towards the gatekeeper, surrounding him and barking, and the guards could not control them. Guo Zhao was greatly surprised and asked about the incident. The gatekeeper said, "I often read the Buddhist *Diamond Sutra* since I was a child, and I always fed the dogs. I don't know anything else." Guo Zhao sighed and said, "Even dogs can feel gratitude, how can I not show kindness." He then released the gatekeeper. #### Zhao Old Man West of Fufeng County, there is a Tianhe Temple, located on a high hill. Below the hill, there is a niche that is open like a hall. An old man named Zhao lived there. The old man had no wife or children, and he suffered from a bent back and weak legs. He often carried a walking stick and begged for food. The villagers pitied his old age, illness, and lack of a home, and they usually gave him food. After receiving food, the old man would always feed the dogs first. After over a year, the old man fell ill with a cold and lay in the niche. It was snowing heavily at the time, and the old man had no clothes, lying naked on the ground, shivering and groaning. The dogs gathered around the old man, wagging their tails and barking. Then they surrounded the old man's bed, competing to support his body with their feet, which helped to reduce the cold. After ten days, the old man died in the niche. The dogs mourned loudly, crying day and night, and only left after several days.

During the Zhenyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, there was a Mr. Han, a judicial official residing temporarily in the southern part of Xihé Commandery. He owned a horse that was very powerful and swift. One morning, the horse suddenly bowed its head at the manger, sweating and panting, as if it had traveled far and was exhausted. The stable master found it strange and reported it to Mr. Han. Mr. Han was angry: "If someone stole the horse and rode it out at night, making my horse exhausted, whose fault is it?" He then ordered the horse to be punished. The stable master had no defense and accepted the punishment. The next day, the horse again sweated and panted. The stable master secretly found it strange, but could not understand it. That night, the stable master, unable to sleep in the stable, closed the door and peered through a crack. Suddenly, he saw Mr. Han's black dog come into the stable, barking and jumping, and soon transformed into a man wearing all-black clothing. The man took the saddle and put it on the horse, then rode away. Riding to the gate, where the wall was very high, the black-clothed man whipped the horse and leaped over the wall. He returned at midnight, dismounted and removed the saddle. The black-clothed man barked and jumped, then transformed back into a dog. The stable master was amazed and dared not tell anyone.

The following night, the black dog once again rode off on the horse, returning only at dawn. The stableman then searched for the horse's tracks, which were clearly distinguishable because of the recent rain and clear weather, leading him over ten miles south to an ancient tomb where the horse's tracks finally disappeared. The stableman built a thatched hut beside the tomb. On the following night, he stayed in the hut to wait for the black dog. As the night was nearly half over, the black-clothed person indeed came riding the horse. He dismounted and tied the horse to a wild tree, then entered the tomb, laughing and talking merrily with several others. The stableman crouched down to listen to them, afraid to move. After several meals' time, the black-clothed person announced his departure, and several others escorted him out of the tomb. Out in the wild, a brown-clothed person turned and said to the black-clothed person, "Where is the Han family's name register now?" The black-clothed person replied, "I have already collected it under the *Shoulian* stone. You have nothing to worry about." The brown-clothed person warned, "Be careful not to leak it. If it is leaked, we will all be in danger." The black-clothed person said, "I will heed your advice." The brown-clothed person asked, "Does the young Han child have a name yet?" The black-clothed person answered, "Not yet. I will wait until he has a name, then compile him in the name register, and I will not forget." The brown-clothed person invited, "Come again tomorrow night, and we will be able to talk and laugh." The black-clothed person nodded and left.

By dawn, the stablekeeper returned and secretly reported the matter to Han. Han immediately ordered meat to lure the dog. Once the dog arrived, he tied it up. Following the information, he searched thoroughly under the stone slab and indeed found a scroll of writing, which fully recorded the names and surnames of the Han family members, including brothers, wives, and servants, with nothing omitted. This was the so-called register of the Han family. There was a son who had been born for a month, but this son was not recorded, which was referred to as "a young child not yet named." Han was greatly surprised and ordered the dog to be brought to the courtyard, where he whipped and killed it. He cooked its meat to feed the servants. Shortly afterwards, he led over a thousand scholars from the neighborhood, armed with bows, arrows, and weapons, to the ancient tomb in the southern part of the county. They opened the tomb, and inside there were several dogs, all with different fur patterns. They killed them all and returned.

○李甲 In the Baoying era, there was a son of the Li family, whose name is unknown, living in Luoyang. His family had a tradition of not liking to kill, so they never kept cats, thus sparing the mice. By the time his grandson, he also understood the intentions of his ancestors. One day, the Li family gathered their relatives and friends for a meal in the hall. After they were seated, hundreds of mice appeared outside the door, standing on their hind legs and clapping their front paws, looking very joyful. The family servant was surprised and informed the Li family. The relatives and friends of the Li family then cleared the hall to watch. As people were leaving, the hall suddenly collapsed, but none of the family members were injured. After the hall collapsed, the mice also left. Alas! Mice are indeed small creatures, yet they can still recognize kindness and repay it; how much more so for humans! If so, those who bestow kindness should broaden their kindness, and those who repay kindness should also strive to repay it. Those who do not care, should feel ashamed by this. ○王缙 During the Dali era, Wang Jin, a high-ranking official of the Tang Dynasty, served alongside Yuan Zai in government. One day, as he was about to enter court, it was still early, and he was sitting by the candlelight. There was a bag in front of his couch, and he ordered the attendant servant to bring it in. The servant carried it in, feeling it was too heavy to lift. Wang Jin opened it to look inside, and suddenly a mouse over a foot long, with a very plump and white body, jumped out of the bag. He was greatly frightened and looked at his son and said, "I am not talented but occupy a high position, lacking virtue but honored, often fearing unexpected misfortune. Now an unusual creature touches my hands and feet, isn't this a sign that disaster is about to come?" A few days later, he indeed committed a crime and was demoted to the governor of Jinyun.

Wang Han of Taiyuan was the commander of the Zhenwu Army. His mother, Jin Shi, was originally a woman from the Hu people, skilled in archery and horsemanship, and always known for her fierce and bold nature. She often rode a strong horse, with a bow on her arm and an arrow in her waist, entering deep mountains to hunt bears, deer, foxes, and rabbits, killing and capturing many. Therefore, everyone feared her ability and respected her greatly. Later, when she was over seventy years old, due to old age and illness, she then lived alone in one room, avoided having maidservants, and did not allow them to get close to her. At night, she locked the door and slept. She often became angry and beat her family members. One evening later, after locking the door, the family suddenly heard a grinding sound and quickly went to look. They saw a wolf open the door and come out from inside. It was not yet dawn, but the wolf returned from outside, entered the room, and locked the door again. The family was very afraid and reported everything to Wang Han.
