That evening, they secretly peeped through a crack, as the family had said. Wang Han was worried and anxious, unable to feel at ease. Until dawn, Jin Shi called Wang Han and ordered him to bring a deer or a gazelle from the market. Wang Han brought a cooked deer or gazelle, but Jin Shi said, "I only need a live one." So, a live deer or gazelle was brought to her, and Jin Shi ate it all at once. Wang Han became even more afraid. Some of the family members whispered about the matter, and Jin Shi heard it, looking very ashamed. That evening, after locking the door, the family waited and watched again, and a wolf then broke the door and came out. From then on, it never returned.

○ Wang Han Wang Han of Taiyuan was the Commander of the Zhenwu Army. His mother, Madam Jin, was originally a woman of Hu ethnicity, skilled in archery and horseback riding, and long known for her fierce and ruthless nature. She would often ride a strong horse, with bow in arm and arrows at her waist, entering deep mountains to hunt bears, deer, foxes, and rabbits, killing and capturing many. Thus, people all feared her ability and held her in high regard. Later, when she was over seventy, due to old age and illness, she lived alone in a single room, dismissing her attendants and forbidding them from approaching her at all. At night, she would lock the door and go to sleep. She often became angry and beat her family members. One night, after locking her door, the family suddenly heard a grinding sound and rushed to see what had happened. They saw a wolf emerging from the room through the door. Before dawn, the wolf returned from outside, entered the room, and locked the door again. The family was greatly frightened and reported the incident to Wang Han. That same night, they peered through a crack in the wall and saw exactly what the family had said. Wang Han was filled with anxiety and fear, unable to find peace. At dawn, Madam Jin summoned Wang Han and ordered him to immediately go and hunt deer and elk. Wang Han brought a freshly killed deer and elk and presented them to her. Madam Jin said, "I only needed it alive." So, they brought a live deer and elk before her, and Madam Jin ate it all at once. Wang Han became even more frightened. Some of the family members whispered about the incident, and Madam Jin, upon hearing this, was greatly embarrassed. That night, after locking the door, the family once again watched from hiding, and a wolf broke through the door and came out. From that day on, it never returned.

North of Jinyang, the land is cold and has few bamboos. Therefore, the residents often grow reeds into forests, to replace the bamboo of the south. In the early years of the Changqing era, there was a resident in the neighborhood whose property was covered with a dense reed forest. During a community banquet, the residents left leftover food in their house, but by the next morning, it was always gone. Some residents who stored silk and cloth in their rooms also lost them. The residents secretly found it strange. Later at night, they heard many babies crying. When they approached to listen, it was silent. The next evening, they heard it again, and the residents were even more afraid. On the following day, they told the other residents in the neighborhood, "We often hear many babies crying in the reed forest. I suspect there should not be babies here, and we are afraid of the strange occurrences." They then worked together to cut down the reed forest and clear the grass. When they dug deeper, they found a hole containing silk, cloth, and food containers. They saw more than ten wild cats, some frowning and bowing, some moaning and looking up, some blinking and nursing, some lying down and sitting, all making sounds as if they were in distress. The residents killed all of them, and from then on, the residents lived in peace.

During the autumn of the twenty-third year of the Kaiyuan era, Emperor Xuanzong of Tang went hunting in the nearby suburbs. Upon arriving at the site of ancient Xianyang, a large deer suddenly appeared before him, its massive and imposing body strikingly different from ordinary deer. The emperor ordered his bowmen to shoot it, and with a single arrow, it was struck. As the emperor returned to the palace, he commanded the kitchen staff to roast the deer and present it to him. Meanwhile, the imperial chef prepared a roasted dish and offered it to the emperor. At that time, Master Zhang Guo, the legendary Taoist sage, was in attendance. The emperor invited Zhang Guo to sit beside him and bestowed the roasted meat upon him. Zhang Guo thanked the emperor and began to eat. After finishing his meal, he said, "Your Majesty, what do you think of this deer?" The emperor replied, "I only know it is a deer, but I do not know what makes it so special." Zhang Guo responded, "This deer is nearly a thousand years old. Your Majesty, you should ask me about it." The emperor laughed and said, "It is merely an animal; how could you claim it is a thousand years old?" Zhang Guo explained, "During the autumn of the fifth year of the Yuanshou era in the Han dynasty, I served Emperor Wu of Han while he hunted in the Shanglin Park. One of his attendants caught this deer alive and presented it to the emperor. The emperor showed it to me, and I advised, 'This is a divine deer, nearly a thousand years old. Since it was captured alive, it would be better to let it live.' Coincidentally, Emperor Wu was deeply interested in the pursuit of immortality, and thus he accepted my advice." The emperor then said, "Master Zhang, you are joking!"

--- ### Translation of the Source Text Moreover, from the fifth year of Emperor Wu's reign (元狩五年) to the present, it has been eight hundred years. How could a deer live for eight hundred years without being hunted? There are also many deer within the parks, so how could it be any different for another deer? In the past, when Emperor Wu captured this deer, he was going to let it go and ordered Eastern朔 to make a copper plate, carve the year into it, and attach it to the left horn. I wish to verify this, so as to show that I am not lying. The emperor immediately placed the deer's head in front of him and ordered the court official Gao Lishi to verify it. For a meal's time, he saw nothing. The emperor laughed and said, "Mr. Official, you are mistaken. Where is the copper plate on the left horn?" The official replied, "I will look for it myself." He looked around and used a pair of iron tongs to pull out a small plate, which was actually made of copper, about two inches in size.

Due to the passage of time, the text was covered by hair and skin, only now can it be seen. The emperor ordered it to be polished and examined. The characters are worn and damaged, and cannot be recognized anymore. The emperor thus believed that Guo was not mistaken. The emperor then asked Guo, saying, "In the fifth year of the Yuanshou era, what position does the year Jiazi hold in the historical records? What events occurred? I will verify this in the historical records. Please tell me about it." Guo replied, saying, "That year was the year Guihai. Emperor Wu began to excavate the Kunming Pool to practice water battles, as a means of hunting and following ritual. As of the year Jiaxu, it has been eight hundred and fifty-two years." The emperor immediately ordered the examination of the Han history, and it was confirmed that the Kunming Pool was indeed excavated in the fifth year of the Yuanshou era, and the Jiazi year is also correct. The emperor then said to the strong man, saying, "How extraordinary! Zhang Guo can speak about events of Emperor Wu's time, truly a sage. I certainly cannot know him."

**Tian Yan** Yingchuan's Tian Yan, courtesy name Ye Meng, was a native of Wuyang who had settled in Dongwu. In the later part of the Jinglong era, he was recommended for official service based on his filial piety and integrity and traveled to the capital. On his way, he reached the south of the Wei River, where he encountered a woman who was exceptionally beautiful, dressed in white, standing at the edge of the road, covering her mouth with her sleeve and crying as if she were suffering from injustice. Tian Yan questioned her, and she wept and replied: "I am a woman from Chu, my family name is Hou, and I live in Yiyang County. My ancestors were known for their noble character in the regions of Xiang and Chu, and thus they chose to live in seclusion in the mountains and forests, never willing to visit high-ranking officials. Although I am just a woman, I also have the ideals of the ancient sages Ji and Yi, who preferred a life of seclusion and moral integrity. I intend to dwell in the mythical islands of Penglai, Yingzhou, Kunlun, and Langya to fulfill my aspirations. I happened to meet a gentleman from Pei State, who served as the magistrate of Yiyang, and he was a close friend with my ancestors, to the point of forgetting formalities. My ancestors admired the noble character of Mr. Liu, so I was married into the Liu family. I have been a member of the Liu family for nearly ten years, and I have never committed any minor fault."

Last spring, Mr. Liu was transferred and appointed as the Magistrate of Zhenyuan. He had not been in office for even a year when he was excused from duty due to illness and returned with his entire family to a suburban residence by the Wei River. Mr. Liu lacked virtue and behavior, and he married a woman surnamed Lu, who was from Puchang. She was extremely fierce and violent, often quarreling with each other. I could not bear the anger any longer, so I fled to this place. Moreover, I originally admired immortals and often wished to soar high into the clouds and mist, to find peace in the mountains and valleys, to enjoy the taste of acorns and chestnuts, which is also sufficient for a long life. How could it merely disturb the mundane world, just adding to the burden? Now, I will no longer belong to the Liu family. Then she frowned and sighed with resentment, as if unable to explain herself. Yan's nature is upright and sincere, and he heard her words and believed them greatly. He asked, "Where do you intend to go?" The woman replied, "I am a poor person, where to go? Although, if you ask me, do you have any intention? If so, how dare I disobey your command?" Yan was pleased and immediately rode with her. They arrived in the capital and lived in Yongchong Lane.

Initially, Rock was very cautious, but later he became disrespectful, often scolding and raging, as if in a state of madness. Rock hated this and regretted it. The next day, when Rock left, the woman immediately closed and locked the door, placing Rock's clothes in the courtyard, where they were almost completely destroyed. By evening, when Rock returned, the woman refused to let him in. Angered, Rock broke the door and entered. He saw that his clothes were all torn apart and scolded the woman for it. The woman suddenly flew into a rage, tearing apart Rock's clothes, almost leaving nothing intact. She scratched his face and bit his flesh, causing him severe injuries, with blood staining the ground. She then howled for a long time. Rock was troubled by this and could not control the situation. The villagers all came to watch, crowding around the door. At that time, there was a layman named Hao living in the village, who was skilled in exorcism and had knowledge of talismans and incantations. Upon hearing the woman's crying, he turned to the villagers and said, "This woman is not human; she is a mountain beast who has taken on a human form to deceive the world." The villagers informed Rock of this. Rock then invited the layman to his home. When the woman saw the layman coming, she was greatly afraid. The layman threw a black talisman into the air, and the woman screamed and suddenly jumped away, standing on the roof of a tile house. Rock was puzzled by this. The layman then threw a red talisman, and the woman fell to the ground, transformed into a monkey, and died.

Ran, having realized the strange and supernatural nature of the event, felt a mix of wonder and fear in his heart. The next day, he went to Weinan and inquired among the local people. Indeed, there was a Mr. Liu, whose dwelling was in the outskirts. Ran then paid a visit and asked him. Mr. Liu said, "I once served as an official in Yiyang, where there are many monkeys. I managed to obtain one, and it has been with me for nearly ten years now. Recently, I met an old acquaintance who came from Puzhou, and he gave me a black dog as a gift. My monkey was bitten by the dog and thus escaped." He did not investigate the matter further, but he recorded it and passed it on. Later, Ran entered officialdom through the *Mingjing* examination and ultimately served as the magistrate of Shanggui in Qin Prefecture. There was a traveler journeying in Taiyuan who happened to stop at a hermitage called Tongguodian to rest. In the Buddhist scriptures of the hermitage, he found the story that Mr. Liu had transmitted, but the writing was quite crude. Later, the original was lost. The traveler told me about it as such.

A Story of a Secretary-General In the Chóngràng Lǐ neighborhood of the Eastern Capital, there was a house owned by the Li family. Local lore held that the house was not a lucky place and should not be inhabited. After the death of the previous owner, the family moved to a villa in Lùhún, leaving the house abandoned. The door remained sealed for several years. During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Wáng Chángshǐ, whose name has been lost to history. Wáng Chángshǐ was a high-ranking official who had fallen from grace due to his reckless behavior after drinking, which offended powerful people. He was demoted to serve as a local official in the remote regions of Wu and Yue. Later, he retired to Luoyang and took possession of the Li family's house through a pledge. Despite hearing of the house's ominous reputation, Wáng Chángshǐ, who was known for his strong character, declared, "My fate is in the hands of heaven, not the house," and moved in. He often stayed in the western part of the main hall. One night, he heard a clear, mournful cry, as if it were the sound of the wind. Wáng Chángshǐ rose to look and saw a figure in black standing on a table. He sternly rebuked the figure, who immediately raised one foot to strike Wáng Chángshǐ's shoulder. Frightened, Wáng Chángshǐ retreated, and the figure disappeared. Shortly after, Wáng Chángshǐ fell ill with a severe wound. A few days later, he began to recover slightly. One night, he heard the mournful cry again. His servants searched for the source and saw the black-clad figure perched on a tree in the courtyard. Wáng Chángshǐ had a brother who was skilled in archery. Wáng Chángshǐ ordered him to shoot at the figure. With one arrow, the figure was hit. It let out a howl and jumped onto the tiles of the western wing of the house, then vanished. The next day, when they searched for traces of the figure, nothing was found. In the autumn, Wáng Chángshǐ called workers to repair the stables and, while opening the inner rooms, discovered a dead monkey with an arrow through its chest. Upon examination, the arrow was identified as belonging to Wáng Chángshǐ's brother. It was then realized that the black-clad figure was, in fact, a monkey.

Zhang Jian of Wu Commandery, a native of Chengdu, was dismissed from his post as the magistrate of Luxi during the Kaiyuan era. After being selected for a new position, he was not appointed by the relevant officials and thus returned to Sichuan. As he was traveling through Bashu, it happened to be evening, and he was urging his horse forward when suddenly a person emerged from a path on the left side of the road. The person bowed and asked, "My lord has heard that you have nowhere to stay tonight and wishes to invite you. Please follow me." Zhang Jian asked, "Who is your lord? Is he summoning me as the governor?" The person replied, "No, he is the Marquis of Bashu." Zhang Jian followed the person, and they walked into the mountain path for about a hundred steps. He saw a very high red gate, with many people and armed guards surrounding the area, even more impressive than the homes of marquises and dukes. After walking several dozen steps further, they arrived at the place. A messenger stopped Zhang Jian at the gate and said, "Please allow me to inform my lord first. You should wait here." After a long while, the messenger came out and led Zhang Jian, saying, "Please come in." Zhang Jian entered and saw a person standing on the hall, wearing a fur coat made of brown leather, with an extremely unusual appearance. Many people in fine silk and adorned with jewels and jade surrounded him. Zhang Jian hurried forward and bowed. After bowing, the person gestured for Zhang Jian to ascend the steps and said, "I am the Marquis of Bashu. I have lived here for several decades. Just now, I learned that you have nowhere to stay tonight, so I immediately invited you. Please stay a little longer to enjoy our hospitality." Zhang Jian bowed again to express his gratitude. Soon after, the host ordered the preparation of a feast and placed wine and food. The items used for play and decoration were all luxurious and precious.

The ruler then ordered his attendants to summon six heroic generals, a white-faced marquis, and a river lord. He also summoned five leopard generals, a marquis of Juxu, and a military officer of Xuanqiu. The messenger was instructed to say: "Today we have honored guests coming, we wish to enjoy a full feast, so I have ordered the summons." The messenger replied and then left. After a long time, they arrived. There were six men in black clothes, with fierce appearances, identified as the "six heroic generals." The Marquis of Bazhi stood up and bowed, and the six generals also bowed. Another person in a brocade robe, wearing a white cap, with a very fierce appearance, was identified as the "white-faced marquis." He stood up and bowed, and the white-faced marquis also bowed. Another person in dark green clothing, with a tall and imposing figure, was identified as the "river lord." The Marquis of Bazhi bowed again, and the river lord also bowed. Another person in spotted clothing, similar to the white-faced marquis but slightly smaller, was identified as the "five leopard generals." The Marquis of Bazhi bowed again, and the five leopard generals also bowed. Another person in a brown robe, with a three-angled head, was identified as the "marquis of Juxu." The Marquis of Bazhi bowed to him. Another person in black clothing, similar to the river lord, was identified as the "military officer of Xuanqiu." The Marquis of Bazhi also bowed to him. Then they invited them to sit. The Marquis of Bazhi sat facing south, and Zhan faced north. The six generals, the white-faced marquis, and the river lord sat on the east side, while the five leopard generals, the marquis of Juxu, and the military officer of Xuanqiu sat on the west side. After they sat down, they served wine and ordered music. There were several beautiful women, singers and dancers, and the music of strings and pipes began, reaching its utmost excellence.

The White-Browed Marquis, after drinking, turned to Zhan and said, "I will dine tonight. Can you provide me with a full meal?" Zhan replied, "I have not yet guessed what Your Excellency desires. I wish to be taught." The White-Browed Marquis said, "Your body can satisfy my hunger. What value is there in other flavors?" Zhan was afraid and left in a state of alarm. The Marquis of Bashu said, "There is no such thing. How can there be a strange occurrence at a feast?" The White-Browed Marquis laughed and said, "My words were just a jest. How could it be like that? It is not so." After a long while, there was a report that Mr. Dongxuan was at the door, wishing to report important matters. Upon hearing this, a man appeared, wearing black clothes, with a long neck and a broad body. He bowed, and the Marquis of Bashu greeted him. They sat down, and the Marquis asked, "Why have you come?" The man replied, "I am a skilled diviner. I know that you will have great troubles, so I have come to report." The Marquis of Bashu asked, "What troubles will I have?" The man said, "There is a person at the feast who intends to harm you. If you do not act now, you will surely suffer later. I hope you will consider it carefully." The Marquis of Bashu was angry and said, "My feast is just getting started. Where is there anything strange?" He ordered the man to be killed. The man said, "If you use my words, all will be well. If you do not use my words, I will die, and so will you. What should be done? Even if you regret it later, it cannot be undone." The Marquis of Bashu then killed the diviner and placed him on the ground.

Around midnight, everyone was drunk and all lay on the bed, while Zhan also pretended to sleep. Just before dawn, he suddenly felt a startle and woke up, seeing himself lying in a large stone niche. Inside was set up with embroidered curtains, and pearls, coral, rhinoceros horn, and ivory were arranged around. There was a giant ape looking like a human, lying drunk on the ground, which is called the Lord of Bashu; then he saw a giant bear lying in front, which is called the Six Heroes General; another tiger with a white forehead, also lying in front, which is called the Lord of White Forehead; another wolf, which is called the Lord of Canglang; there was also a striped leopard, which is called the Five Leopards General; another giant deer and a fox, all lying in front, which are called the Lord of Juxu and the Colonel of Xuanqiu. And all were in a state of deep sleep, as if drunk. Another turtle, with a very unusual appearance, was dead in front of the niche, which was the recently killed Master Dongxuan.

A mysterious creature was seen, and upon seeing it, the people were greatly startled. They immediately went out through the mountain path and rushed to inform the villagers. The villagers gathered in large numbers, numbering around a hundred, and then armed with bows and arrows, they entered the mountain. Upon reaching the spot, later a monkey suddenly started and said, "Not listening to the words of the Master of Profound Mystery, today it is indeed as such." They then surrounded the niche and killed all of them. The objects displayed were all precious and beautiful. They prepared the details and reported to the magistrate. Before this, people who carried pearls and fine fabrics came to this place and all lost them without reason, and this had been going on for several years. From then on, the problem was eliminated. --- ○ Eldest Man Yang During the early years of the Qianyuan era, there was an elderly man named Yang in Kuaiji who was known throughout the county for his abundant wealth. One day, the old man was about to die, lying in bed and moaning, and this continued for several months. The old man had a son named Zong Su, who was known among the villagers for his filial piety. When his father fell ill, he used up all his wealth to seek medical treatment. Later, he obtained a man named Chen, who investigated the cause: "The old man's illness is a heart ailment. Because his wealth was abundant, his heart was driven by profit. Therefore, his heart had left his body. Unless one eats the heart of a living person, it cannot be healed. But how can the hearts of all living people be obtained? As such, it is beyond my knowledge." Zong Su, having heard this, thought that the heart ailment could not be obtained, and so he solely practiced Buddhist practices, hoping to alleviate his father's illness. He immediately called monks to recite sutras, ordered craftsmen to cast his father's image, and afterward brought food himself, going to a Buddhist temple in the county to feed the monks.

One day, as I took food with me and went out, I mistakenly entered a mountain path. Below the mountain, I saw a stone niche, and inside the niche was a foreign monk. The monk appeared very old and emaciated, wearing a robe made of brown wool threads. He sat on a large, flat rock. Master Zongsu thought him a remarkable person and immediately bowed and asked, "Master, who are you? Why do you live alone in a remote valley, making a home in a place where human footprints are rare? You have no attendants, yet you do not fear the wild animals of the mountains. Is there any harm to you? Or is it because you have mastered the teachings of Buddhism?" The monk replied, "I am originally from the Yuan family. My ancestors lived in Bashan. Later generations of our family, some live in Yiyang, scattered and living in various valleys. All can continue and maintain our ancestral business, becoming reclusive scholars in forests and springs, extremely skilled in singing and whistling. People who love poetry often praise their skill in singing and whistling, and thus they gradually became known throughout the world. There is the Sun family, also a branch of our family, who often visit the homes of the rich and powerful. They are also skilled in jesting and joking, so they also visit marketplaces. Every time they play, they can bring profit to others. Only I like the Buddhist way, detaching from worldly affairs, dwelling in the mountains and valleys without moving, and I have been here for many years. I often admire King Goriputra's self-mutilation and Bodhidharma throwing himself off a cliff to wait for hungry tigers. Therefore, I eat acorns and chestnuts, drink flowing water, and regret that there are no tigers or wolves to devour me. I am also willing to accept it." Master Zongsu then said, "Master, you are truly a sage, able to give up your body without hesitation, intending to feed the mountain beasts. You can be called both compassionate and courageous. However, my father has been ill for several months, despite treatment, he has not recovered. I am worried day and night, and I have no idea what to do."

A physician said, "This is a disease of the heart. To cure it, one must eat the heart of a living person, which is indeed impossible." Now, if the teacher can sacrifice himself to the wild beasts to save his father's hunger, how much more virtuous is it to sacrifice one's life for the benefit of others? I hope the teacher will consider this carefully." The monk replied, "If that is true, then it aligns with my intentions. The layperson seeks me for his father, and I have no objections. Moreover, sacrificing myself to wild beasts is less virtuous than helping others. However, I have not yet eaten today, so I wish to have one meal before I die." Zong Su was both joyful and grateful, and immediately placed the food he had brought in front of the monk. The monk ate it quickly and then said, "I have eaten, and now I will follow your instructions, but first I must pay respects to the four directions." He then straightened his clothes, stepped out of the niche, and began to pay respects. After completing his respects to the east, he suddenly leaped and climbed a tall tree. Zong Su thought the monk had supernatural powers and found it difficult to understand. Soon, the monk called out to Zong Su in a stern voice and asked, "What did you request earlier?" Zong Su replied, "I wished to obtain a human heart to cure my father's illness." The monk said, "What you wish for, I have already agreed to. Now, I wish to first discuss the profound meaning of the *Diamond Sutra*. Would you like to listen?" Zong Su said, "I have always respected Buddhism. Today, honored to meet my teacher, how could I not listen?" The monk said, "The *Diamond Sutra* states, 'Past thoughts cannot be attained, present thoughts cannot be attained, and future thoughts cannot be attained.' If you wish to take my heart, it too cannot be attained." After saying this, he suddenly leaped, shouted, and transformed into a monkey and left. Zong Su was surprised and frightened, and returned home.

Lín Jǐngxuán of the Tang Dynasty was from Jingzhao but lived as a guest in Yānmén. He took hunting with horse and bow as his duty. The prefect was pleased with his ability and thus recruited him as a military officer. He often went hunting with his followers, riding strong horses, holding bows, arrows, and weapons, with a falcon on his arm and a dog in front, all galloping across the fields, obtaining many deer, hares, foxes, and rabbits. Because of this, the prefect allowed him to go wherever he pleased and did not make him handle administrative tasks. Once, while hunting on a high hill outside the city, a rabbit suddenly appeared from the bushes. Lín Jǐngxuán whipped his horse to chase it, covering about ten miles, when the rabbit hid in a grave. Lín Jǐngxuán dismounted, immediately ordered two soldiers to guard beside the grave, tied up his horse, and rested. Suddenly, he heard someone speaking from the grave, saying, "My fate is earth, and the element that overcomes earth is wood. The day is on the second position, and the hour is at the卯position. Both woods are dominant. Am I going to die?" Afterward, he sighed for a long time. He then said, "Someone coming from the east, I will not escape." Lín Jǐngxuán heard the words and found them strange. He looked into the grave and saw an old man, wearing white clothes, with a long white beard, holding a scroll of books, with many dead birds and crows in front. Lín Jǐngxuán immediately asked him, and the old man was surprised and said, "Indeed! The one who will bring disaster to me is coming." He then began to curse and scold. Lín Jǐngxuán silently thought about it and said, "This grave is very small, yet the old man lives in it. Is he not a ghost? Otherwise, he is a thief hiding here." He immediately destroyed the grave, and the old man then transformed into an old fox, lay down quietly on the ground. Lín Jǐngxuán then shot him and killed him. He looked at the book he held, the strokes were very unusual, seeming like Sanskrit but not Sanskrit characters, made of white silk, only several dozen feet long. Lín Jǐngxuán burned it.

○Qixian Villager In the Tang Dynasty, there was a villager from Qixian who transported fodder and grain to Taiyuan by cart. On his return journey, as evening approached, he saw a white-clad woman standing by the roadside and addressing him: "I have come from the capital today and am very exhausted. May I be allowed to ride in your cart?" The villager agreed, and she climbed aboard. They had not traveled three or four miles when, due to the need to grease the wheel hubs, the villager noticed a solitary tail in the gap of the cart, hanging down from the front. The villager immediately cut it off with a sickle. The woman then transformed into a tailless white fox and howled as it disappeared. ○Li Linfu (II) During the reign of Li Linfu as chancellor, he once sat in the front hall after returning from court. He saw a black fox, which was very large, with fur as dark and lustrous as that of a cow or horse, emerging from the hall and galloping to the courtyard, looking around. Li Linfu ordered an arrow to shoot it, but before he could, the fox had already vanished. For several days thereafter, every time he sat during the day, a black fox would appear. That year, Li Linfu's estate was confiscated and distributed. ○Li Kui (I) In the early period of the Tang Dynasty, when Li Kui served as a secretary in the imperial chancellery, he once returned home after a day of court. He saw a white fox in the courtyard, standing on a stone used for pounding silk, and ordered his servant to chase it. The fox had already disappeared. At that time, a guest at Li Kui's door heard about the incident and said, "This is an auspicious omen. I dare to congratulate you." The next day, Li Kui was indeed selected as a deputy minister of the Board of Rites.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, there was a local official in Jiangling named Péi, whose name is not recorded. He had a son over ten years old who was intelligent, well-versed in literature, and had a bright and refined appearance. Péi deeply cared for him. Later, the son became ill, and his condition worsened day by day, with medicine proving ineffective. Péi then sought a Taoist magician to use exorcism, hoping to alleviate his son's suffering. One day, a man knocked on the door and introduced himself as a son of the Gao family, whose profession was magic. Péi invited him in and asked him to examine his son. The magician said, "This child is not suffering from a common illness, but is bewitched by a fox spirit. However, I have a method that can cure him." Péi immediately thanked him and prayed for help. The magician then performed his rituals. After a meal, the son suddenly sat up and said, "My illness seems to have subsided." Péi was greatly pleased and called the magician a true practitioner of magic. He prepared food and drink, then heavily rewarded him with money and silk, and thanked and dismissed him. The magician said, "From now on, I will come every day to visit." He then left. Although the son's physical illness had subsided, his spirit was weak, and he often spoke wildly, sometimes laughing or crying uncontrollably. Every time the magician came, Péi prayed to him for help. The magician explained, "This child's spirit has been struck by a demon, and it has not yet returned. In no more than ten days, he will recover, please do not worry." Péi believed him. After several days, another man named Wang arrived. He claimed to have a divine talisman that could banish demons and cure their diseases. He came to pay respects, and Péi spoke with him. Wang said, "Hearing that your beloved son is ill, and the illness has not yet healed, I offer my assistance."

I wish to see him once." Mr. Péi immediately had his son brought in, and the young man was greatly surprised and said, "This young master is afflicted by a fox spirit. If it is not treated quickly, it will worsen." Mr. Péi then mentioned Gāo, and Wáng laughed and said, "How can you be sure Gāo is not a fox himself?" and sat down. Just as he was setting up a seat and preparing to forbid the fox, Gāo suddenly arrived. After entering, he shouted angrily, "How can this son of yours have recovered, yet you invite a fox into the house? He is the one who is sick!" Wáng, upon seeing Gāo, again shouted, "Indeed, it is an evil fox! It has indeed arrived. Why use other methods to summon it?" The two of them were in disarray, continuously insulting each other. The Péi family was greatly shocked. Suddenly, a Taoist priest arrived at the door, privately telling the servant, "I have heard that Mr. Péi has a son afflicted by a fox. I am skilled at seeing ghosts. Just tell him, and I will come in to pay respects." The servant rushed to tell Mr. Péi, who went out to speak about the matter. The Taoist priest said, "It is easy to deal with." Upon entering, he saw the two men, who again insulted him, saying, "This is also an evil fox! How can a Taoist priest deceive people?" The Taoist priest also scolded them, saying, "The fox should return to the countryside among tombs. Why trouble people?" They then closed the door and began to fight. After a few meals' time, Mr. Péi became even more afraid. His servant was panicked and confused, with no solution. By evening, there was complete silence, and no sound was heard. When they opened the door to look, the three foxes were lying on the ground, panting and unable to move. Mr. Péi beat them to death with a whip. His son recovered after a few months.

During the middle of the Yuanhe period of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Jì Zhēn, who originally hailed from the region of Qing and Qi. He once traveled westward to Chang'an and arrived in Shaanxi. Jì Zhēn was on good terms with an official there, and on the day he was about to depart, the official invited him to drink. They did not part ways until late in the evening. After traveling for just ten miles, Jì Zhēn suddenly fell off his horse, and his two servants drove away his belongings. By the time Jì Zhēn woke up from his drunken stupor, it was already dusk, and his horse had also left. He looked back along the path and saw horse urine on a small trail, so he followed it. Unaware of how far he had walked, he suddenly came upon a grand entrance with a very high red gate, surrounded by lush locust and willow trees. Jì Zhēn, having lost his servants and horse, felt despondent and proceeded to knock on the door. It was already locked, and a small child came out to see him. Jì Zhēn immediately asked, "Whose residence is this?" The child replied, "It is the villa of Officer Li." Jì Zhēn then requested to enter and pay his respects. The child hastily informed the owner. Shortly after, someone was sent to invite Jì Zhēn to rest in the guesthouse.

Immediately led into the house, to the left was a guest seat, which was very clean and spacious. The screens and partitions set up were adorned with ancient landscapes, famous paintings, books, and cushions, generally clean but not overly ornate. Zhen sat there for a long time. A young servant came out and said, "The master is about to arrive." Soon, a man appeared, about fifty years old, wearing a red sash and silver seal, with a very dignified and imposing appearance. He met Zhen, and after exchanging bows, they sat down. Zhen then explained, "I stayed behind to drink with an old friend and got so drunk on the way that I didn't notice it was getting dark, and both my servant and horse were lost. May I stay here for one night, please?" Li said, "I only worry that this place is humble and small, unsuitable for a distinguished guest. Is there any other room?" Zhen felt ashamed and thanked him. Li added, "I once served in Shu, but soon resigned due to illness. Now I have retired here." They then engaged in conversation, and Li was very intelligent and knowledgeable. Zhen admired him greatly. Li then ordered the servant to find Zhen's servant and horse, and soon they all arrived. He left them there. Afterward, they prepared a meal and ate together. After finishing the meal, they drank several cups of wine and fell asleep. The next morning, Zhen got up early and bid farewell. Li said, "I wish to stay one more day, to enjoy your company." Zhen was moved by his kindness and stayed. The next day they parted.

When he arrived in the capital city, he lived there for more than a month. One day, someone knocked on his door and introduced himself as the scholar-official Dugu Zhao. Zhen invited him to sit and talk. Dugu Zhao was very intelligent and eloquent, and he said to Zhen, "I come from Shaanxi. I came from the west yesterday and visited Li, the scholar-official. He spoke of your virtues without pause and wishes to make a marital alliance with you. So he sent me to convey this intention. What do you think about it?" Zhen was pleased and agreed. Dugu Zhao said, "I am returning to Shaanxi today. You are heading east. You should visit Li again and thank him for his intentions." He then left. After ten days or so, Zhen returned and visited Li's villa. Li saw Zhen arrive and was very happy. Zhen immediately told Li what Dugu Zhao had said and thanked him for it. Li then kept Zhen and chose a date for the ceremony. His wife was very beautiful and was also intelligent, sensitive, and gentle. Zhen stayed for ten days or so, then took his wife and children back to Qing and Qi. From then on, Li's news and letters did not cease. Zhen practiced Daoism and would read the *Huangting Neijing* every morning. Li often stopped him and said, "You like Daoism. Would you be like Emperor Qin or Emperor Han? The effort to seek immortality. Who could match the efforts of Emperor Qin or Emperor Han? Those two were emperors, rich with all the seas, and spent all their wealth to learn immortality. They still died at Shaoqiu and were buried at Maoling. As for you, a commoner, you are still confused by seeking immortality?" Zhen scolded him and then finished reading. Thinking he was knowledgeable, he did not suspect him to be of another kind. After a year or so, Zhen took his family to participate in the imperial examination and reached the outskirts of Shaanxi. Li kept his daughter and sent Zhen to the capital city. The following autumn, he was appointed as a military officer in Yan Prefecture, and Li followed him to his post. After several years, he retired and returned to Qing and Qi.

Another ten years passed, and Li had seven sons and two daughters, all of whom surpassed the common people in talent, appearance, and demeanor. Yet, Li herself retained a dignified and beautiful appearance, though not as youthful as before. The man grew even more attached to her. Soon after, Li fell gravely ill, and the man sought out doctors and sorcerers, trying every possible remedy, but she did not recover. One day, she privately held the man's hand, sobbing and weeping, and said to herself, "I know death is near, but I endure shame to reveal my innermost thoughts to you. I hope you will forgive my faults and allow me to speak my mind fully." Afterward, she sobbed uncontrollably. The man was also moved to tears and comforted her. She then said, "I know I will be blamed for speaking up, but considering that nine young children are still alive, they are a burden to you, and I dare to speak once more. And I am not an ordinary human; fate has destined me to be your companion. For twenty years, I have served you with my humble fox form, and I have never caused any offense. I fear that other creatures might bring you sorrow. A woman's sincere heart believes it has been fully devoted. Today, I seek to depart, and I dare not leave behind my remaining supernatural essence with you. Considering the many young ones, all are ordinary humans, to continue the lineage. When I am gone, I hope you will remember the young ones, do not view my bones as an enemy, and allow my body to be buried in the earth. It will be a great gift for a hundred lifetimes." After finishing her words, she sobbed again, with tears streaming down her face. The man was shocked, pleased, and saddened, unable to speak. They wept together for a long time. She then covered her head with a blanket and lay facing the wall, and after a while, there was no sound. The man then uncovered the blanket and saw a dead fox in it. He was particularly moved and saddened, and he buried the fox, performing the burial rites as if for a human.

The ceremony concluded, the man went straight to Shan, visiting the residence of the Li family. The burial grounds were overgrown with brambles, silent and empty, with nothing to be seen, and he returned home with a feeling of melancholy. After living there for over a year, seven sons and two daughters died one after another. When he looked at their remains, they were all human, and ultimately, they had no evil intentions. ○ Yin Yuan Yin Yuan, once having taken the imperial examination but not passing, served as the magistrate of Jinyang in Taiyuan. After retiring from office, he retired to the countryside, finding pleasure in literature and writing. One day, a plain-clothed man came to visit him, introducing himself as: "A son of the Zhu family from Wu County, who loved learning since early years. I have heard that the respected scholar takes pride in his literary achievements and wish to ask questions of you, please do not reject me." Yin Yuan invited him in to talk and inquired about his views. He said: "I lived in Lan River, and in early years, I visited the northern gate with Inspector Wang. Now, I have been living in Wang's subsidiary estate for many years." From then on, he came every four days. He was sharp and profound in argument, with elegant and dignified speech. Yin Yuan deeply admired him. Yin Yuan then said: "Your sharp and profound arguments can make you travel to various regions, becoming a distinguished guest of princes and dukes. Why do you choose to remain obscure, hiding in the wilderness?" The man replied: "I am not unwilling to visit princes and dukes, but I fear unforeseen dangers every day."

Aun said, "Why speak of ill omens?" Zhu replied, "Since this year, my dreams and divinations have shown signs of an end." Aun then comforted him with words. The student felt somewhat ashamed. Later, on the Double Ninth Festival, someone gave Aun a bottle of strong wine. Zhu also came, and Aun offered him the wine. Initially, Zhu declined, saying he was unwell and dared not drink. But then he said, "On such a good festival, how could I not enjoy the host's hospitality?" He took a full drink and, after a short while, became drunk and left. Not having walked more than a dozen steps, he suddenly fell to the ground and transformed into an old fox, drunk and immobile. Aun immediately killed it. He then visited Wang the magistrate's former residence, where an old farmer told him, "Wang the magistrate was a deputy general who was stationed in Lan Prefecture years ago and died from a fox spirit illness. It has been many years since then." The tomb was located several steps north of the village. Aun then ordered his servant to find the magistrate's tomb, and indeed, there was a grave. Later, Aun became a magistrate and secretly recounted this event. It was at the beginning of the Taiping era of the Tang dynasty.

○Wei's Son The son of the Wei family from Dulings lived in Hancheng and had a villa about ten miles north of the town. In the autumn of the tenth year of the Kai Cheng era, he went from the town to visit his villa. As evening fell, he saw a woman dressed in white, carrying a gourd, coming from the north. She said to Wei, "I have lived in this village for many years, and my family is very poor. Today, I have been insulted by the village headman and am planning to sue him in court. I hope you, sir, will write down my story with paper and ink, so I can present it to the town officials and hope to clear my name." Wei agreed. The woman then bowed to Wei and invited him to sit in the fields, and she produced a wine cup from her clothes, saying, "There is wine in the gourd. I wish to drink to our hearts' content." She then poured the wine and offered it to Wei. As Wei was about to raise the cup, a hunting party came from the west, leading several dogs. The woman saw them and immediately ran eastward for several dozen steps, transforming into a fox. Wei was greatly frightened and looked at the cup in his hand, which turned out to be a skull, and the wine was like cow urine. Wei became ill with a fever and recovered after more than a month.

○兴福寺 The Xingfu Temple in Chang'an has a Ten-Light Buddha Hall, whose buildings are extremely grand and beautiful, said to have been built during the Sui dynasty. During the Zhenguan era, the temple monks, considering its age, feared it might collapse, so they immediately calculated the expenses and labor and planned to renovate the wooden structures. As they were about to demolish and rebuild, when they opened the door, they saw thousands of snakes lined up on the ground. The snakes coiled and piled up like a heap, shaking their heads and opening their mouths, as if they were about to devour something. The monks were greatly afraid, thinking that heaven pitied the heavy labor and had temporarily manifested as a miracle, so they dared not remove or destroy the snakes. ○李林甫（三） Li Linfu's residence was originally Li Jing's residence. There was a Daoist master named Hong Shi, famous for his Daoist arts during the reign of Emperor Ruizong. He once passed by the residence and told someone, "Those who live here in the future will be extremely honored." For a long time afterward, no one lived there. In the early Kaiyuan era, Li Linfu held the position of Fengyu (Imperial Attendant) and moved in. Someone reported to Hong Shi, saying, "How strange! My words have indeed come true. He will hold the position of chancellor for nineteen years and be renowned as a powerful and honored person throughout the world. However, I fear that if he changes the middle gate, disaster will befall him." Li Linfu indeed became chancellor to Emperor Xuanzong, relying on his power and status, and people had been disappointed by him for a long time. By the end of his reign, someone presented a very tall good horse, but the gate was slightly low and the horse could not pass through. So he changed and rebuilt it. After demolishing the eaves, suddenly there were thousands of snakes in the roof tiles. Li Linfu disliked this and stopped but could not destroy them. Not long after, Li Linfu was eventually dispossessed. From the beginning of his chancellorship to his dispossesion, it was indeed for nineteen years.

The Pavilion of Wei Yu Chun In the region of Linhuai, there was a pavilion by the side of the Sui River. The pavilion was surrounded by a large tree, its circumference spanning dozens of gong, suddenly growing strong and tall, casting shade over a hundred steps. Often, there were fierce winds and thunderstorms that would erupt within the pavilion. People would see two lights within the pavilion, rising and falling, shining brightly like lightning. When the wind subsided, the lights would also disappear. During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Wei Yu Chun, known for his bravery and strength. One day, while traveling in Linhuai, Wei Yu Chun heard of this phenomenon. He said, "I can observe it." Thus, he carried his clothes and bag and stayed in the pavilion to wait. One evening, a fierce storm with thunder and lightning suddenly struck the ground, shaking the pavilion. Indeed, he saw two lights moving within the pavilion. Wei Yu Chun then gathered his clothes and descended, suddenly feeling something coiled around him, cold as ice, tightly bound and unable to be untied. Looking back, he saw the two lights behind him. Wei Yu Chun then struggled and swung his arms, making a sound, and the binding was released. He returned to the pavilion. Shortly after, the storm cleared up, and he smelled a fishy odor within the pavilion, like that of a fish market. The next day, he saw a huge snake cut in half and dead, with blood covering the ground. The locals came to see, thinking Wei Yu Chun was going to die, but they were greatly surprised to see him alive. From then on, the pavilion no longer suffered from storms.

○ Fearless Master During the Tianbao era, the Fearless Master was in Luoyang. At that time, there was a giant snake, its appearance very unusual, over ten feet tall, with a circumference of fifty feet, coiling like a massive serpent, emerging from the mountains. The people of Luoyang all saw it. The Fearless Master then said, "After this, this snake will break the water and flood the city of Luoyang." He immediately expounded on the profound meanings of Buddhist scriptures. At dusk, the snake would come riding the wind and mist, as if listening attentively. The Fearless Master then rebuked it, saying, "You, the snake, dwell securely in the deep mountains; what are you doing here? If you intend to spread poison in the world, then go quickly; do not harm the living." Upon hearing this, the snake bowed its head to the ground, as if showing shame, and died shortly thereafter. Later, An Lushan occupied Luoyang, destroying all the palaces and temples, fulfilling the prophecy of the Fearless Master that the waters of the Luo River would flood the city. ○ Li the Recorder of Lishui During the Kai Cheng era, there was a man named Li from Longxi who served as the Recorder of Lishui, residing in the official residence. One morning, he saw hundreds of snakes in the courtyard and was greatly frightened, ordering them to be thrown into the outskirts. The next morning, the snakes gathered again in the courtyard. Li was even more afraid and found this strange, so he also ordered them to be thrown away. The following day, the snakes returned again. Li was shocked and said, "Is it that heaven is bringing disaster upon me?" His expression was sad for a long time. After more than ten days, Li was accused of a crime and reported to the governor, who sent officials to his door to investigate his guilt and report to the emperor. Li was terrified and unable to find peace, so he hanged himself from a tree in the courtyard and died by cutting off his neck. Li had a wife who felt that her husband did not die a natural death and also hanged herself. As a result, the household servants were terrified and several of them threw themselves into the well. It truly matched the disaster predicted by the appearance of the snakes. The governor was Li Xingshu.

In the autumn of the fourteenth year of the Zhenyuan era, an unusual bird with a green color and a shape resembling a magpie or jay flew over the outskirts of Suizhou and landed in a thicket of trees. A multitude of birds of various kinds, all leading their own species, arranged themselves around the central bird, both in front and behind. Every morning and evening, the birds would each carry insects and grains to offer to the central bird. Whenever this bird flew, the surrounding birds would all chirp and lead the way in front, flanking it on the sides, and following behind, as if guarding or protecting it. When the birds stopped, they would circle around and face the central bird, their behavior resembling the respectful etiquette of ministers serving the emperor, which is unparalleled in dignity. The people of Suizhou all went out to the fields to watch the spectacle, considering it a manifestation of the most auspicious of all birds. However, its appearance did not resemble that of a phoenix, which made it even more extraordinary. At the time, Li Ao was a guest in Suizhou. Li Ao said, "This is indeed a phoenix." He then composed a chapter titled "Knowledge of the Phoenix," fully recording the event.

A Man from Yecheng During the time when Xue Song was stationed in Wei, there was a man from Yecheng who enjoyed raising falcons and hawks. One day, someone brought a falcon to him and informed the man from Yecheng, who then bought it. The falcon was extremely spirited and agile, and the man from Yecheng had raised many falcons and hawks, none of which could compare to this one. He would often carry it on his arm for play, never letting it out of his hand. Later, an Eastern barbarian saw it and offered to pay more than a hundred pieces of silk for it. The man from Yecheng replied, "I am just thinking about this and don't know what use it will have." The Eastern barbarian said, "This is a sea hawk, good at warding off the dangers of dragons and chimeras. You should release it in the south of Yecheng to see its use." Before this, the dragon in the southern pond of Yecheng had been a constant nuisance to the people of the city for many years. The man from Yecheng then took the falcon there. The sea hawk suddenly dove into the pond, and after a while, it emerged with a small dragon in its beak. After emerging, it finished eating the dragon. From then on, the people of Yecheng were free from the dragon's troubles. Someone reported this to Xue Song, and he ordered the man from Yecheng to investigate the matter. The man from Yecheng then presented the sea hawk to Xue Song.

A Man from the Zhou Family A man from the Zhou family in Runan, originally from Wu County, but having lost his name, lived in Kunshan County. During the Yuanhe era, he passed the Mingjing examination with distinction and was appointed as a magistrate of Kunshan. After setting out for his post, he stayed at an inn not far from the town. That night, he dreamed of a man dressed in white, with a very handsome appearance, and blood staining his collar, as if he had been injured in the chest. The man bowed and wept, saying to the Zhou man, "I am a recluse who lives in the mountains and forests. Because I do not value worldly affairs, I have been able to live in peace for many years. Now, while walking in the fields, I unfortunately encountered one of your household servants, who tied me up. I am a recluse by nature, and being bound causes me great distress. Moreover, a mad dog bit my chest, which filled me with anger. I hope you will pity me and pardon me. Otherwise, I will die soon." The Zhou man replied, "I will follow your advice and never forget it." After speaking, he woke up suddenly, feeling quite surprised. The next day, he arrived at his home. That evening, he dreamed again of the white-clad man, who said, "I previously told you about my situation, and you kindly promised to help me. However, I am still bound. Please do not change your kind heart. Quickly free me from my bonds so that I am not kept as a prisoner in your household. That would be fortunate." The Zhou man then asked, "Then, may I know your name and surname?" The man replied, "I am a bird." He said this and then left. The next day, the Zhou man told his servants about the dream and asked them about the incident. It turned out that one of the family members had gone into the fields and caught a goose, which they brought back in a cage. The night before, a dog had bitten the goose in the chest. The Zhou man immediately ordered the goose to be released. That evening, he dreamed again of the white-clad man, who thanked him and then left.

**吕生的妻子** 东平的吕生，是鲁国人，居住在郑地。他的妻子黄氏，病重将死，对她的婆婆说：“我病重将死。然而我听说人死后会变成鬼，我常常遗憾人鬼之间不能相通，使得活着的人更加悲伤。现在婆婆您对我如此疼爱，我死后，一定能通过梦境告诉您。”等到她死后，婆婆梦见黄氏前来，哭泣着说：“我一生中行为不端，现在成了异类，生在郑地东边的荒野中，有黑色的翅膀、发出哀鸣的鸟，就是我。七天后，我会来拜访您。希望婆婆您能记得我生前的情谊，不要因为我现在是异类而拒绝我。”说完，便离开了。七天后，果然有一只鸟从东方飞来，到了吕家，停在庭院的树上，哀鸣了很久。婆婆哭泣着说：“果然是我的梦啊。你不要隐藏你的本性，直接到我家来。”那只鸟随即飞进堂中，盘旋哀鸣，只过了片刻，便向东飞走了。

○Wei Shizi Rong In Qianyang County, there was a temple dedicated to Lady Zhang. During the Shangyuan era, Wei Shizi Rong of the Wei clan was traveling through Qianyang and came to the temple, where he untied his saddle and rested. Suddenly, he saw two sandals made of grass on the ground inside the temple. He examined them and found that they were intricately crafted, with fine texture, white in color, and of exquisite design. Wei Shizi Rong then stored them in his bag and left. Upon reaching the county, the governor housed him in a pavilion. That evening, he placed the sandals in front of him and went to sleep. The next day, they had disappeared, and he could not find them. After only a meal's time, they reappeared on the roof of the pavilion. The servant was startled and informed Wei Shizi Rong, who immediately ordered him to climb the roof and retrieve them. After retrieving them, he placed them in front of him again. The next day, they disappeared once more and reappeared on the roof of the pavilion. This happened three times. Wei Shizi Rong whispered to his servant, "This must be a supernatural occurrence; we should secretly observe it." That evening, the servant secretly watched from a crack. As the night grew late, the sandals suddenly transformed into a white bird and flew onto the roof. Wei Shizi Rong ordered it to be burned, and the bird flew away.

Han Yu, a high-ranking official of the Tang Dynasty, was demoted from his position as Vice Minister of Justice to the governor of Chaozhou. Prior to his arrival, there was a large marsh on the west side of the city, inhabited by a crocodile about a hundred feet long. Whenever the crocodile became angry, the water in the marsh would surge violently, and the surrounding mountains and fields would shake. Any livestock that came near the water would be sucked up and eaten in an instant. The number of victims was countless, and the people had suffered from this for many years. Upon arriving in the region, Han Yu asked about the people's grievances, and they all replied, "It is the crocodile in the marsh on the west side of the city." Han Yu said, "I have heard that sincere devotion can move the spirits: in ancient times, Lu Gong, as the governor of Zhongmu, tamed wild pheasants and caused locusts to flee; Huang Ba, as the governor of Jiuzhou, drove away tigers. This shows that the influence of good governance can even transform wild animals." He then ordered the court officials to set up offerings of meat and wine near the marsh and prayed, saying, "You, a creature of the water, should not harm the people." Afterward, he poured wine over the offerings. That night, there was a wind and thunder in the west of the city, shaking the mountains and fields, and it cleared up by midnight. The next day, the local people saw that the marsh had dried up. The governor ordered someone to investigate the situation, and they found that sixty miles west, a new marsh had formed, and the large crocodile had moved there. From then on, the people of the region were free from this danger. The official of the Ministry of Works, Huang Fu Shi, wrote a *Shendao* stele for Han Yu, which recorded, "As the governor of Chaozhou, he said, 'The mountain tribes and sea barbarians were all transformed; the crocodiles, rice, and crabs no longer harmed the people's property.'" This is what is referred to here.

During the Tang dynasty, Liu Zongyuan, the governor of Liuzhou from Hedong, was often demoted from his position as a provincial inspector to a lower-ranking official in Yongzhou. On his way to Jingmen, he stayed in the inn of the post station. That night, he dreamed of a woman dressed in yellow, who bowed twice and wept, saying, "I am a person from Chu Water. Now I am unfortunate, and I will die soon. Only you can save me. If I can be saved, I will not only be grateful but also repay your kindness. I can also grant you a salary and help you become a general or chancellor, and it will not be difficult. Please think about it." Liu Zongyuan thanked her and agreed. After waking up, he silently found it strange. When he fell asleep again, he dreamed of the woman who both prayed and thanked him, and she stayed for a long time before leaving. The next morning, an official came, claiming to be acting on the order of the governor of Jing, to invite Liu Zongyuan to a banquet. Liu Zongyuan ordered his carriage, but since it was still early, he took a short nap.

Again, I apologize for the oversight. Here is the final, polished translation of the source text: --- Again, I dreamed of a woman, her face sorrowful and anxious, with no time to spare. She turned and said to Zong Yuan, "My fate is now like a broken thread hanging in the wind, in danger of breaking and drifting away. And you cannot consider the urgency of the matter? Please act quickly. Otherwise, it will also break like the broken thread. I hope you will agree to it." After speaking, she bowed again. After she left, I still did not understand. I then lowered my head and thought, saying, "I dreamed of the woman three times in one night, and her words were very earnest. Is it that my officials are treating people unfairly? Or is it that the fish served at the banquet is meant for me? To save it would also be my duty." I immediately ordered a carriage to the banquet in the prefecture. I then told the governor of Jing about my dream and summoned the officials to question them. The officials said, "A day ago, fishermen caught a large yellow-scaled fish, meant for the meal, but now its head is severed." Zong Yuan was startled and said, "Indeed, it was the dream from that night." So I ordered it to be taken and thrown into the river. However, the fish was already dead. That night, I dreamed of the woman again, but she had no head. Zong Yuan found it even more strange.

Once again, Liu Zongyuan dreamed of a woman, her expression sorrowful and anxious, too distressed to speak at first, but then she turned to him and said: "My fate is now like a broken thread hanging in the wind—dangerously close to snapping and drifting away. Yet you do not recognize the urgency of this matter? Please act swiftly. Otherwise, I too shall be severed, like that broken thread. I beg you, noble sir, to grant my request." After speaking, she knelt and prayed. When she departed, Liu Zongyuan's mind still did not understand. He then lowered his head and reflected: "I have dreamed three times in one night of this woman warning me, her words very earnest. Could it be that one of my officials has been unjust to someone? Or is it that the one preparing the banquet has used a fish as my meal? If so, to save it would also be my duty." He immediately ordered his carriage and went to the banquet. Later, he recounted his dream to the governor of Jingzhou and summoned the officials to inquire. The official replied: "The day before, a fisherman had caught a large yellow-scaled fish, intending to serve it as a dish, but now its head has been severed." Liu Zongyuan was startled and said, "Indeed, this was the dream of that very night." He then ordered the fish to be carried and cast into the river, but the fish was already dead. That same night, he dreamed again of the woman, but this time she had no head. Liu Zongyuan was even more astonished by this.

A Woman Named Liuyi During the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Liuyi from Hedong who was a guest in Luoyang. One spring, he went fishing on the Yi River and caught a huge fish. He carried it back home and placed it in a basin of water. Before this event, Liuyi had a child who was six or seven years old. That night, Liuyi dreamed that the fish was biting the child's chest with its mouth. Liuyi woke up startled and indeed heard the child crying, saying that he had dreamed of a large fish biting his chest, causing unbearable pain, and thus he was crying. The child's dream was the same as Liuyi's. Liuyi found it strange and then looked at the child's chest and indeed found a wound with blood. Liuyi became even more afraid. The next morning, he threw the fish back into the Yi River and also ordered a monk to recite scriptures and draw a picture. After only ten days or so, the child's wound healed. Liuyi never fished again after that.

Deng Cheng In the Dangtu County of Xuancheng Prefecture, there were two men named Deng Cheng and Li Hui, both of whom were ignorant of farming. They often used a large boat to carry fish and crabs, selling them in Wu and Yue. In the spring of the thirteenth year of the Tianbao era during the Tang Dynasty, they both set out from the Xin'an River, heading to DanYang Prefecture. They traveled to Xia Cha Pu, forty li from Xuancheng, when it got dark and they anchored their boat. Both men then disembarked. At that time, Li Hui went to a village in Pu Yan, while Deng Cheng remained on the shore. Looking around at the cloud-shrouded islands, there was no sign of human activity. Suddenly, Deng Cheng heard someone shouting "Amitabha" from the boat, the voice very loud. Startled, he looked and saw a large fish in the boat wagging its whiskers and shaking its head, calling out "Amitabha" in a human voice. Deng Cheng was both afraid and terrified, his hair standing on end, and he hid in the reeds to watch. Soon, all the fish in the boat jumped and called out to Buddha, the sound shaking the ground. Deng Cheng was greatly frightened and quickly climbed onto the boat, throwing all the fish into the river. After a while, Li Hui arrived, and Deng Cheng told him everything. Li Hui angrily said, "You fool, how could you be so foolish?" He spat and scolded him for a long time. Deng Cheng had no way to prove himself, so he used his clothes and money to pay for the fish. Later, he had some leftover money, which he used to buy more than ten bundles of reeds, bringing them to the shore. The next day, he moved the reeds into the boat, and suddenly felt it was too heavy to lift. He untied it and saw fifteen thousand strings of cash, with a note saying, "This is your payment for the fish." Deng Cheng was even more amazed. That day, at Guazhou, he met with a group of monks and ate with them, giving them the money as alms. At that time, there was a man named Wan Zhuang, who had retired from his position as magistrate of Jingyang and lived in Guazhou. He heard about the entire incident and recorded it.

○ Li Kui (Part II) During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, Li Kui served as a Counselor of the Ministry of Rites. One day, he was sitting in the daytime in the front hall. Suddenly, he heard a sound in the hall that was extremely loud, as if a wall had collapsed. Li Kui was shocked and went in to see what it was. He saw a toad lying on the ground, several feet tall, with a strange and eerie appearance. Li Kui was both shocked and amazed, unable to understand where it had come from. He immediately ordered a servant to cover it with a large jar. Someone explained, "The toad is an insect from the moon, also a messenger of heaven. Today, the messenger has come to your hall; could it not be that the Heavenly Emperor has entrusted you with a secret command?" The next morning, when he opened the jar to look, the toad had already disappeared. A few days later, Li Kui was indeed appointed as a Counselor of the Secretariat and Chancellor of the State Affairs. ○ Shi Xian There was a person named Shi Xian, whose register was compiled in Taiyuan, and who engaged in commerce, often trading in Dai North. In the summer of the second year of the Changqing era, on the road through Yanmen Pass, the heat was intense, and Shi Xian happened to be resting under a large tree. Suddenly, he dreamed of a monk with bee-like eyes, wearing a simple robe, with a strange and unusual appearance. The monk approached Shi Xian and said, "I reside south of Wutai Mountain, where there is a dense forest and a pool of water, far removed from the mundane world, truly a place where monks can cool off in the heat. Venerable one, would you like to accompany me on a journey? If not, I foresee that you will fall ill with the heat and die; will you not regret your heart?" Shi Xian, thinking that the heat was intense, and the monk was trying to sway him with words of fortune and misfortune, replied, "I wish to accompany you, Master." The monk then led Shi Xian westward, and after walking several miles, there indeed was a dense forest and a pool of water, where he saw a group of monks in the water. Shi Xian was surprised and asked, "What is this Xuan Yin Pool? Why do your disciples bathe in it to cool off the heat?" The monk replied, "This is the Xuan Yin Pool. We bathe in it to cool off the heat." The monk then led Shi Xian to walk around the pool.

The author found it strange that the monks were in the water, and they all looked the same. As night fell, one monk said, "Patron, you may listen to our chanting." The author stood by the pool, and the monks chanted together in the water. After about an hour, one monk took his hand and said, "Patron, come with me to bathe in the Dark Pool. Please do not be afraid." The author followed the monk into the pool and suddenly felt extremely cold, shivering uncontrollably. He then woke up with a start. He found himself lying under a large tree, his clothes soaking wet, and he was extremely cold and frightened. By then, it was already evening, so he went to a nearby village. The next day, he felt slightly better. As he walked along the road, he heard frogs croaking, which sounded like the monks' chanting. He went directly to search for them and found them after walking several miles. There were many frogs in the dense forest and standing water, and these were indeed the Dark Pool. The author thought, "These frogs can change their form to influence people. Are they not especially strange or monstrous beings?" He then killed them all.

During the early years of the Baoli era, there was a poor farmer named Wang Sǒu in Changsha. One day, while working in the fields, he was stung by an earthworm on his arm, and the pain was severe. He rushed back home, but the pain became increasingly unbearable. He moaned at night and groaned during the day, and this continued for about ten days. A traditional Chinese physician said, "This is a very severe poison. At the beginning of the illness, perhaps medicine could help. But now the condition is deep, and I cannot know what to do." After several more days, his illness worsened. Suddenly, he heard a faint sound from his arm, as if it were the sound of an earthworm. A few days later, the sound grew louder, as if combining thousands of sounds, and the pain increased accordingly. That night, he died.

○Wei Jun There was a magistrate named Wei Jun, who once served in Jiangxia. Later, on a mission to the capital, he returned and stayed at an inn in Shangyu along the way. Suddenly, he saw a white spider crawling down from the pillar, very small in size. Wei Jun said, "This is a trouble for people. I heard that even though you are small, you can sting people, and good medicine will be too late." He then killed it with his finger. Soon another white one came down, just like the one he had killed. Looking up, there was a web as a den. Wei then ordered his attendants to sweep it all away with brooms and said, "The troublemaker, I have already eliminated." The next day, when he was leaving, he touched the pillar with his hand and suddenly felt his finger painfully unbearable. It turned out that a white spider had stung him. Wei was startled and immediately brushed it off. Soon it swelled and spread, within a few days, the entire arm was affected. As a result, he was carried to Jiangxia on a stretcher. Medicine could not help, and eventually, his left arm became sores filled with blood, and he died after the blood ran out. Before this, Wei's wife was in Jiangxia and dreamed of a white-clad person who said, "We three brothers, two of us were killed by your son. I told the Heavenly Emperor, who pitied our injustice and granted my request." After saying this, the wife woke up in shock. She found it very strange and could not express her feelings. A few weeks later, Wei arrived, and she learned the details, realizing what she had dreamed. Realizing that the day of the dream was the same as the day he stayed at the inn, she wept and said, "How long can he last?" A few days later, Wei died.

A Man Named Lu Yong Lu Yong from Wu Prefecture lived in Changcheng, and his family had been officials through the imperial examinations on the Confucian classics. From a young age, Lu Yong enjoyed eating, and the more he ate, the thinner he became. As he grew older, he was selected from his prefecture to submit to the Ministry of Rites for the imperial examinations, but he failed and subsequently became a student at the Imperial Academy.

Several months later, a group of several barbarians brought wine and food to Yu's door. After sitting down, they turned and said to Yu, "I am a person from Nanyue, growing up among the barbarians. I have heard that the Emperor of Tang gathers the talented people from all over the world, and wishes to use culture and civilization to influence the four barbarian tribes. Therefore, I have sailed across the sea and climbed over the mountains to come to China, to see the brilliance of the Taixue's culture and civilization. Only your son has a towering hat, a flowing robe, a dignified appearance, and a solemn demeanor. Truly, he is a scholar of the Tang Dynasty. Therefore, I wish to befriend you." Yu thanked them, saying, "I am fortunate to be registered at the Taixue, but I have no other talents. Why do you love me so much?" They then drank together, had a great feast, and left happily. Yu, a trustworthy man, believed that the group of barbarians did not deceive him. After several days, the group of barbarians came again, bringing gold and silk to wish Yu a long life. Yu suspected that they had another intention, so he firmly refused them. The barbarians said, "Your son lives in Chang'an, looking hungry and cold. Therefore, we brought gold and silk to cover your expenses for a day, so that we could be friends with you. We have no other intentions. Please do not doubt us." Yu had no choice but to accept the gold and silk. When the barbarians left, the other students at the Taixue heard about it and came together to tell Yu, "Those barbarians are greedy for profit and do not care about themselves. They fight over small things like salt and rice, even to the point of killing each other. How could they give up gold and silk to wish a friend a long life? Moreover, there are many students at the Taixue. Why did you treat me so favorably? You hid yourself in the suburbs to avoid them coming again." Yu then moved to live by the Wei River and closed his door, not going out.

Only a month or so later, a group of barbarians came to his door. You (the addressee) were greatly surprised, but the barbarian was pleased and said, "When you were still at the national university, I could not fully express myself. Now that you have retreated to the outskirts, this is exactly what I hoped for." After they sat down, the barbarian took You's hand and said, "My coming here was not by chance. I have a request for you, and I hope you will agree. Moreover, what I wish for will cause you no harm, but it will be a great benefit for me." You replied, "I will follow your advice." The barbarian then asked, "Do you like eating noodles?" You answered, "Yes." The barbarian continued, "The one who eats the noodles is not you, but a worm in your stomach. I wish to give you a single pill. If you take it, you will vomit out the worm. Then I will exchange it for a high price. Would that be acceptable?" You said, "If you truly believe this, how can there be anything wrong?" Soon after, the barbarian produced a pill that shone a bright purple color and instructed You to take it. After a short while, You vomited out a worm about two inches long, green in color, and shaped like a frog. The barbarian said, "This is called the 'noodle-eating worm,' and it is indeed a rare treasure in the world."

Zheng asked, "How do you know this?" The trapper replied, "Every morning I see the aura of treasure spanning the sky, located in the Imperial Academy, so I came to visit you to obtain it. However, for more than a month, when I looked at it early in the morning, I saw the aura had moved to the Wei River. Indeed, you have moved there. This insect is formed by the harmonious essence of heaven and earth, so it likes to eat noodles. Because wheat is sown in autumn and only ripens in summer of the following year, it receives the complete essence of the four seasons from heaven and earth, therefore it loves its taste. You should feed it with noodles, and you will see." Zheng immediately brought a basket of noodles to the insect, and it ate them all at once. Zheng then asked, "What use is this insect?" The trapper said, "All the rare treasures in the world share the harmonious essence. This insect embodies the essence of harmony. To grasp the root and take the branch, is it far from that?" Afterward, he put the insect in a tube, locked it in a golden box, and instructed Zheng to place it in his bedroom. He told Zheng, "Tomorrow I will come again." By the next morning, the trapper arrived with ten heavily loaded carts, carrying a large amount of gold, jade, silk, and cloth, and offered them to Zheng. Together, they carried the golden box away. From then on, Zheng became very wealthy, building gardens and houses as means of livelihood, eating fine food every day, wearing fine clothes, and wandering around Chang'an, being called a wealthy and influential man.

After only a year or so, the group of barbarians came again and said to Yong, "Can you, my son, accompany me on a journey to the sea? I wish to explore the sea for its wondrous treasures to dazzle the world, and aren't you, my son, a man of curiosity?" Yong, having become very wealthy and having always enjoyed a life of leisure and self-satisfaction, then accompanied the group of barbarians to the sea. The barbarians built a dwelling and settled there. They placed oil and fat in a silver cauldron, set fire beneath it, and threw insects into the cauldron.
